> **Definition (Heating Curve):** The **heating curve** of an electrical machine is a graph of the temperature rise ($\theta$) of the machine above the ambient (surrounding) temperature, plotted as a function of time ($t$) when the machine is subjected to a constant load from a cold (ambient) state.

It shows how the machine temperature rises **exponentially** from zero to a final steady-state value $\theta_f$ (also called $\theta_{max}$ or $\theta_{ss}$).

### 1.3 Formal Definition — Cooling Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Cooling Curve):** The **cooling curve** of an electrical machine is a graph of the temperature rise ($\theta$) above the ambient temperature, plotted against time ($t$) after the load is removed (or reduced), starting from an initial temperature rise $\theta_i$ down to zero.

It is also an exponential decay curve, governed by a similar time-constant relationship.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-world Analogy — The Pressure Cooker on a Stove:**

Imagine heating a pot of water on a stove at a **constant flame** (constant heat input):
- Initially, the water temperature rises **rapidly** because the temperature difference between the water and surroundings is small → less heat escapes.
- As the water gets hotter, the temperature difference increases → more heat escapes to the surroundings.
- Eventually, a balance is reached: **heat input = heat dissipated**. The temperature stops rising. This is the **steady-state temperature ($\theta_f$)**.

This curve of temperature vs. time is exactly the **heating curve** of an electrical machine!

Now, when you **turn off the flame** (load removed):
- The water starts losing heat to the surroundings.
- Initially, the water is much hotter than the surroundings → cools **fast**.
- As it approaches room temperature, the cooling rate **slows down**.
- The temperature exponentially approaches ambient — this is the **cooling curve**.

The **thermal time constant ($\tau$)** is analogous to the "time it takes for the water to reach about 63.2% of its final temperature rise."

### 1.5 Classes of Duty — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC Standard 60034-1 defines 8 standard classes of duty (S1 to S8).** These classifications describe the **load sequence** to which a machine is subjected, including starting, running, braking, no-load, and rest periods.

The eight duty classes are:
- **S1** — Continuous running duty
- **S2** — Short-time duty
- **S3** — Intermittent periodic duty
- **S4** — Intermittent periodic duty with starting
- **S5** — Intermittent periodic duty with starting and braking
- **S6** — Continuous-operation periodic duty
- **S7** — Continuous-operation periodic duty with starting and braking
- **S8** — Continuous-operation periodic duty with related load/speed changes

### 1.6 Loading Conditions

The **loading condition** of a motor refers to the **pattern of mechanical load** (torque demanded) imposed on the motor shaft over time. Common loading conditions include:

| Loading Type | Description | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous (Constant) Load** | Steady torque for long durations | Fans, centrifugal pumps, conveyors |
| **Intermittent (Pulsating) Load** | Load ON for a short time, then OFF | Cranes, hoists, presses, lifts |
| **Variable (Fluctuating) Load** | Load varies continuously in a cycle | Rolling mills, electric vehicles |
| **Short-time Load** | High torque for a brief period | Valve actuators, braking operations |
| **Reversing / Plugging Load** | Frequent starting, stopping, reversing | Traction motors, machine tools |

### 1.7 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Units used in this topic:**
> - **Heating Time Constant ($\tau_h$):** Time (in seconds) for the temperature to reach **63.2%** of $\theta_f$ → units: **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**.
> - **Cooling Time Constant ($\tau_c$):** Time for the temperature to fall to **36.8%** of its initial value → units: **seconds (s)**.
> - **Final Steady Temperature Rise ($\theta_f$ or $\theta_{max}$):** in **°C** or **K**.
> - **Specific Heat ($C$ or $S$):** Heat capacity of the machine body → units: **J/°C** or **W·s/°C**.
> - **Thermal Resistance ($R_{th}$ or $\theta$-coefficient):** units: **°C/W** (or s).
> - **Ambient Temperature Reference:** **40 °C** (as per IS/IEC standards).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Flow Equation in an Electrical Machine — The First-Order System

The thermal behavior of an electrical machine is modeled as a **first-order thermal system**, analogous to an **electrical RC circuit**:

| Electrical Analog | Thermal Equivalent | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Current (Heat generated) $I$ | Heat input $P_{loss}$ (W) | $P$ |
| Capacitor (Stores energy) $C$ | Thermal capacitance $C_{th}$ (J/°C) | $C$ |
| Resistor (Dissipates energy) $R$ | Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ (°C/W) | $R$ |
| Voltage (Temperature rise) $V$ | Temperature rise $\theta$ (°C) | $\theta$ |

### 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Explanation

The fundamental **heat balance equation** is:

> **Heat Generated = Heat Stored + Heat Dissipated**

This can be written as:

$$P \cdot dt = C \cdot d\theta + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} \cdot dt$$

Where:
- $P$ = total power loss in the machine (W) — assumed constant
- $C$ = thermal capacitance of the machine (J/°C)
- $R_{th}$ = thermal resistance of the machine surface to ambient (°C/W)
- $\theta$ = instantaneous temperature rise above ambient (°C)
- $t$ = time (s)

**Logic Behind Each Term:**
1. **$P \cdot dt$** → Total heat energy produced in time $dt$ (in Joules).
2. **$C \cdot d\theta$** → Heat stored in the machine body causing a temperature rise $d\theta$ (the machine has thermal mass, just like water in a boiler).
3. **$\frac{\theta}{R_{th}} \cdot dt$** → Heat lost (dissipated) to the surroundings by radiation, convection, and conduction — proportional to the temperature difference $\theta$.

Rearranging:

$$C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} = P$$

This is a **linear first-order ordinary differential equation (ODE)** with constant coefficients.

## 2.3 Solution for the Heating Curve

The **general solution** is:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right)$$

Where the **final steady-state temperature rise** is:

$$\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th}$$

And the **heating time constant** is:

$$\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th}$$

### Why does this exponential form arise?

When the machine is switched ON at $t = 0$ with $\theta(0) = 0$ (cold start):

- **At $t = 0$:** $\theta = 0$ → No heat is being lost → All heat goes into raising temperature → **fastest rate of rise**.
- **At $t = \tau_h$:** $\theta = \theta_f(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632\,\theta_f$ → Temperature has reached **63.2%** of its final value.
- **At $t = 5\tau_h$:** $\theta = \theta_f(1 - e^{-5}) = 0.993\,\theta_f \approx \theta_f$ → Machine is considered to have reached **thermal equilibrium** (practically fully heated).

## 2.4 Solution for the Cooling Curve

When the load is removed ($P = 0$) at time $t = 0$, with initial temperature rise $\theta_i$:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$$

Where **cooling time constant**:

$$\tau_c = C \cdot R_{th}'$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooling time constant ≠ Heating time constant (generally):**
> - During **heating**, the rotor (with its fans) is rotating → enhanced forced air cooling → $R_{th}$ is smaller → $\tau_h$ is smaller.
> - During **cooling** (after shutdown), the rotor is stationary → only natural convection → $R_{th}'$ is larger → $\tau_c$ is **2 to 3 times** $\tau_h$ for self-ventilated machines.
> - For **separately ventilated** (TEFV) machines, $\tau_c \approx \tau_h$.

## 2.5 Heating Curve with Pre-Existing Temperature

If the machine is switched ON at $t = 0$ with an initial temperature rise $\theta_i$ (warm start), the heating equation becomes:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_f - (\theta_f - \theta_i) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_h}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the HIGH-YIELD formulas for KTU examinations. Master every entry.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right)$ | Heating curve (cold start) | °C |
| 2 | $\theta(t) = \theta_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$ | Cooling curve | °C |
| 3 | $\theta(t) = \theta_f - (\theta_f - \theta_i)e^{-t/\tau_h}$ | Heating curve (warm start) | °C |
| 4 | $\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th}$ | Final steady-state temperature rise | °C |
| 5 | $\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th}$ | Heating time constant | seconds |
| 6 | $\tau_c = C \cdot R_{th}'$ | Cooling time constant | seconds |
| 7 | $P = \frac{\theta_f}{R_{th}}$ | Power loss for given $\theta_f$ | W (watts) |
| 8 | $G = \frac{P}{\theta}$ | Heat dissipated (Newton's law of cooling) | W |
| 9 | $\theta_{max} = \theta_{amb} + \theta_f$ | Absolute hot-spot temperature | °C |
| 10 | $P_{rating} = \frac{\theta_{ins} - \theta_{amb}}{R_{th}}$ | Rated power loss for insulation class | W |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the field of industrial drives and control, the study of heating and cooling curves is **fundamental** for:

1. **Motor Sizing (Rating Selection):** Selecting the right motor for a given duty cycle (e.g., a 5-minute rated motor cannot run continuously).
2. **Overload Protection:** Designing thermal overload relays that mimic the $I^2t$ heating characteristic of the motor.
3. **Insulation Life Estimation:** Per **Montsinger's rule**, every **10 °C rise** above the rated insulation temperature **halves the insulation life** (Arrhenius relationship).
4. **Determining Safe Restart Time:** Knowing $\tau_c$ tells the operator how long to wait before restarting a hot motor.
5. **Variable Load Analysis:** Using the **method of equivalent current, torque, or power** to convert complex load profiles into an equivalent steady load.

## 2.8 Classes of Duty — Detailed (IEC 60034-1)

| Class | Name | Load Sequence | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Continuous running duty | Operation at constant load for long enough to reach thermal equilibrium | Pumps, fans, compressors |
| **S2** | Short-time duty | Operation at constant load for a **short, specified time** ($N$) less than that required to reach thermal equilibrium, followed by a de-energized rest period long enough to bring the machine back to ambient | Sluice gate drives, valve actuators, crane motions |
| **S3** | Intermittent periodic duty | A sequence of identical duty cycles, each consisting of a period of operation at constant load and a de-energized rest period. **Cyclic duration factor = $N/(N+R)$** where $N$ = running time, $R$ = rest time. Starting currents do not significantly affect the temperature rise. | Presses, hoists, conveyors with intermittent loads |
| **S4** | Intermittent periodic duty with starting | Similar to S3 but with **significant starting (acceleration) currents** affecting temperature | Machine tools, elevators |
| **S5** | Intermittent periodic duty with starting & electric braking | S3 + starting + electric (plugging/dynamic) braking | Cranes with rapid reversals |
| **S6** | Continuous-operation periodic duty | Sequence of identical cycles, each consisting of a period of operation at constant load and a period of operation at no-load. **No de-energized rest period.** | Machine tools, printing presses |
| **S7** | Continuous-operation periodic duty with starting & braking | S6 + electric braking | Rolling mills, large machine tools |
| **S8** | Continuous-operation periodic duty with related load/speed changes | Sequence of identical cycles, each consisting of a period of operation at one load and speed, followed by periods at other loads and speeds (e.g., two different speeds) | Pole-changing induction motors, multi-speed drives |

### 2.9 Cyclic Duration Factor (CDF) — Important KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Cyclic Duration Factor, CDF):** The ratio of the **loading time ($N$)** to the **total cycle time ($N + R$)**, expressed as a percentage:
> $$\text{CDF} \,(\%) = \frac{N}{N + R} \times 100$$
> Standard values: **15%, 25%, 40%, 60%** (as per IEC 60034-1).
> 
> **Why is CDF critical?** A motor rated for S3 duty at 25% CDF means it can deliver **higher rated power** during the ON period than a continuous-duty (S1) motor of the same frame size, because it has cooling-off time to dissipate heat.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Heating Curve Equation

### Derivation 1: From Heat Balance to Exponential Heating

**Step 1 — Write the heat balance equation.**

In time $dt$, the heat produced in the machine = heat stored + heat dissipated:

$$P \cdot dt = C \cdot d\theta + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} \cdot dt$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into a standard first-order ODE form.**

Divide both sides by $dt$:

$$P = C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}}$$

Rearranging:

$$C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} - P = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state boundary condition.**

At thermal equilibrium ($t \to \infty$): $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = 0$ and $\theta = \theta_f$.

From the equation:

$$0 + \frac{\theta_f}{R_{th}} - P = 0$$

$$\boxed{\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th}}$$

**Step 4 — Transform the ODE to eliminate the constant $P$.**

Subtract the steady-state equation from the general equation:

$$C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} - \frac{\theta_f}{R_{th}} = 0$$

$$C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{(\theta - \theta_f)}{R_{th}} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate.**

Let $u = \theta - \theta_f$:

$$C \cdot \frac{du}{dt} + \frac{u}{R_{th}} = 0$$

$$\frac{du}{u} = -\frac{dt}{C \cdot R_{th}}$$

Integrating both sides:

$$\ln(u) = -\frac{t}{C \cdot R_{th}} + K_1$$

where $K_1$ is the integration constant.

$$u = e^{K_1} \cdot e^{-t/(C \cdot R_{th})}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the initial condition.**

At $t = 0$, $\theta = 0$ (cold start), so $u(0) = -\theta_f$:

$$-\theta_f = e^{K_1} \cdot e^{0} = e^{K_1}$$

Therefore $e^{K_1} = -\theta_f$.

**Step 7 — Final heating curve equation.**

$$u(t) = -\theta_f \cdot e^{-t/(C \cdot R_{th})}$$

$$\theta(t) - \theta_f = -\theta_f \cdot e^{-t/(C \cdot R_{th})}$$

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_h = C \cdot R_{th}}$$

---

### Derivation 2: Cooling Curve Equation (Self-Contained)

**Step 1 — Heat balance with $P = 0$.**

When load is removed, no heat is being generated ($P = 0$):

$$0 = C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}'}$$

**Step 2 — Separate and integrate.**

$$C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\frac{\theta}{R_{th}'}$$

$$\frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{dt}{C \cdot R_{th}'}$$

Integrating:

$$\ln(\theta) = -\frac{t}{\tau_c} + K_2$$

$$\theta(t) = e^{K_2} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$$

**Step 3 — Apply initial condition $\theta(0) = \theta_i$.**

$$\theta_i = e^{K_2}$$

**Step 4 — Final cooling curve equation.**

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_c = C \cdot R_{th}'}$$

---

### Derivation 3: General Heating from an Initial Temperature $\theta_i$

This combines both equations. For a machine that is switched ON at $t = 0$ with initial temperature rise $\theta_i$ (e.g., a hot restart):

**Step 1 — Modify the differential equation with new initial condition.**

The ODE is the same, but the integration constant changes.

From Step 5 of Derivation 1:

$$u(t) = e^{K_1} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_h}$$

At $t = 0$, $u(0) = \theta_i - \theta_f$, so $e^{K_1} = \theta_i - \theta_f$.

**Step 2 — Final general heating equation.**

$$\theta(t) - \theta_f = (\theta_i - \theta_f) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_h}$$

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_f - (\theta_f - \theta_i) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_h}}$$

---

### Derivation 4: Heat Dissipation by Newton's Law of Cooling

**Statement:** The rate of heat loss from a body is **directly proportional** to the temperature difference between the body and its surroundings.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$G = \frac{dQ}{dt} = K \cdot \theta$$

Where $G$ = heat dissipated (W), $K$ = heat dissipation constant (W/°C), $\theta$ = temperature rise (°C).

**Note:** $K = 1/R_{th}$ — the reciprocal of thermal resistance.

**Integration of Newton's Law:**

$$Q = K \cdot \theta \cdot t \quad \text{(for constant } \theta\text{)}$$

For a varying $\theta$:

$$\int dQ = \int K \cdot \theta \cdot dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = K \int_0^t \theta(t) \, dt$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix maps common KTU exam problem types to the relevant formula and key steps.**

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | Steps |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Final temperature rise for a given loss | $P$, $R_{th}$ | $\theta_f$ | $\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th}$ | Direct substitution |
| Temperature after time $t$ (cold start) | $P$, $R_{th}$, $C$, $t$ | $\theta(t)$ | $\theta(t) = \theta_f(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h})$ | Calculate $\theta_f$ first, then apply |
| Cooling to a safe temperature | $\theta_i$, $\tau_c$, target $\theta$ | Time $t$ | $t = \tau_c \ln(\theta_i/\theta)$ | Use $\theta = \theta_i e^{-t/\tau_c}$ and take log |
| Required rating for short-time duty | $\theta_{max}$, $R_{th}$, short time $t_s$ | $P_{short}$ | $\theta_{max} = P \cdot R_{th} (1 - e^{-t_s/\tau_h})$ | Solve for $P$ |
| Motor size for intermittent duty | CDF, $\theta_{max}$ | Effective load | $P_{eq} = P \cdot \sqrt{\text{CDF}}$ | Square-root method |
| Equivalent power for variable load | Variable $P(t)$ profile | $P_{eq}$ | $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum P_i^2 \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}}$ | RMS method |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style):**
> A 15 kW, 400 V, 3-phase induction motor has a full-load copper loss of 600 W and iron + friction loss of 400 W. The thermal resistance is 0.05 °C/W, and the thermal capacitance is 1200 J/°C. The motor operates at rated load from a cold start. Calculate:
> 
> **(a)** The final steady-state temperature rise.
> **(b)** The heating time constant.
> **(c)** The temperature rise after 2 hours.
> **[Assume ambient = 40 °C, use natural logarithm approximations]**

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $P_{cu} = 600$ W
- $P_{iron+friction} = 400$ W
- Total loss $P = 600 + 400 = 1000$ W
- $R_{th} = 0.05$ °C/W
- $C = 1200$ J/°C
- $t = 2$ hours $= 7200$ s

**Part (a) — Final steady-state temperature rise:**

$$\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th} = 1000 \times 0.05 = 50 \, °C$$

**Part (b) — Heating time constant:**

$$\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th} = 1200 \times 0.05 = 60 \text{ seconds} = 1 \text{ minute}$$

**Part (c) — Temperature rise after 2 hours:**

$$t = 7200 \text{ s}, \quad t/\tau_h = 7200/60 = 120$$

$$e^{-120} \approx 0 \text{ (practically zero)}$$

$$\theta(7200) = 50(1 - 0) = 50 \, °C$$

$$\text{Absolute hot-spot temperature} = 40 + 50 = 90 \, °C$$

This confirms that the machine has reached thermal equilibrium in 2 hours (which is $120\tau_h$, far exceeding the $5\tau_h$ rule).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heating and Cooling Curve — Schematic Representation

The following block-level architecture depicts the **thermal model** of an electrical machine (treated as a first-order system) and its relation to the heating and cooling curves.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply 400V AC] --> B[Electrical Machine Motor]
    B -- Power Loss P watts --> C[Thermal Mass Body Windings Core]
    C -- Heat Stored C times dTheta --> D[Temperature Rise theta degree C]
    D -- Heat Dissipated theta divided by Rth --> E[Ambient Air 40 degree C]
    C -- Heating Time Constant tau h --> F[Heating Curve Exponential Rise]
    C -- Cooling Time Constant tau c --> G[Cooling Curve Exponential Decay]
    F --> H[Final Steady State theta f]
    G --> I[Ambient Equilibrium 0 degree C]
    H --> J[Insulation Class Limit]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Safe Motor Operation]
```

## 4.2 Eight Classes of Duty — Master Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classes of Duty IEC 60034-1] --> B[S1 Continuous Running]
    A --> C[S2 Short Time Duty]
    A --> D[S3 Intermittent Periodic]
    A --> E[S4 Intermittent with Starting]
    A --> F[S5 Intermittent with Starting and Braking]
    A --> G[S6 Continuous Periodic]
    A --> H[S7 Continuous with Starting and Braking]
    A --> I[S8 Continuous with Load Speed Changes]
    
    B --> B1[Steady Load Thermal Equilibrium Pumps Fans]
    C --> C1[Short Run Long Rest Sluice Gates]
    D --> D1[Cycle with Rest Period Presses Hoists]
    E --> E1[Cycle plus Starting Currents Machine Tools]
    F --> F1[Cycle plus Braking Reversal Cranes]
    G --> G1[Load and No Load No Rest Printing Presses]
    H --> H1[Load No Load plus Braking Rolling Mills]
    I --> I1[Variable Speed Pole Changing Multi Speed Drives]
```

## 4.3 Heating Curve — Graphical Form (Mermaid XY Chart)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Heating Curve of an Electrical Machine"
    x-axis "Time (multiples of tau_h)" [0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    y-axis "Temperature Rise theta (degree C)" 0 --> 100
    line [0, 39, 63, 78, 86, 95, 98, 99]
```

> **Reading the chart:**
> - At $t = 0$: $\theta = 0$ (cold start)
> - At $t = 1\tau_h$: $\theta = 63.2\%$ of $\theta_f$
> - At $t = 5\tau_h$: $\theta \approx 99.3\%$ of $\theta_f$ (thermal equilibrium reached)

## 4.4 Cooling Curve — Graphical Form (Mermaid XY Chart)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Cooling Curve of an Electrical Machine"
    x-axis "Time (multiples of tau_c)" [0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    y-axis "Temperature Rise theta (degree C)" 0 --> 100
    line [100, 61, 37, 22, 14, 5, 2, 1]
```

> **Reading the chart:**
> - At $t = 0$: $\theta = \theta_i = 100\%$
> - At $t = 1\tau_c$: $\theta = 36.8\%$ of $\theta_i$
> - At $t = 5\tau_c$: $\theta \approx 0.7\%$ of $\theta_i$ (fully cooled)

## 4.5 Thermal Equivalence — RC Circuit Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Thermal Model
        S[Current Source P watts] --> R[Thermal Resistance Rth degree C per W]
        R --> C[Thermal Capacitance C J per degree C]
        C --> G[Ground 0 degree C]
    end
    S -.Heat Input.-> M[Motor Winding]
    M -.Temperature Rise theta.-> R
    C -.Heat Storage.-> M
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the standard **electrical RC analogy** — current source $P$ (heat), resistor $R_{th}$ (dissipation), capacitor $C$ (storage), and node voltage $\theta$ (temperature rise). The time constant $\tau = R_{th} \times C$ governs the exponential behavior.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define the heating time constant of an electrical machine. Explain its significance.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The **heating time constant $\tau_h$** of an electrical machine is defined as the time required for the temperature rise $\theta$ of the machine to reach **63.2% (i.e., $1 - 1/e$)** of its final steady-state value $\theta_f$, starting from a cold state (ambient temperature).

> **Significance (2 marks):** 
> 1. It indicates the **thermal inertia** of the machine — a larger $\tau_h$ means the machine takes longer to heat up but also more energy storage capacity.
> 2. It determines the **safe operating duration** for short-time and intermittent duties.
> 3. It is given by $\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th}$, where $C$ is the thermal capacitance (J/°C) and $R_{th}$ is the thermal resistance (°C/W).
> 4. **At $t = 5\tau_h$**, the machine is considered to have reached **thermal equilibrium** ($\theta \approx 0.993\,\theta_f$).

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between the heating and cooling time constants of a self-ventilated machine.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | Heating Time Constant $\tau_h$ | Cooling Time Constant $\tau_c$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Time for $\theta$ to reach 63.2% of $\theta_f$ during heating | Time for $\theta$ to fall to 36.8% of $\theta_i$ during cooling |
> | **State of machine** | Rotor is rotating (load applied) | Rotor is stationary (load removed) |
> | **Cooling mode** | Forced air cooling (shaft-mounted fan) | Natural convection only (no fan action) |
> | **Thermal resistance** | $R_{th}$ (small — forced air) | $R_{th}'$ (large — natural convection) |
> | **Magnitude** | Smaller | **Larger (typically 2 to 3 times $\tau_h$)** |
> | **Equation** | $\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th}$ | $\tau_c = C \cdot R_{th}'$ |
> 
> For **separately ventilated (TEFV)** machines, $\tau_c \approx \tau_h$ since the external fan continues to operate even when the motor is de-energized.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the heating curve of an electrical machine, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 10 kW, 230 V DC motor has the following losses at full load: copper loss = 500 W, iron loss = 200 W, friction and windage = 100 W. The thermal resistance is 0.06 °C/W and the thermal capacitance is 1500 J/°C. The ambient temperature is 40 °C. Calculate: (i) the final steady-state temperature rise, (ii) the heating time constant, (iii) the time taken to reach 50 °C above ambient, and (iv) the temperature after 1 hour of operation from cold. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer:

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The machine is treated as a **homogeneous body** (lumped thermal model).
2. The heat dissipation is proportional to the temperature rise above ambient (Newton's law of cooling).
3. The power loss $P$ is assumed **constant** during the heating period.
4. The thermal capacitance $C$ and resistance $R_{th}$ are **constant** (no temperature dependence).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Heat balance equation:

$$P \cdot dt = C \cdot d\theta + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} \cdot dt$$

Dividing by $dt$:

$$\frac{C \cdot d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} = P$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta}{C \cdot R_{th}} = \frac{P}{C}$$

At steady state ($t \to \infty$, $d\theta/dt = 0$):

$$\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th}$$

The standard form solution with initial condition $\theta(0) = 0$ (cold start) is:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right)$$

where $\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th}$ is the heating time constant.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Total loss $P = 500 + 200 + 100 = 800$ W
- $R_{th} = 0.06$ °C/W
- $C = 1500$ J/°C
- $\theta_{amb} = 40$ °C

**(i) Final steady-state temperature rise (1.5 marks):**

$$\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th} = 800 \times 0.06 = 48 \, °C$$

**(ii) Heating time constant (1.5 marks):**

$$\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th} = 1500 \times 0.06 = 90 \text{ seconds} = 1.5 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) Time to reach 50 °C rise (2 marks):**

Using $\theta(t) = \theta_f(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h})$:

$$50 = 48(1 - e^{-t/90})$$

$$1 - e^{-t/90} = \frac{50}{48} = 1.0417$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** Since the required temperature rise (50 °C) **exceeds** the final steady-state value (48 °C), the motor **can never reach 50 °C above ambient** at this load. The question is physically infeasible. However, if the answer expects 50 °C rise, the student should note this discrepancy and state that the maximum possible rise is 48 °C.

> **Correct interpretation:** The required $\theta = 50$ °C is greater than $\theta_f = 48$ °C → **Impossible to reach** at this load. The maximum rise is 48 °C, and the time to reach, say, 47 °C would be:

$$47 = 48(1 - e^{-t/90})$$

$$e^{-t/90} = \frac{1}{48} = 0.0208$$

$$t = 90 \ln(48) = 90 \times 3.871 = 348.4 \text{ s} \approx 5.81 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iv) Temperature after 1 hour from cold (2 marks):**

$$t = 3600 \text{ s}, \quad t/\tau_h = 3600/90 = 40$$

$$e^{-40} \approx 0$$

$$\theta(3600) = 48(1 - 0) = 48 \, °C$$

**Absolute temperature** = $40 + 48 = 88$ °C

This is below the **Class B insulation limit** (130 °C) → Motor operates safely.

---

> ### **Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the heating and cooling curves of an electrical machine. Discuss the significance of the heating and cooling time constants. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the different classes of duty (S1 to S8) as per IEC 60034-1 with suitable examples. A motor is rated 10 kW for S3 duty with a cyclic duration factor of 25%. What is the equivalent continuous (S1) rating of the motor? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer:

#### **Part (a) — Heating and Cooling Curves (7 marks):**

**Diagram description (3 marks):**

The heating curve starts from origin and rises **exponentially** to the final steady-state temperature $\theta_f$. Key points:
- $t = 0$: $\theta = 0$ (cold start, ambient)
- $t = \tau_h$: $\theta = 0.632\,\theta_f$
- $t = 5\tau_h$: $\theta \approx 0.993\,\theta_f$ (thermal equilibrium)

The cooling curve is a **mirror exponential decay** that starts at $\theta_f$ and decays to 0:
- $t = 0$: $\theta = \theta_f$ (load removed, hot)
- $t = \tau_c$: $\theta = 0.368\,\theta_f$
- $t = 5\tau_c$: $\theta \approx 0$ (cooled to ambient)

**Significance of time constants (4 marks):**

1. **Heating time constant $\tau_h$:** Determines how quickly the machine reaches its operating temperature. Influences the choice of starting method (direct-on-line vs. soft starter) to limit inrush heating.

2. **Cooling time constant $\tau_c$:** Governs the safe restart time and the required pause between duty cycles. Critical for **S2 and S3** rated machines.

3. **Ratio $\tau_c/\tau_h$:** Indicates the type of cooling — values > 2 indicate self-ventilated machines; values ≈ 1 indicate separately cooled machines.

4. **Thermal overload relay setting:** The relay curve must closely match the heating curve ($\tau_h$) of the protected motor.

#### **Part (b) — Classes of Duty + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Classes of Duty (4 marks):** (See SECTION 2.8 table for the complete description.)

Brief summary:
- **S1** — Continuous steady load
- **S2** — Short duration, then long rest
- **S3** — Intermittent cycles with rest
- **S4** — S3 + significant starting
- **S5** — S4 + electric braking
- **S6** — Load + no-load cycles (no rest)
- **S7** — S6 + braking
- **S8** — Variable load/speed cycles

**Numerical — Equivalent Continuous Rating (3 marks):**

For **S3 duty**, the equivalent continuous (S1) power rating is obtained from the **mean-square (RMS) method**:

$$P_{S1} = P_{S3} \cdot \sqrt{\text{CDF}}$$

Given:
- $P_{S3} = 10$ kW
- CDF = 25% = 0.25

$$P_{S1} = 10 \times \sqrt{0.25} = 10 \times 0.5 = 5 \text{ kW}$$

**Interpretation:** A motor rated 10 kW for S3 at 25% CDF can deliver only 5 kW continuously (S1 duty) because continuous operation does not allow cooling-off periods.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing heating and cooling time constants:** Students often write $\tau_c = \tau_h$. Remember — for self-ventilated motors, $\tau_c > \tau_h$ (typically 2-3 times) because there is no forced cooling during shutdown.
> 2. **Not stating the value of ambient temperature** (40 °C is standard as per IS/IEC). Always add ambient to the temperature rise to get absolute temperature.
> 3. **Forgetting to use consistent units** — if $C$ is in J/°C and $R_{th}$ in °C/W, then $\tau_h$ is in seconds. Do not mix minutes and seconds.
> 4. **Wrong application of CDF formula:** CDF is a ratio (0 to 1), not a percentage. Multiply by 100 only for display.
> 5. **S3 vs S6 confusion:** S3 has de-energized rest; S6 has no-load operation (machine still running). Both are "periodic" but differ in the OFF state.
> 6. **Insulation class limits:** A, E, B, F, H — students often mix these up. Class A = 105 °C, E = 120 °C, B = 130 °C, F = 155 °C, H = 180 °C.
> 7. **Exponential evaluation:** For $t \gg \tau$, do not forget to consider $e^{-t/\tau} \to 0$ — this gives the steady-state value directly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final high-density revision list — Memorize for the KTU exam:**

- [x] **Heating Curve:** $\theta(t) = \theta_f(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h})$ — exponential rise from 0 to $\theta_f$.
- [x] **Cooling Curve:** $\theta(t) = \theta_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$ — exponential decay from $\theta_i$ to 0.
- [x] **General Heating (warm start):** $\theta(t) = \theta_f - (\theta_f - \theta_i) e^{-t/\tau_h}$.
- [x] **Final Temperature Rise:** $\theta_f = P \cdot R_{th}$ (always).
- [x] **Time Constant:** $\tau_h = C \cdot R_{th}$ and $\tau_c = C \cdot R_{th}'$.
- [x] **5$\tau$ Rule:** Machine is at thermal equilibrium after $t = 5\tau$ (99.3% of $\theta_f$).
- [x] **63.2% Rule:** At $t = \tau$, the heating reaches 63.2% of $\theta_f$.
- [x] **Cooling > Heating:** For self-ventilated machines, $\tau_c \approx 2$ to $3 \times \tau_h$.
- [x] **Equivalent S1 Rating from S3:** $P_{S1} = P_{S3} \cdot \sqrt{\text{CDF}}$.
- [x] **CDF Definition:** $\text{CDF} = N/(N+R)$, standard values 15%, 25%, 40%, 60%.
- [x] **Eight Duty Classes (S1–S8):** S1 continuous, S2 short-time, S3 intermittent (rest), S4 intermittent + starting, S5 + braking, S6 periodic (no rest), S7 + braking, S8 variable load/speed.
- [x] **Newton's Law of Cooling:** Heat dissipated $\propto$ temperature difference.
- [x] **Thermal RC Analogy:** Power loss = current source, $R_{th}$ = resistor, $C$ = capacitor, $\theta$ = node voltage.
- [x] **Insulation Limits (°C):** A=105, E=120, B=130, F=155, H=180.
- [x] **Montsinger's Rule:** Every 10 °C above insulation limit → insulation life halves.
- [x] **Equivalent Current (RMS) Method:** $I_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum I_i^2 \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}}$.
- [x] **Ambient Standard:** 40 °C (per IS/IEC).
- [x] **Thermal Overload Relay:** Mimics the $I^2t$ characteristic with class 10, 20, 30 trip curves.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Power Rating for Drive Motors with Regard to Thermal Overloading and Load Variation Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Power Rating for Drive Motors with Regard to Thermal Overloading and Load Variation Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Rating Selection of Drive Motors** is the systematic engineering procedure of choosing an appropriately sized electric motor such that its nameplate rating (in kW / HP) satisfies the mechanical load demand continuously, without exceeding the permissible temperature rise of its insulation class under the given **load variation factors** and **thermal overloading** conditions specified by the duty cycle (S1 to S10, IEC 60034-1).

The selection must account for:
- **Thermal Overloading**: The transient temperature excursion of the motor windings when subjected to short-duration loads exceeding the rated capacity.
- **Load Variation Factors**: The deviation of the actual mechanical load from the rated load over time, including starting, braking, no-load, and cyclic loading phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Focus**: This is a high-weightage topic in Module 1. The examiner expects students to demonstrate mastery over (i) the heating/cooling thermal model, (ii) duty cycle classification S1–S10, and (iii) the *equivalent* current, torque, and power methods for variable-load motor rating.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket and Tap" Model

Imagine a water bucket (the **motor winding**) being filled by a tap whose flow rate depends on how hard the motor works (the **I²R + iron losses**). At the same time, a small hole at the bottom lets water leak out (the **heat dissipation to ambient**).

- If you turn the tap on too hard (overload), the bucket overflows → **insulation burns out**.
- If the tap is just right, water level stabilises → **motor runs at rated temperature**.
- If the tap is pulsed on and off (intermittent duty), the bucket never overflows because water leaks out between pulses → **smaller motor can do a bigger job intermittently**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Because motors dissipate heat *between* load pulses, a motor rated for, say, 10 kW continuously might be able to deliver 25 kW for short bursts — *provided* its thermal time constant allows the temperature to recover between pulses.

## 1.3 Critical Constants & Standards (Must Memorise)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Insulation Class B** | **130 °C** max temperature | Standard industrial limit |
| **Insulation Class F** | **155 °C** max temperature | Common in VFD-fed drives |
| **Insulation Class H** | **180 °C** max temperature | High-temp / traction drives |
| Ambient reference temp | **40 °C** | Standard benchmark (IEC) |
| Typical thermal time constant τ | **30 to 90 minutes** (small to large motors) | Heating/cooling inertia |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermal Model — Heating & Cooling of a Motor

Every motor is fundamentally a **heat exchanger**. Electrical input power converts into:
- **Useful mechanical power** at the shaft (P_out)
- **Losses** (P_loss) — copper (I²R), iron (core), friction, and windage

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss}$$

These losses manifest as heat. The governing differential equation of the motor's thermal behaviour:

$$P_{loss}\,dt = C\,d\theta + H\,\theta\,dt$$

Where:
- $C$ = **thermal capacity** of the motor (J/°C)
- $H$ = **heat dissipation coefficient** (W/°C) — heat lost to ambient per °C rise
- $\theta$ = **temperature rise** above ambient (°C)
- $\theta_\infty = P_{loss}/H$ = **final steady-state temperature rise**

### 2.1.1 Heating Phase (Constant Loss Applied from Cold)

$$\theta(t) = \theta_\infty \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)$$

### 2.1.2 Cooling Phase (Power Removed, Cooling from Initial Rise θ₀)

$$\theta(t) = \theta_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_{th}'}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical distinction**: $\tau_{th}$ (heating time constant) ≠ $\tau_{th}'$ (cooling time constant). For *self-cooled* motors, $\tau_{th}' \approx 2\tau_{th}$ to $4\tau_{th}$ because the cooling fan slows down (or stops entirely) when the motor is de-energised. For *separately-cooled* motors, $\tau_{th}' \approx \tau_{th}$.

## 2.2 Duty Cycles — IEC 60034-1 Classification (S1 to S10)

| Duty | Name | Description | Selection Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Continuous duty | Constant load until thermal equilibrium | Select rated power ≥ load power |
| **S2** | Short-time duty | Run for short time, then long rest | Use short-time rating curves |
| **S3** | Intermittent periodic duty | Sequence of identical cycles (no starting) | Equivalent power method |
| **S4** | Intermittent with starting | Cycles include starting transients | Include starting losses |
| **S5** | Intermittent with braking | Cycles include electric braking | Include braking losses |
| **S6** | Continuous with intermittent load | Sequence of identical loading/unloading cycles | Equivalent method |
| **S7** | Continuous with electric braking | Long braking cycles | |
| **S8** | Continuous with pole-changing | Load & speed changes in cycle | |
| **S9** | Non-periodic duty | Random load/speed variations | Statistical / mean method |
| **S10** | Discrete constant loads | Multiple distinct load points | Per-point evaluation |

## 2.3 Equivalent Methods for Variable-Load Selection

When a motor is subjected to a **variable load cycle** (e.g., a press, crane, conveyor), direct rating is impossible. Instead, we calculate an **equivalent constant load** that produces the same total heating.

### 2.3.1 Equivalent Current Method (Most Accurate)
Valid when **speed ≈ constant** and power factor ≈ constant:

$$I_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i^{\,2}\,t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}}$$

### 2.3.2 Equivalent Torque Method
Valid when **speed varies but flux is constant**:

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{\,2}\,t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}}$$

### 2.3.3 Equivalent Power Method
Valid when **speed is approximately constant** and torque ∝ power:

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i^{\,2}\,t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta(t)=\theta_\infty(1-e^{-t/\tau})$ | Heating from cold | °C, s |
| 2 | $\theta(t)=\theta_0\,e^{-t/\tau'}$ | Cooling from initial θ₀ | °C, s |
| 3 | $\theta_\infty = P_{loss}/H$ | Final temperature rise | °C |
| 4 | $\tau = C/H$ | Thermal time constant | s |
| 5 | $I_{eq}=\sqrt{\sum I_i^2 t_i / \sum t_i}$ | Variable current load | A |
| 6 | $T_{eq}=\sqrt{\sum T_i^2 t_i / \sum t_i}$ | Variable torque load | N·m |
| 7 | $P_{eq}=\sqrt{\sum P_i^2 t_i / \sum t_i}$ | Variable power load | W |
| 8 | $P_{rated} \ge P_{load} \times S_F$ | Apply service factor | W |
| 9 | $OL_f = P_{peak}/P_{rated}$ | Overload factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{motor} = P_{load}/\eta_{motor}$ | Account for motor efficiency | W |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Heating Curve Equation

### Starting Point — Energy Balance

In an infinitesimal time $dt$, the heat generated by losses must equal the heat stored in the motor mass **plus** the heat dissipated to the surroundings.

$$P_{loss}\,dt = \underbrace{C\,d\theta}_{\text{heat stored}} + \underbrace{H\,\theta\,dt}_{\text{heat lost to ambient}}$$

### Step 1 — Rearrange into a Standard First-Order ODE

$$C\,\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + H\,\theta = P_{loss}$$

Divide through by $C$:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + \dfrac{H}{C}\,\theta = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{C}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Thermal Time Constant

Define:

$$\tau_{th} = \dfrac{C}{H}$$

The ODE becomes:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{th}}\,\theta = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{C}$$

### Step 3 — Solve via Integrating Factor

The integrating factor is $e^{t/\tau_{th}}$:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\theta\,e^{t/\tau_{th}}\right) = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{C}\,e^{t/\tau_{th}}$$

Integrate from 0 to $t$:

$$\theta\,e^{t/\tau_{th}} - \theta(0) = \dfrac{P_{loss}\,\tau_{th}}{C}\left(e^{t/\tau_{th}} - 1\right)$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using $\tau_{th} = C/H$

$$\dfrac{P_{loss}\,\tau_{th}}{C} = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{H} = \theta_\infty$$

### Step 5 — Final Heating Equation (Cold Start, θ(0)=0)

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_\infty \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Final Cooling Equation (Power Removed at t = 0, θ(0)=θ₀)

Setting $P_{loss} = 0$ in the ODE and solving with $\theta(0)=\theta_0$:

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_0\,e^{-t/\tau_{th}'}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cooling time constant $\tau_{th}'$ is often **larger** than $\tau_{th}$ because in self-ventilated motors the cooling fan stops when the motor is switched off, reducing $H$ and thus increasing $\tau_{th}' = C/H'$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Power Method

### Physical Premise
A variable load cycle $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ acting for durations $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ produces the same total losses as a constant equivalent load $P_{eq}$ acting for the total cycle duration $T = \sum t_i$.

### Step 1 — Express Losses for Each Phase
For a motor operating at near-constant speed, losses = constant loss + variable loss (∝ I² ∝ P²):

$$L_i = k_c + k_v P_i^2$$

where $k_c$ = constant (iron + friction) losses and $k_v$ = variable (copper) loss coefficient.

### Step 2 — Total Losses in the Variable Cycle

$$L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(k_c + k_v P_i^2)t_i = k_c\sum t_i + k_v\sum P_i^2 t_i$$

### Step 3 — Total Losses in the Equivalent Cycle

$$L_{eq} = (k_c + k_v P_{eq}^2)T = k_c T + k_v P_{eq}^2 T$$

### Step 4 — Equate $L_{total} = L_{eq}$ and Solve

$$k_c T + k_v P_{eq}^2 T = k_c T + k_v \sum P_i^2 t_i$$

Cancelling common terms:

$$P_{eq}^2 T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i^2 t_i$$

$$\boxed{P_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i^2 t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}}}$$

### Step 5 — Selection Criterion
The motor rated power must satisfy:

$$P_{rated} \ge P_{eq} \times \text{safety margin (typically 1.1 to 1.25)}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem**: A motor drives a load with the following cycle:
> - 20 A for 30 s, 35 A for 20 s, 50 A for 10 s, then rest for 40 s.
> - Calculate the equivalent current. If the motor is rated at 40 A continuous, can it handle this cycle? Justify.

### Solution

Step 1 — Compute numerator $\sum I_i^2 t_i$:

$$\sum I_i^2 t_i = (20)^2(30) + (35)^2(20) + (50)^2(10) + (0)^2(40)$$
$$= (400)(30) + (1225)(20) + (2500)(10) + 0$$
$$= 12{,}000 + 24{,}500 + 25{,}000 = 61{,}500 \;\text{A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$

Step 2 — Compute total time:

$$\sum t_i = 30 + 20 + 10 + 40 = 100\;\text{s}$$

Step 3 — Compute equivalent current:

$$I_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{61{,}500}{100}} = \sqrt{615} \approx 24.8\;\text{A}$$

Step 4 — Compare with rated current:

$$I_{eq} = 24.8\;\text{A} \;<\; 40\;\text{A} = I_{rated} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Conclusion**: The 40 A motor is thermally suitable for this duty cycle. The peak current of 50 A is well within the short-term thermal overload capability of a standard induction motor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thermal Equivalent Circuit (Block Architecture)

The motor's thermal behaviour maps directly to an **electrical RC circuit analogue**:

- Thermal capacity $C$ ↔ Capacitor $C$
- Heat dissipation $H$ ↔ Conductance $1/R$
- Temperature rise $\theta$ ↔ Voltage
- Loss power $P_{loss}$ ↔ Current source

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Power Input P_in] --> B[P_loss Source]
    B --> C{Thermal Capacitor C}
    C --> D[Temperature Rise theta]
    D --> E[Heat Dissipation H]
    E --> F[Ambient at 40 C]
    G[Steady State theta_inf] --> D
    H[Time Constant tau = C over H] --> D
```

## 4.2 Heating and Cooling Curve Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HEATING
    H1[Power ON t equals 0] --> H2[Temperature rises exponentially]
    H2 --> H3[Asymptote theta_inf at P_loss over H]
    H3 --> H4[Time to reach 0.632 theta_inf equals tau_th]
    end
    subgraph COOLING
    C1[Power OFF t equals 0] --> C2[Temperature decays exponentially]
    C2 --> C3[Reaches ambient after approx 4 tau_th]
    C3 --> C4[Cooling tau_th prime larger than tau_th for self cooled motors]
    end
    H4 --> C1
```

## 4.3 Motor Power Rating Selection Flow Chart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Identify Load Profile] --> B{Duty Type}
    B -->|S1 Continuous| C[Select P_rated ge P_load]
    B -->|S2 Short Time| D[Use short time rating chart]
    B -->|S3 to S6 Cyclic| E[Apply Equivalent Method]
    B -->|S9 Variable| F[Statistical Mean Method]
    E --> G[Compute P_eq or I_eq or T_eq]
    G --> H{Include Starting Losses?}
    H -->|Yes S4 S5| I[Augment numerator with starting I_squared t]
    H -->|No S3 S6| J[Direct Equivalent Formula]
    I --> K[Select P_rated ge P_eq x SF]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Check Thermal Limit}
    L -->|theta_max within class| M[Motor Selected]
    L -->|Exceeds insulation| N[Go to Next Higher Frame Size]
    N --> K
    F --> M
    C --> M
    D --> M
```

## 4.4 Variable Load Cycle Diagram (Typical Industrial Drive)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Time t equals 0] --> P1[Phase 1 Load P1 duration t1]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 2 Load P2 duration t2]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Load P3 duration t3]
    P3 --> P4[Rest or Light Load duration t4]
    P4 --> P5[Cycle Repeats Period T]
    P5 --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "thermal time constant" of an electric motor and explain why the cooling time constant is usually larger than the heating time constant in self-ventilated motors.**

**Model Answer**:
The **thermal time constant** $\tau_{th}$ is defined as the time required for the motor temperature rise to reach $63.2\%$ of its final steady-state value $\theta_\infty$ when a constant loss is applied starting from cold. Mathematically:

$$\tau_{th} = \dfrac{C}{H}$$

where $C$ = thermal capacity (J/°C) and $H$ = heat dissipation coefficient (W/°C).

For self-ventilated (TEFC) motors, the cooling fan is mounted on the shaft. When the motor is switched off, the fan stops, drastically reducing convective heat transfer. Hence $H$ decreases, while $C$ remains constant, causing $\tau_{th}' = C/H' \gg \tau_{th}$. Typically, $\tau_{th}' \approx 2\tau_{th}$ to $4\tau_{th}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four standard duty cycles as per IEC 60034-1 and state the key difference between S1 and S2 duties.**

**Model Answer**:
Four standard duties: **S1 (continuous), S2 (short-time), S3 (intermittent periodic), S6 (continuous with intermittent load).**

**Key Difference**:
- **S1**: The motor operates at constant load long enough to reach **thermal equilibrium** (steady-state temperature). Rating is selected such that continuous load ≤ rated power.
- **S2**: The motor operates for a **specified short duration** (e.g., 10, 30, 60, 90 min), followed by a long de-energised rest period sufficient to re-cool to ambient. Short-time rating can exceed continuous rating because the winding never reaches thermal equilibrium.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]`

**a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the heating curve equation of an electric motor, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A motor is subjected to a variable load cycle as follows:
- Phase 1: 10 kW for 20 s
- Phase 2: 20 kW for 15 s
- Phase 3: 30 kW for 10 s
- Phase 4: Rest for 25 s

Determine the equivalent continuous power rating of the motor. Recommend a suitable standard motor rating with a 15% safety margin. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions**:
1. Motor treated as a **lumped thermal mass** (single time constant).
2. **Constant losses** during the heating period.
3. **Heat dissipation** is proportional to temperature rise above ambient (Newton's law of cooling).
4. Thermal capacity $C$ and dissipation coefficient $H$ are **constant**.

**Derivation**:
Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps:

$$C\,\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + H\,\theta = P_{loss}$$

With $\tau = C/H$ and $\theta_\infty = P_{loss}/H$:

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_\infty(1 - e^{-t/\tau})}$$

The sketch shows exponential rise from 0 with asymptote $\theta_\infty$, reaching $0.632\theta_\infty$ at $t = \tau$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1** — Numerator $\sum P_i^2 t_i$:

$$10^2 \times 20 + 20^2 \times 15 + 30^2 \times 10 + 0^2 \times 25$$
$$= 2000 + 6000 + 9000 + 0 = 17{,}000\;\text{kW}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$

**Step 2** — Denominator $\sum t_i = 20 + 15 + 10 + 25 = 70\;\text{s}$

**Step 3** — Equivalent power:

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{17{,}000/70} = \sqrt{242.86} \approx 15.58\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 4** — Apply 15% safety margin:

$$P_{design} = 15.58 \times 1.15 = 17.92\;\text{kW}$$

**Recommended Standard Motor**: Select a **18.5 kW (or 20 HP)** standard industrial induction motor from the next-higher IEC frame size (e.g., 160L frame).

---

### Question B — `[Alternative Internal Choice, 14 Marks]`

**a)** Explain the equivalent current, torque, and power methods for rating motors with variable load cycles. State the conditions under which each method is applicable. **(7 marks)**

**b)** The following current values are taken by a motor during a duty cycle: 30 A for 40 s, 50 A for 30 s, 20 A for 20 s, and no-load (0 A) for 10 s. If the motor is rated at 35 A continuous duty, determine whether it is suitable. If not, suggest the next standard rating. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Method | Formula | Applicable When |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalent Current | $I_{eq} = \sqrt{\sum I_i^2 t_i / \sum t_i}$ | Flux & power factor approximately constant |
| Equivalent Torque | $T_{eq} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2 t_i / \sum t_i}$ | Flux constant but speed varies |
| Equivalent Power | $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\sum P_i^2 t_i / \sum t_i}$ | Speed approximately constant |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1** — Numerator:

$$30^2 \times 40 + 50^2 \times 30 + 20^2 \times 20 + 0^2 \times 10$$
$$= 36{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 8000 + 0 = 119{,}000\;\text{A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$

**Step 2** — Denominator: $40 + 30 + 20 + 10 = 100\;\text{s}$

**Step 3** — Equivalent current:

$$I_{eq} = \sqrt{119{,}000/100} = \sqrt{1190} \approx 34.5\;\text{A}$$

**Step 4** — Comparison with rated 35 A:

$$I_{eq} = 34.5\;\text{A} < 35\;\text{A} = I_{rated}$$

**Conclusion**: The 35 A motor is **marginally suitable** but with no safety margin. **Recommended**: Use a **40 A** standard motor to ensure adequate thermal reserve and account for supply voltage variation and ambient temperature rise.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls** — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:
> 1. **Forgetting to square** the current/torque/power values — this is the single most common error.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean** instead of RMS (root mean square) for the equivalent — examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 3. **Ignoring starting and braking losses** in S4 and S5 duties — must add $I_{start}^2 \cdot t_{start}$ to numerator.
> 4. **Not applying a service/safety factor** when recommending the final motor rating.
> 5. **Mixing up heating and cooling time constants** — remember $\tau_{cool} > \tau_{heat}$ for self-ventilated motors.
> 6. **Stating $P_{motor} = P_{load}$** without accounting for motor efficiency — required power at motor shaft must be $P_{load}/\eta_{motor}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **thermal model** of a motor is mathematically identical to an **RC charging circuit**.
- ✅ **Thermal time constant** $\tau = C/H$ — time to reach **63.2%** of final temperature rise.
- ✅ **Heating equation**: $\theta(t) = \theta_\infty (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- ✅ **Cooling equation**: $\theta(t) = \theta_0 e^{-t/\tau'}$ — with $\tau' > \tau$ for self-cooled motors.
- ✅ **Duty cycles S1–S10** are defined by IEC 60034-1 — memorise at least S1, S2, S3, S4, S6.
- ✅ **Equivalent methods** are RMS-type averaging — *always square, average, then square root*.
- ✅ Select motor such that **$P_{rated} \ge P_{eq} \times \text{safety factor}$** (typically 1.1 to 1.25).
- ✅ **Insulation classes**: B (130°C), F (155°C), H (180°C); ambient reference is **40°C**.
- ✅ **Starting losses** matter for S4, S5 duties — must augment the equivalent formula.
- ✅ **Equivalent current** is the most general method; **equivalent power** assumes constant speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Characteristics - Speed-Torque Characteristics of Various Types of Load and Drive Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Characteristics - Speed-Torque Characteristics of Various Types of Load and Drive Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanical Characteristics in Electrical Drives

In any **Industrial Electric Drive** system, the **mechanical characteristic** is the graphical (or mathematical) relationship between the **shaft speed (N)** and the **electromagnetic torque (T)** developed by either the motor or demanded by the load. It is the most fundamental signature used to evaluate, compare, and match machines to their driven equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The *speed–torque characteristic* (also called the *mechanical characteristic*) of a drive is a plot of motor (or load) speed on the X-axis and developed (or demanded) torque on the Y-axis. It is the foundation for **drive selection, stability analysis, starting performance, and dynamic equilibrium** of the mechanical coupling.

Mathematically, a generic mechanical characteristic is expressed as:

$$T = f(N)$$

where **T** is the electromagnetic torque in **N·m** and **N** is the rotational speed in **rpm**.

## 1.2 Why This Topic Matters (KTU Module 2 Context)

A motor and its mechanical load are *coupled* at the same shaft. The drive will operate stably only at the intersection of the two characteristics. Without understanding these curves:

- A motor may **stall** (insufficient starting torque),
- Operate at an **unintended speed** (e.g., unstable operating point),
- Overheat, draw excessive current, or fail to start the load.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Hands Pushing a Cart"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for first-time learners:** Imagine two people on roller skates pushing a heavy cart. One person is the **motor** (pushes forward), the other is the **load** (pushes back via friction/gravity). The cart accelerates until the pushing force equals the resisting force. The *speed–torque curve* of each is like their "force-versus-speed" behavior. If the pusher's strength drops too quickly with speed, the cart never starts; if the load's resistance grows too fast, the pusher is shoved backward (motor stalls). Equilibrium = where curves intersect.

## 1.4 Classification — Loads vs. Motors

A drive is evaluated by comparing **two** speed–torque curves simultaneously:

| Element | Curve Name | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Load** | Load Torque Characteristic ($T_L$ vs $N$) | Mechanical process being driven |
| **Motor** | Motor Torque Characteristic ($T_m$ vs $N$) | Built-in electromagnetic property |

The stable operating point occurs where:

$$T_m = T_L \quad \text{(and} \quad \frac{dT_m}{dN} < \frac{dT_L}{dN} \text{ for stability)}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Load Torque Characteristics

Industrial loads are grouped into **four major classes** based on how their resisting torque varies with speed. The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus explicitly lists the first three as compulsory, with the fourth as a critical addition.

### 2.1.1 Constant Torque Load

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A load in which the demanded torque remains *approximately constant* regardless of speed.

**Examples:** Conveyors, hoists, winches, elevators, compressors, positive-displacement pumps, reciprocating machines, extruders.

**Equation:**

$$T_L = \text{constant} = K_c$$

**Physical Reason:** The work done per revolution is fixed; friction and material handling torque dominate, neither depending strongly on speed.

### 2.1.2 Quadratic Torque (Parabolic) Load

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A load whose torque requirement is *proportional to the square of the speed*.

**Examples:** Centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, propellers, mixers, compressors (centrifugal type), and similar fluid/kinetic-energy devices.

**Equation:**

$$T_L = K_q \, N^2$$

**Physical Reason:** The load involves moving a fluid or imparting kinetic energy. Power required scales as $N^3$, so torque scales as $N^2$.

**Power relation:**

$$P_L = T_L \cdot \omega = K_q N^2 \cdot N = K_q N^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_L \propto N^3$$

### 2.1.3 Linear Torque Load

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A load whose torque requirement increases *linearly* with speed.

**Examples:** Some calenders, mixers, and viscous-friction-dominated machines. Less common as a pure category — often a transitional form.

**Equation:**

$$T_L = K_\ell \, N$$

### 2.1.4 Constant Power Load

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A load that demands a *constant power* across a speed range, meaning torque *decreases inversely* with speed.

**Examples:** Machine tool spindle drives, coil winders/unwinders, traction drives, electric vehicles, centre-driven lathes.

**Equation:**

$$P_L = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_L = \frac{P_L}{\omega} = \frac{K_p}{N}$$

**Physical Reason:** For winding a sheet at constant linear tension, as the roll radius grows, speed must fall to maintain material tension — torque must rise inversely.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Motor Speed–Torque Characteristics

### 2.2.1 DC Shunt Motor

Field current is held constant by a shunt field. Armature reaction and supply variations cause a very small *drop* in speed as load increases.

**Equation:**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$$

$$T = K \phi \, I_a$$

Slope: Speed drops *very slightly* as torque increases — essentially a **shallowly drooping straight line** (nearly constant-speed family).

**Feature:** Good speed regulation, no high starting torque, suitable for **constant-speed** applications.

### 2.2.2 DC Series Motor

Field winding in series with armature; flux $\phi$ is proportional to armature current (up to saturation).

**Equation (unsaturated):**

$$\phi \propto I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = K \phi I_a = K' I_a^2$$

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T}{K'}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{V}{K \phi} - \frac{R_a I_a}{K \phi}$$

Eliminating $I_a$ between $N$ and $T$ gives the **inverse-square** speed–torque relation:

$$N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$

**Feature:** Extremely high starting torque, but no-load runaway risk; perfect for **traction, cranes, hoists**.

### 2.2.3 DC Compound Motor

A combination of shunt and series. Two sub-types:

- **Cumulative Compound:** Series flux *aids* shunt flux → high starting torque, moderate speed regulation.
- **Differential Compound:** Series flux *opposes* shunt flux → very flat characteristic; rarely used due to instability.

### 2.2.4 Three-Phase Induction Motor (Squirrel Cage)

The most widely used industrial motor. Torque depends on **slip (s)**:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}$$

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot \frac{R_r}{s}}{\left( \frac{R_r}{s} \right)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$

**Key Points on the Curve:**

- **Starting Torque** $T_{st}$: at $s = 1$.
- **Maximum (Pull-out) Torque** $T_{max}$: occurs at slip $s_{max} = \frac{R_r}{X_s + X_r}$.
- **Slip at Maximum Torque is independent of $V$**, but $T_{max} \propto V^2$.

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s (X_s + X_r)}$$

**Normal operating region:** Near $s \approx 0.02$–$0.05$ (linear, near-constant-speed).

### 2.2.5 Three-Phase Synchronous Motor

**True constant-speed** motor: speed is locked to supply frequency.

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

Torque angle $\delta$ determines torque (salient-pole or cylindrical rotor):

$$T = \frac{3 V E}{X_s \omega_s} \sin \delta$$

There is **no starting torque** inherently — it must be brought up to near-synchronous speed by auxiliary means (damper winding, VFD, pony motor, etc.).

### 2.2.6 Special-Purpose Motors

- **Brushless DC (BLDC):** Trapezoidal speed–torque, electronically commutated.
- **Reluctance Motor / Switched Reluctance:** High starting torque, robust construction.
- **Stepper Motor:** Open-loop position control, holding torque at zero speed.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following compact table consolidates **all equations** the KTU 2024 examiner expects for a 14-mark derivation or comparison problem.

| # | Equation / Law | Symbol Meaning | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = K \phi I_a$ | Motor torque constant $K$ | All DC motors |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$ | Speed equation | DC motors |
| 3 | $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$ (unsaturated series DC) | Inverse-square law | DC series motor |
| 4 | $T \propto s$ (small $s$, linear region) | Linear torque–slip | Induction motor |
| 5 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s (X_s + X_r)}$ | Pull-out torque | Induction motor |
| 6 | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_s + X_r}$ | Slip at $T_{max}$ | Induction motor |
| 7 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | Synchronous/induction |
| 8 | $T = \dfrac{3 V E}{X_s \omega_s} \sin \delta$ | Torque angle | Synchronous motor |
| 9 | $T_L = K_c$ (constant) | Constant torque load | Hoists, conveyors |
| 10 | $T_L = K_q N^2$ | Quadratic load | Fans, pumps |
| 11 | $T_L = K_p / N$ | Constant power load | Winders, lathes |
| 12 | $T_{acc} = T_m - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Newton's law for drives | Stability & dynamics |
| 13 | $P = T \cdot \omega = 2\pi N T / 60$ | Power in W (N in rpm, T in N·m) | All drives |
| 14 | $\dfrac{dT_m}{dN} < \dfrac{dT_L}{dN}$ | Stability criterion | Operating point |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In practice, a controls engineer uses these characteristics to (i) **size** the motor to handle the load's worst-case demand, (ii) ensure the motor's starting torque exceeds the load's breakaway torque, (iii) guarantee stable operation, and (iv) choose a starter/VFD if the natural starting current is unacceptable.

---

## 2.4 The Stability Condition (KTU Favourite)

A drive equilibrium point $(N_0, T_0)$ is **stable** only if a small perturbation causes the system to return. From the equation of motion:

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_m(\omega) - T_L(\omega)$$

Stability requires that an increase in speed must cause motor torque to drop *faster* than load torque:

$$\left. \frac{\partial T_m}{\partial \omega} \right|_{\omega_0} < \left. \frac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega} \right|_{\omega_0}$$

This is the classical "**negative slope steeper than positive slope**" rule at the intersection. It is why a **differential compound DC motor** (positive slope for motor) is unstable, and why **series motors** must never be started without load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Speed–Torque Relation of a DC Series Motor

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 2 university exams.

**Starting Equations:**

Back-emf of a DC motor:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

But for a series motor, $E_b = K \phi \omega$, and the field flux (unsaturated) is:

$$\phi = k_f I_a$$

Therefore:

$$E_b = K (k_f I_a) \omega = K k_f I_a \omega$$

**Step 1 — Express $E_b$ in terms of $I_a$ and $\omega$:**

$$E_b = K k_f I_a \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into back-emf equation:**

$$K k_f I_a \omega = V - I_a R_a$$

**Step 3 — Express electromagnetic torque:**

$$T = K \phi I_a = K (k_f I_a) I_a = K k_f I_a^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $I_a$ from torque equation:**

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T}{K k_f}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\omega$ from back-emf equation:**

$$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K k_f I_a} = \frac{V}{K k_f I_a} - \frac{R_a}{K k_f}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I_a$ from Step 4 into Step 5:**

$$\omega = \frac{V}{K k_f \sqrt{\frac{T}{K k_f}}} - \frac{R_a}{K k_f}$$

$$\omega = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f T}} - \frac{R_a}{K k_f}$$

**Step 7 — Convert $\omega$ to speed in rpm ($N = 60\omega / 2\pi$):**

$$N = \frac{60}{2\pi} \left( \frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f T}} - \frac{R_a}{K k_f} \right)$$

**Step 8 — Final speed–torque equation:**

$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{A}{\sqrt{T}} - B\,}$$

where $A = \dfrac{60 V}{2\pi \sqrt{K k_f}}$ and $B = \dfrac{60 R_a}{2\pi K k_f}$ are machine constants.

This is the **inverse-square speed–torque law** of an unsaturated DC series motor — the *exact* equation KTU examiners expect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical check:** As $T \to 0$ (no load), $N \to \infty$ — the famous "**series-motor runaway**" hazard. As $T \to T_{max}$ (stall), $N \to 0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Condition for Maximum Torque in a Three-Phase Induction Motor

**Starting equation (per-phase equivalent circuit torque):**

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot (R_r / s)}{(R_r / s)^2 + X_{eq}^2}$$

where $X_{eq} = X_s + X_r$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $T$ with respect to slip $s$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite the torque in a more convenient form.** Let $R = R_r / s$. Then $dR/ds = -R_r / s^2 = -R/s$. So:

$$T = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R}{R^2 + X_{eq}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $R$:**

$$\frac{dT}{dR} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R^2 + X_{eq}^2) - R \cdot 2R}{(R^2 + X_{eq}^2)^2} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X_{eq}^2 - R^2}{(R^2 + X_{eq}^2)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Set derivative to zero:**

$$X_{eq}^2 - R^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = X_{eq}$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to slip.** Since $R = R_r / s$:

$$\frac{R_r}{s_{max}} = X_{eq} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{max} = \frac{R_r}{X_s + X_r}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $T_{max}$ by substituting $R = X_{eq}$:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X_{eq}}{X_{eq}^2 + X_{eq}^2} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X_{eq}}$$

**Final boxed results:**

$$\boxed{\,s_{max} = \frac{R_r}{X_s + X_r}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s (X_s + X_r)}\,}$$

**Key engineering insight:** $T_{max} \propto V^2$, so a **drop in voltage reduces pull-out torque dramatically** — a 10% voltage drop cuts $T_{max}$ by ~19%.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Load vs. Motor Suitability

| Load Type | Required Starting Torque | Ideal Motor Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Torque** (conveyor) | High | DC compound, Induction motor (slip-ring with rotor resistance starter) | Motor must produce constant torque across speed range |
| **Quadratic Torque** (fan) | Low (≈ 15–30% rated) | Squirrel-cage induction, BLDC | Motor torque naturally rises with slip in low-slope region |
| **Linear Torque** (mixer) | Moderate | DC shunt or AC induction | Linear speed-torque complements linear load |
| **Constant Power** (winder) | Varies (high at low speed) | DC series + field weakening, Vector-controlled induction | Motor torque must fall as speed rises |

---

## 3.4 Quantitative Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A DC series motor runs at 1000 rpm drawing 50 A from a 220 V DC supply. $R_a = 0.25 \, \Omega$. Find the speed when the current rises to 80 A. Assume linear magnetic circuit.

**Step 1 — Back-emf at 50 A:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 220 - 50 \times 0.25 = 220 - 12.5 = 207.5 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Speed at 50 A (from $E_b \propto N\phi$, and $\phi \propto I_a$):**

$$E_{b1} = K \phi_1 N_1 = K k_f I_{a1} N_1$$

$$207.5 = K k_f \cdot 50 \cdot 1000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K k_f = \frac{207.5}{50000} = 4.15 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Back-emf at 80 A:**

$$E_{b2} = 220 - 80 \times 0.25 = 220 - 20 = 200 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Speed at 80 A:**

$$E_{b2} = K k_f \cdot I_{a2} \cdot N_2$$

$$200 = (4.15 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 80 \cdot N_2$$

$$N_2 = \frac{200}{4.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 80} = \frac{200}{0.332} = 602.4 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Result:** As load current increased from 50 A to 80 A, speed dropped from 1000 rpm to ≈ 602 rpm — consistent with the steeply drooping series-motor characteristic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Drive-Equilibrium and Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
