# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1. Define "mechanical characteristic" of a drive. Name the four common types of load torques encountered in industrial drives.**

**Model Answer:**
The mechanical characteristic of a drive is the functional relationship between the *developed torque* and the *shaft speed* of either the motor or the load, expressed as $T = f(N)$ and plotted with $N$ on the X-axis and $T$ on the Y-axis.
The four common types of load torques are:
1. **Constant Torque Load** ($T_L = K_c$) — e.g., conveyors, hoists.
2. **Quadratic Torque Load** ($T_L = K_q N^2$) — e.g., fans, centrifugal pumps.
3. **Linear Torque Load** ($T_L = K_\ell N$) — e.g., viscous mixers.
4. **Constant Power Load** ($T_L = K_p / N$) — e.g., machine tool spindles, winders.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. State and explain the stability criterion for the operating point of an electric drive.**

**Model Answer:**
For a drive to operate stably at its equilibrium point $(N_0, T_0)$, a small increase in speed must cause the motor torque to drop *more rapidly* than the load torque rises. Mathematically, the stability criterion is:

$$\left. \frac{dT_m}{dN} \right|_{N_0} < \left. \frac{dT_L}{dN} \right|_{N_0}$$

The *motor characteristic must have a steeper negative slope* than the *positive slope* of the load characteristic at the intersection. This guarantees that any disturbance (e.g., a momentary speed rise) produces a *net decelerating torque*, returning the system to the operating point.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Internal Choice Q1]**
> **(a) Derive the speed–torque relationship of an unsaturated DC series motor. Explain why a series motor must never be started without mechanical load. (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) With neat characteristic curves, explain the four common types of load torques in industrial drives. Mention at least one example for each. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A (a)

**Step 1:** For an unsaturated DC series motor, $\phi \propto I_a$, i.e., $\phi = k_f I_a$.

**Step 2:** Back-emf: $E_b = V - I_a R_a = K \phi \omega = K k_f I_a \omega$.

**Step 3:** Torque: $T = K \phi I_a = K k_f I_a^2$, so $I_a = \sqrt{T / (K k_f)}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute in back-emf equation:

$$\omega = \frac{V}{K k_f I_a} - \frac{R_a}{K k_f} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f T}} - \frac{R_a}{K k_f}$$

**Step 5:** Converting $\omega$ to rpm:

$$N = \frac{60}{2\pi} \left( \frac{V}{\sqrt{K k_f T}} - \frac{R_a}{K k_f} \right) = \frac{A}{\sqrt{T}} - B$$

Hence $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$ — the **inverse-square speed–torque law** of an unsaturated series motor.

**Why no-load starting is dangerous:** From the derived equation, as $T \to 0$, $N \to \infty$. Without mechanical load, the series motor *races* to destructive speeds because there is no resisting torque to balance the developed torque. The windings may fling out, commutator may be damaged, and bearings may seize. Hence series motors are always mechanically coupled to the load before energization — typical in traction, cranes, and hoist applications.

#### Model Solution — Part A (b)

The four load-torque characteristics are best summarized with these equations and shapes:

| Type | Equation | Curve | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constant Torque | $T_L = K_c$ | Horizontal line | Conveyor belt |
| Quadratic Torque | $T_L = K_q N^2$ | Parabola | Centrifugal pump |
| Linear Torque | $T_L = K_\ell N$ | Straight line through origin | Viscous mixer |
| Constant Power | $T_L = K_p / N$ | Rectangular hyperbola | Coil winder |

A load may also be a *combination* of these — for example, a fan has a *friction component* (constant) plus a *quadratic windage* component, giving $T_L = T_f + K_q N^2$.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Internal Choice Q2]**
> **(a) For a three-phase induction motor, derive the condition for maximum (pull-out) torque and show that the slip at which it occurs is independent of supply voltage. (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) Compare the speed–torque characteristics of (i) DC shunt motor, (ii) DC series motor, (iii) squirrel-cage induction motor, and (iv) synchronous motor. Identify the most suitable motor for driving a centrifugal pump. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B (a)

The per-phase torque equation of a 3-$\phi$ induction motor is:

$$T = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_r / s)}{(R_r / s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$

Let $R = R_r / s$ and $X_{eq} = X_s + X_r$. Then:

$$T = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R}{R^2 + X_{eq}^2}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $R$ and setting $dT/dR = 0$:

$$\frac{dT}{dR} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X_{eq}^2 - R^2}{(R^2 + X_{eq}^2)^2} = 0$$

This gives $R = X_{eq}$, i.e.:

$$\frac{R_r}{s_{max}} = X_s + X_r \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{max} = \frac{R_r}{X_s + X_r}$$

The maximum torque is:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X_{eq}}{2 X_{eq}^2} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s (X_s + X_r)}$$

**Observation:** $s_{max}$ depends *only* on the rotor and stator leakage reactances and rotor resistance — **it is independent of supply voltage $V$**. In contrast, $T_{max} \propto V^2$, so voltage reduction lowers $T_{max}$ quadratically.

#### Model Solution — Part B (b)

| Motor | Shape of $T$–$N$ Curve | Speed Regulation | Starting Torque |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Shunt** | Almost horizontal straight line (slight droop) | Excellent (~ 5–10%) | Moderate (1.2–1.5 pu) |
| **DC Series** | Steep inverse-square curve $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$ | Poor (no-load runaway) | Very high (2–3 pu) |
| **Squirrel Cage Induction** | Parabolic with pull-out torque at $s_{max}$ | Good (~ 3–5%) | Moderate (1.5–2 pu) |
| **Synchronous** | Horizontal at $N_s$ (locked to frequency) | Perfect (zero) | Zero (needs auxiliary start) |

**Best choice for centrifugal pump:** A **squirrel-cage induction motor** is the most suitable. The pump load is *quadratic* ($T_L \propto N^2$), and the induction motor's natural torque–slip curve closely matches this shape in the operating region. Additionally, squirrel-cage induction motors are rugged, low-cost, and require minimal maintenance — ideal for continuous-duty fluid-pumping applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up motor and load torques** — remember $T_m$ (developed by motor) and $T_L$ (demanded by load) act in *opposite* directions at the shaft. Stable equilibrium is at $T_m = T_L$ with the correct sign convention.
> 2. **Confusing $T \propto s$ with the full torque equation** — the *linear approximation* $T \propto s$ is valid only for *small slip* (operating region), not for the starting or pull-out regions.
> 3. **Forgetting the $N_s = 120f/P$ formula** for synchronous-speed calculations — examiners test this directly.
> 4. **Ignoring stability criterion** — when asked to comment on a motor–load match, students often draw the curves and stop. The 2-mark "stability check" is the differentiator between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.
> 5. **Sign errors in inverse-square derivation** of the DC series motor — the algebra must be carried out *in terms of $T$* before eliminating $I_a$.
> 6. **Confusing $T_{max}$ with $T_{st}$** — $T_{max}$ is the *pull-out* torque at $s_{max}$, while $T_{st}$ is the *starting* torque at $s = 1$. Both occur at *different* slips.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [x] **Mechanical characteristic** = $T$–$N$ plot of motor *and* load.
- [x] **Four load types:** Constant $T$, Quadratic $T \propto N^2$, Linear $T \propto N$, Constant Power $T \propto 1/N$.
- [x] **Quadratic load** examples: fan, centrifugal pump, blower — $P_L \propto N^3$.
- [x] **Constant power load** examples: winder, machine tool spindle, traction.
- [x] **DC Shunt Motor:** nearly flat $T$–$N$ curve → constant-speed, low-starting-torque.
- [x] **DC Series Motor:** $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$ (unsaturated) → high starting torque, must never be run without load.
- [x] **DC Compound (Cumulative):** characteristic between shunt and series.
- [x] **Induction Motor:** $T \propto s$ (small $s$), $T_{max} \propto V^2$, $s_{max} = R_r / (X_s + X_r)$.
- [x] **Synchronous Motor:** exact constant speed $N_s = 120f/P$, no inherent starting torque.
- [x] **Stability Criterion:** $\frac{dT_m}{dN} < \frac{dT_L}{dN}$ at operating point.
- [x] **Equation of motion:** $T_m - T_L = J \frac{d\omega}{dt}$.
- [x] **Operating point** = intersection of motor and load characteristics; *must* satisfy stability.
- [x] **Power relation:** $P = T \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (with $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in W).
- [x] **Quadratic load + Squirrel-cage induction motor** = the *most common* industrial drive combination.
- [x] **Series motor + traction/hoist** = the *classic* high-starting-torque pairing.

> **End of Module 2 Topic — Mechanical Characteristics of Loads and Drive Motors**

topics.title: Braking of Electrical Motors - DC Motors: Shunt, Series, Compound Motors - Single Phase and Three Phase Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Braking of Electrical Motors - DC Motors: Shunt, Series, Compound Motors - Single Phase and Three Phase Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Electrical Braking?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Electrical Braking is a method of rapidly reducing the speed of an electric motor (or holding it stationary against a load torque) by utilizing the motor itself as a generator or by causing the electromagnetic torque to act in a direction opposite to the direction of rotation. The kinetic energy of the rotating mass is dissipated either as heat in a resistor (rheostatic/dynamic), returned to the supply (regenerative), or counteracted by a reverse electromagnetic torque (plugging).*

In simple language, electrical braking is using the motor's own electrical properties to **stop it faster than letting it coast to a halt naturally**. Just as a bicycle uses friction on the brake pads to dissipate kinetic energy as heat, an electrical drive uses **controlled resistance, regenerative feedback, or reversed terminals** to absorb the rotational energy.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a roller coaster train at the top of a hill. There are three ways to control its speed coming down:
1. **Climbing another hill** (Regenerative) – The train gains potential energy that can be used later.
2. **Applying friction brakes** (Dynamic/Rheostatic) – The energy is converted to heat in the brake pads.
3. **Driving the motor backwards** (Plugging) – A reverse force is applied to fight the motion.

Electrical braking uses the **motor's own electromagnetic action** to achieve these same effects, without mechanical brake wear.

### 1.3 The Three Universal Braking Modes (Applicable Across Motor Types)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Holy Trinity of Electrical Braking:**
>
> 1. **Regenerative Braking** — Motor acts as a generator; energy is fed back to the supply.
> 2. **Dynamic (Rheostatic) Braking** — Motor acts as a generator; energy is dumped into a resistor.
> 3. **Plugging (Counter-Current) Braking** — Supply polarity is reversed; motor develops a reverse torque.

### 1.4 Why Electrical Braking Over Mechanical Braking?

| Parameter | Mechanical Brake | Electrical Brake |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Wear & Tear** | High (friction pads) | Negligible |
| **Response Time** | Slow | Fast (ms) |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Localized (at brake) | Distributed (in resistor) |
| **Precision Control** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent | Minimal |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | Moderate-High |

**Standard Metric of Interest:** Braking torque in **N·m**, braking time in **seconds (s)**, and energy dissipated in **Joules (J)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Energy Equation During Braking

The kinetic energy stored in a rotating system is:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} J \omega_m^2
$$

Where:
- $J$ = Moment of inertia of the rotating parts (kg·m²)
- $\omega_m$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)

This entire energy must be **dissipated or recovered** during the braking cycle. The total braking time is governed by:

$$
t_b = \frac{J \cdot \omega_m}{(T_b + T_L)}
$$

Where:
- $T_b$ = Braking torque developed by the motor (N·m)
- $T_L$ = Load torque (N·m), assists braking

---

### 2.2 Braking of DC Shunt Motor

#### 2.2.1 Regenerative Braking of DC Shunt Motor

**Principle:** When the back-EMF ($E_b$) exceeds the supply voltage ($V$), the armature current reverses, and the motor acts as a generator feeding power back to the supply.

**Condition:** $E_b > V \implies V - I_a R_a < 0 \implies I_a$ is negative.

**Methods to achieve this:**
- **Field Weakening:** Reduce flux $\phi$, so $E_b = k\phi\omega$ drops momentarily, but if load drives the motor, $\omega$ rises such that $E_b > V$.
- **Over-Voltage Supply:** Supply voltage is suddenly reduced below $E_b$ (used in elevators during descent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The torque equation $T = k\phi I_a$ remains valid, but $I_a$ is negative, making $T$ negative (opposing motion).

#### 2.2.2 Dynamic Braking of DC Shunt Motor

**Circuit Operation:**
1. Armature is disconnected from the supply.
2. Armature is connected across a braking resistor $R_b$.
3. Field winding remains energized (separately excited).
4. The rotating armature now acts as a separately excited generator, with $E_b$ as the source.

$$
I_a = \frac{-E_b}{R_a + R_b} = \frac{-k\phi\omega}{R_a + R_b}
$$

The braking torque is:
$$
T_b = k\phi I_a = \frac{-(k\phi)^2 \omega}{R_a + R_b}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign indicates torque opposes rotation. Braking torque is **proportional to speed** — high at high speeds, low at low speeds.

#### 2.2.3 Plugging of DC Shunt Motor

**Operation:** Armature terminals are reversed while the motor is running. The supply voltage and back-EMF now add up:

$$
I_a = \frac{-V - E_b}{R_a} \quad \text{(initially large, current limiting resistor } R_{add} \text{ is essential)}
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{-V - k\phi\omega}{R_a + R_{add}}
$$

**Disadvantage:** Very high current, large energy dissipation, and the supply switch must be opened at zero speed (else motor accelerates in reverse).

---

### 2.3 Braking of DC Series Motor

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Challenge:** A series motor's flux $\phi$ depends on $I_a$. During dynamic braking, if the field remains in series with the armature, the rapidly falling $I_a$ collapses the field, destroying braking torque. This requires special circuit arrangements.

#### 2.3.1 Dynamic Braking of DC Series Motor — Two Configurations

**Configuration A: Series Field with Armature (Self-Excitation)**

The motor is disconnected and connected to $R_b$. The flux direction must be maintained by ensuring current direction does not reverse through the field winding.

**Configuration B: Field Separately Excited (Most Common)**

Field winding is excited from a separate DC source, and the armature feeds $R_b$. This is the **preferred method** because flux is constant.

For self-excited dynamic braking, the characteristic becomes unstable if the field resistance line is not crossed.

#### 2.3.2 Plugging of DC Series Motor

Reversing the armature terminals (NOT the field). Since $T \propto \phi I_a$ and both $\phi$ and $I_a$ reverse (relative), the torque reverses correctly. A current-limiting resistor is mandatory.

#### 2.3.3 Regenerative Braking of DC Series Motor

Rarely feasible in pure series mode because $E_b$ can never exceed $V$ easily. Requires a parallel-series conversion (special configuration used in traction: series for motoring, parallel for regenerative braking on downhill).

---

### 2.4 Braking of DC Compound Motor

> [!NOTE]
> A compound motor offers **flexibility** because both shunt and series fields exist.

- **Cumulative Compound:** Series field aids shunt field. During dynamic braking, the series field opposes the shunt field if armature current reverses — must use **polarity-reversing switch on the series field** to maintain aiding action.
- **Differential Compound:** Series field opposes shunt field. Easier dynamic braking since fields aid during reverse current.

Braking methods: Same three types (regenerative, dynamic, plugging) with field-control switches added for proper operation.

---

### 2.5 Braking of Three-Phase Induction Motor

#### 2.5.1 Regenerative Braking

**Principle:** When rotor speed $N$ exceeds synchronous speed $N_s$, slip $s < 0$. The motor acts as an induction generator.

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} < 0
$$

The rotor resistance seen from stator is $R_2/s$, which is now **negative** — equivalent to a generator feeding power back.

**Methods:**
- **Sub-synchronous to super-synchronous transition** (used in V/f controlled drives, e.g., hoist descending with load).
- **Variable frequency reduction** (during deceleration, reduce $f$, keeping $V/f$ constant; rotor speed remains above new $N_s$ momentarily).

#### 2.5.2 Dynamic (DC Injection) Braking of Induction Motor

**Circuit Operation:**
1. Stator AC supply is disconnected.
2. DC current is injected into any two stator terminals (single-phase excitation producing a stationary magnetic field).
3. The rotating rotor cuts this stationary field, inducing currents and developing braking torque.

**Approximate braking torque equation:**

$$
T_b \propto \phi_{DC}^2 \cdot \omega_r
$$

Where $\phi_{DC}$ depends on the DC excitation current.

#### 2.5.3 Plugging of Three-Phase Induction Motor

**Operation:** Two stator leads are interchanged, reversing the rotating field direction.

The new slip becomes:

$$
s_{new} = \frac{-N_s - N}{-N_s} = \frac{N_s + N}{N_s} = 2 - s
$$

For $s \approx 0.03$ (near rated speed), the new slip is $\approx 1.97$ — the motor draws very high current and produces a strong reverse torque.

**Disadvantage:** High current, must cut supply at zero speed.

---

### 2.6 Braking of Single-Phase Induction Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Single-phase induction motors have **no inherent reverse torque** when the supply is reversed (they continue rotating in the same direction due to the single-phase field). Hence, **plugging is not effective** without auxiliary windings.

**Common Braking Methods:**

1. **DC Injection Braking (Most Widely Used):** Inject DC through the main winding after disconnecting AC. Creates a stationary field that the rotor cuts. Used in washing machines, fans.

2. **Capacitor Discharge Braking:** Used in capacitor-start motors. The capacitor discharges through the start winding in a direction opposing rotation, creating a reverse torque.

3. **Reverse Rotation Braking (with centrifugal switch):** A reverse connection is briefly applied. The motor develops a transient reverse torque. Used in applications like record players.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_b = k\phi\omega$ | Back-EMF equation (DC motors) | V |
| 2 | $T = k\phi I_a$ | Motor torque (DC motors) | N·m |
| 3 | $I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | Armature current during motoring | A |
| 4 | $I_a = \frac{-E_b}{R_a + R_b}$ | Dynamic braking current (DC) | A |
| 5 | $I_a = \frac{-V - E_b}{R_a + R_{add}}$ | Plugging current (DC) | A |
| 6 | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of induction motor | RPM |
| 7 | $s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $s_{new} = 2 - s$ | Effective slip during plugging (IM) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_b \propto \frac{-s R_2}{(R_2)^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Braking torque (IM, motoring convention) | N·m |
| 10 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} J\omega^2$ | Kinetic energy of rotating mass | J |
| 11 | $t_b = \frac{J \omega}{T_b + T_L}$ | Approximate braking time | s |
| 12 | $E_{dissipated} = \int T_b \cdot \omega \, dt$ | Energy dissipated in braking resistor | J |

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications:**
> - **Hoists & Cranes:** Regenerative braking to lower heavy loads safely.
> - **Electric Trains/Trams:** Regenerative braking feeds power back to the grid (modern 3-phase systems).
> - **Elevators:** Plugging or DC injection for precise stopping at floors.
> - **Washing Machines:** DC injection braking for the spin cycle.
> - **Paper Mills:** Dynamic braking to stop rollers quickly for tear prevention.
> - **Printing Presses:** Precise DC braking for sheet alignment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Braking Time for a DC Shunt Motor Under Dynamic Braking

**Problem Statement:** A DC shunt motor running at rated speed is suddenly switched to dynamic braking mode. Derive the expression for the time taken for the motor to come to rest, assuming constant flux and load torque.

#### Step 1: Define the Governing Dynamic Equation

When the armature is switched to a resistor $R_b$, the motor acts as a generator. The induced EMF drives a current that creates a braking torque. The mechanical equation of motion is:

$$
J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -T_b - T_L
$$

The negative signs indicate that both braking torque and load torque oppose the existing rotation (assuming load is frictional-type, aiding braking).

#### Step 2: Express Braking Torque in Terms of Speed

The back-EMF is:
$$
E_b = k\phi\omega
$$

The armature current during dynamic braking is:
$$
I_a = -\frac{E_b}{R_a + R_b} = -\frac{k\phi\omega}{R_a + R_b}
$$

The braking torque is:
$$
T_b = k\phi I_a = -\frac{(k\phi)^2\omega}{R_a + R_b}
$$

#### Step 3: Substitute into the Equation of Motion

$$
J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -\left(\frac{(k\phi)^2}{R_a + R_b}\right)\omega - T_L
$$

Let $K_b = \frac{(k\phi)^2}{R_a + R_b}$ (a constant called the braking coefficient).

Then:
$$
J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -K_b \omega - T_L
$$

#### Step 4: Rearrange and Integrate

$$
\frac{d\omega}{K_b \omega + T_L} = -\frac{dt}{J}
$$

Integrating from $\omega = \omega_0$ (initial speed) to $\omega = \omega$ (speed at time $t$):

$$
\int_{\omega_0}^{\omega} \frac{d\omega}{K_b \omega + T_L} = -\frac{1}{J}\int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

The left integral evaluates to:
$$
\frac{1}{K_b} \left[ \ln(K_b \omega + T_L) \right]_{\omega_0}^{\omega} = -\frac{t}{J}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{K_b} \ln\left(\frac{K_b \omega + T_L}{K_b \omega_0 + T_L}\right) = -\frac{t}{J}
$$

#### Step 5: Solve for Time

$$
\ln\left(\frac{K_b \omega + T_L}{K_b \omega_0 + T_L}\right) = -\frac{K_b t}{J}
$$

$$
\frac{K_b \omega + T_L}{K_b \omega_0 + T_L} = e^{-K_b t / J}
$$

$$
K_b \omega + T_L = (K_b \omega_0 + T_L) e^{-K_b t / J}
$$

$$
\omega(t) = \frac{(K_b \omega_0 + T_L) e^{-K_b t / J} - T_L}{K_b}
$$

#### Step 6: Final Speed as $t \to \infty$

The exponential term vanishes, leaving:
$$
\omega_{\infty} = -\frac{T_L}{K_b}
$$

This represents a small residual speed at which $T_b = T_L$ (braking torque balances load torque). For an overhauling load, this is **dangerous** — the motor continues to rotate. For a frictional load that disappears at zero speed, the motor decelerates fully.

**Time to Reach a Specific Speed $\omega_1$:**

$$
t_1 = \frac{J}{K_b} \ln\left(\frac{K_b \omega_0 + T_L}{K_b \omega_1 + T_L}\right)
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Braking Methods

| Parameter | Regenerative | Dynamic (Rheostatic) | Plugging |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Destination** | Returned to supply | Dissipated as heat in $R_b$ | Dissipated in $R_a$ + supply |
| **Braking Torque** | Limited (only when $E_b > V$) | Variable, controllable via $R_b$ | Very high, controllable via $R_{add}$ |
| **Speed Range of Operation** | Above base speed | Full range | Full range |
| **Stopping Precision** | Poor (motor keeps running at low speed) | Good | Excellent (cuts at zero) |
| **Heating of Motor** | Minimal | Low (armature only) | High (armature + supply) |
| **Switching Complexity** | Low-Medium | Low | High (zero-speed sensing required) |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Best Application** | High inertia, frequent stops | General purpose | Emergency / precision stop |
| **Power Supply Stress** | Receives power | None | Severe (back-fed energy) |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Plugging Time of DC Shunt Motor

**Given Data:**
- Motor: 220 V DC shunt motor
- $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- Rated speed: $N_0 = 1500$ RPM
- $k\phi = 1.5$ V·s/rad (machine constant)
- $J = 5$ kg·m² (total inertia at motor shaft)
- Load torque $T_L = 0$ (frictionless for simplicity)

**Step 1: Find Initial Angular Velocity**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi N_0}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2: Find the Back-EMF at Rated Speed**

$$
E_{b0} = k\phi \cdot \omega_0 = 1.5 \times 157.08 = 235.62 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: Plugging Braking Torque Equation**

During plugging, armature current is:
$$
I_a = \frac{-V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{-220 - k\phi\omega}{0.5}
$$

Braking torque:
$$
T_b = k\phi I_a = -1.5 \times \frac{220 + 1.5\omega}{0.5} = -3(220 + 1.5\omega)
$$

$$
T_b = -660 - 4.5\omega \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Equation of Motion**

$$
5 \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -T_b = 660 + 4.5\omega
$$

$$
\frac{d\omega}{660 + 4.5\omega} = \frac{dt}{5}
$$

**Step 5: Integrate from $\omega_0$ to 0**

$$
\int_{157.08}^{0} \frac{d\omega}{660 + 4.5\omega} = \frac{1}{5}\int_{0}^{t_{stop}} dt
$$

$$
\left[\frac{1}{4.5}\ln(660 + 4.5\omega)\right]_{157.08}^{0} = \frac{t_{stop}}{5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{4.5}\left[\ln(660) - \ln(660 + 4.5 \times 157.08)\right] = \frac{t_{stop}}{5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{4.5}\left[\ln(660) - \ln(1366.86)\right] = \frac{t_{stop}}{5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{4.5}\ln\left(\frac{660}{1366.86}\right) = \frac{t_{stop}}{5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{4.5}\ln(0.4829) = \frac{t_{stop}}{5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{4.5} \times (-0.7278) = \frac{t_{stop}}{5}
$$

$$
-0.1617 = \frac{t_{stop}}{5}
$$

$$
t_{stop} \approx 0.81 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common mistake is forgetting that during plugging, **both $V$ and $E_b$ add up**. Students often write $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ — this is **wrong for plugging**. Always use $I_a = -(V + E_b)/R_a$ in plugging mode.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Regenerative Braking of 3-Phase IM

**Given Data:**
- 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor
- Rated speed: 1455 RPM
- Rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$ (referred to stator)
- The motor is overhauled by the load to 1600 RPM

**Step 1: Calculate Synchronous Speed**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Slip at 1600 RPM**

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1600}{1500} = -\frac{100}{1500} = -0.0667
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

Negative slip means the motor is acting as an **induction generator**. The rotor resistance referred to the stator is:

$$
\frac{R_2}{s} = \frac{0.2}{-0.0667} = -3.0 \, \Omega
$$

The negative effective resistance means power flows **from rotor to stator to supply** — true regenerative action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow: Selection of Braking Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Motor Running at Speed N] --> decA{Need to Stop?}
    decA -- No --> runA[Continue Motoring]
    decA -- Yes --> decB{Energy Recovery Required?}
    decB -- Yes, Above Ns --> regA[Regenerative Braking]
    decB -- No, Any Speed --> decC{Quick Stop Required?}
    regA --> fieldCtrl1[Field Weakening OR Reduce V/f]
    fieldCtrl1 --> supply1[Power Returns to Supply]
    decC -- Yes, Emergency --> plugA[Plugging Braking]
    decC -- Standard Stop --> dynA[Dynamic Braking]
    plugA --> reverseA[Reverse Armature Polarity]
    reverseA --> cutZero[Cut Supply at Zero Speed]
    dynA --> disconnA[Disconnect Armature from Supply]
    disconnA --> resA[Connect Armature to Rb]
    resA --> dissipateA[Energy Dissipated as Heat]
    supply1 --> stop1[Motor Decelerates to Ns or Lower]
    cutZero --> stop2[Motor Reaches Zero Speed]
    dissipateA --> stop3[Motor Decelerates to Zero]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Dynamic Braking of DC Series Motor (Self-Excited)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    supplyA[DC Supply] --> switchA[Selector Switch SW1]
    switchA -- Position 1: Motoring --> seriesA[Series Field SF + Armature A]
    seriesA --> motor1A[DC Series Motor M]
    motor1A --> runA[Rotation]
    switchA -- Position 2: Dynamic Braking --> resistorA[Braking Resistor Rb]
    resistorA --> seriesB[Series Field SF Reversed Polarity]
    seriesB --> motor1B[Same Motor M acting as Generator]
    motor1B --> res2A[Energy Dissipated in Rb]
    seriesB -.Stability Check.-> lineA[Field Resistance Line]
    lineA --> critA{Critical: Ensure Self-Excitation Stable}
    critA -- Yes --> res2A
    critA -- No --> warningA[Use Separately Excited Braking]
    warningA --> sepFieldA[Field from Separate DC Source]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: DC Injection Braking of 3-Phase IM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    runState[3-Phase IM Running] --> stopCmd[Stop Command Issued]
    stopCmd --> contactor1[Contactor K1 Opens]
    contactor1 --> acDisc[AC Supply Disconnected from Stator]
    acDisc --> contactor2[Contactor K2 Closes]
    contactor2 --> dcInject[DC Source Connected to Two Stator Terminals]
    dcInject --> statField[Stationary Magnetic Field Established in Stator]
    statField --> rotorCut[Rotor Conductors Cut Stationary Field]
    rotorCut --> rotCurrent[Induced Rotor Current Flows]
    rotCurrent --> brkTorque[Braking Torque Developed T proportional to omega]
    brkTorque --> decel[Rotor Decelerates]
    decel --> zeroDetect{Zero Speed Reached?}
    zeroDetect -- Yes --> k2Open[K2 Opens, DC Removed]
    zeroDetect -- No --> decel
    k2Open --> mechBrake[Mechanical Brake Applied for Final Hold]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Tree: Braking Methods Across Motor Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[Electrical Braking Methods]
    root1 --> regNode[Regenerative Braking]
    root1 --> dynNode[Dynamic Rheostatic Braking]
    root1 --> plugNode[Plugging Counter Current Braking]
    regNode --> dcShunt[DC Shunt: Field Weakening or V Reduction]
    regNode --> im3ph[3-Phase IM: Super Synchronous Operation]
    regNode --> dcSeries[DC Series: Parallel Mode Only Rare]
    dynNode --> dcShuntDyn[DC Shunt: Armature to Rb]
    dynNode --> dcSeriesDyn[DC Series: Self or Separate Excitation]
    dynNode --> im3phDyn[3-Phase IM: DC Injection Stator]
    dynNode --> im1phDyn[1-Phase IM: DC Injection Main Winding]
    plugNode --> dcShuntPlug[DC Shunt: Reverse Armature]
    plugNode --> dcSeriesPlug[DC Series: Reverse Armature Only]
    plugNode --> im3phPlug[3-Phase IM: Reverse Two Stator Leads]
    plugNode --> im1phPlug[1-Phase IM: Reverse with Centrifugal Switch]
```

### 4.5 Stability Analysis Chart (Self-Excited Dynamic Braking of DC Series Motor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    speedAxis[Speed omega on X axis] --- voltAxis[Voltage V on Y axis]
    voltAxis --> ocCurve[Open Circuit Characteristic: V equals k phi omega]
    speedAxis --> frLine[Field Resistance Line: V equals I Rf]
    ocCurve -.Intersect.-> critPointA[Critical Point for Stability]
    frLine -.Intersect.-> critPointA
    critPointA --> zoneA[Stable Region: Above OC curve]
    critPointA --> zoneB[Unstable Region: Below OC curve]
    zoneA --> okOp[Operation Permits Stable Braking]
    zoneB --> notOp[Operation Not Permitted Brake Failure]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain regenerative braking of a DC shunt motor. Under what conditions does it occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Regenerative braking in a DC shunt motor occurs when the **back-EMF ($E_b$) exceeds the supply voltage ($V$)**, making the armature current negative. Under this condition, the motor acts as a generator and feeds power back to the supply.

$$
E_b = k\phi\omega > V
$$

This is achieved by:
- **Field Weakening:** Reducing flux $\phi$ momentarily so that the load overhauls the motor.
- **Overhauling Loads:** The load drives the motor above its no-load speed (e.g., electric locomotive descending a gradient).

The torque reverses, opposing rotation, and provides a braking effect. The kinetic energy of the moving parts is **converted to electrical energy and returned to the supply**, making it highly efficient. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why is dynamic braking of a DC series motor more complex than that of a DC shunt motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a DC series motor, the field winding carries the same current as the armature. During dynamic braking, when the motor is switched to a resistor, the armature current (and hence field current) starts to fall rapidly as speed decreases. Since flux $\phi$ is proportional to $I_a$ in a series motor, the **field collapses** before significant braking can occur, and braking torque $T_b = k\phi I_a$ vanishes.

To overcome this, either:
1. The motor is **separately excited** from an external DC source, OR
2. The **self-excitation is carefully designed** so that the field resistance line intersects the open-circuit characteristic in the stable region.

This makes the circuit more complex than the simple case of a DC shunt motor, where the shunt field remains energized naturally from the supply. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **dynamic braking of a three-phase induction motor using DC injection**. Derive the expression for the braking torque developed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC series motor has armature resistance $0.3 \, \Omega$ and series field resistance $0.2 \, \Omega$. At a certain load, the motor takes 40 A and runs at 800 RPM. The motor is to be braked by plugging. Calculate:
(i) The initial braking current if no additional resistance is added.
(ii) The value of additional resistance to limit the current to 60 A. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution:**

**(a) DC Injection Braking of 3-Phase Induction Motor — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Circuit Operation:**

When a stop command is issued, the AC contactor disconnects the 3-phase AC supply from the stator terminals. Simultaneously, a DC contactor connects a **DC source** across **any two of the three stator terminals** (typically U and V). This single-phase DC excitation produces a **stationary (non-rotating) magnetic field** in the air gap.

The rotating rotor, driven by its kinetic energy, cuts this stationary field. By Faraday's law, an EMF is induced in the rotor conductors. Since the rotor cage is short-circuited, currents flow in the rotor bars. These currents interact with the stationary field to produce a **braking torque** that opposes the rotation.

**Expression for Braking Torque:**

The equivalent rotor resistance as seen from the stator at slip $s = 1$ (rotor is initially rotating while the field is stationary) is:

$$
\frac{R_2}{s} = R_2 \text{ at } s = 1
$$

The DC flux $\phi_{DC}$ is proportional to the DC excitation current. The induced rotor frequency is $f_r = s \cdot f$ where $s \to 1$ initially.

The braking torque is given by:
$$
T_b = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
$$

For DC injection, with constant $\phi_{DC}$ and $s \approx 1$:

$$
T_b \propto \phi_{DC}^2 \cdot \omega_r
$$

The torque is **proportional to rotor speed** — high at the start of braking, decreasing as the rotor slows down. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Plugging Calculation — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $I_a = 40$ A, $N = 800$ RPM.

**Step 1: Calculate the Back-EMF at the given operating point**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 40(0.3 + 0.2) = 220 - 20 = 200 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Part (i) — Initial Braking Current Without Additional Resistance**

During plugging, the supply voltage polarity is reversed, and $V$ now acts in the **same direction** as $E_{b1}$:

$$
I_{brake} = \frac{-(V + E_{b1})}{R_a + R_{se}} = \frac{-(220 + 200)}{0.3 + 0.2} = \frac{-420}{0.5} = -840 \text{ A}
$$

The magnitude is **840 A**, which is catastrophically high. This is why additional resistance is mandatory. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Part (ii) — Additional Resistance to Limit Current to 60 A**

$$
60 = \frac{-(220 + 200)}{0.5 + R_{add}}
$$

Taking magnitudes:
$$
60 = \frac{420}{0.5 + R_{add}}
$$

$$
0.5 + R_{add} = \frac{420}{60} = 7.0
$$

$$
R_{add} = 7.0 - 0.5 = 6.5 \, \Omega
$$

**The additional resistance required is $6.5 \, \Omega$.** **(5 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the three methods of **electrical braking of a DC shunt motor** with circuit diagrams and the relevant equations. Compare their braking torques versus speed characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. It is driving a fan load. If the load overhauls the motor to a speed of 1560 RPM, determine:
(i) The slip at this speed.
(ii) Whether the motor is acting as a motor or generator.
(iii) The effective rotor resistance referred to the stator if actual rotor resistance is $0.5 \, \Omega$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution:**

**(a) Three Methods of Braking a DC Shunt Motor — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Regenerative Braking:**

In regenerative braking, the motor acts as a generator and feeds power back to the supply. This occurs when $E_b > V$.

**Condition:**
$$
E_b = k\phi\omega > V
$$

The armature current becomes:
$$
I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} < 0
$$

This is achieved by reducing the field flux (field weakening) or by an overhauling load. The braking torque is:
$$
T_b = k\phi I_a \text{ (negative)}
$$

**2. Dynamic (Rheostatic) Braking:**

The armature is disconnected from the supply and connected across a braking resistor $R_b$. The shunt field remains energized.

**Current equation:**
$$
I_a = \frac{-E_b}{R_a + R_b} = \frac{-k\phi\omega}{R_a + R_b}
$$

**Braking torque:**
$$
T_b = -\frac{(k\phi)^2\omega}{R_a + R_b}
$$

Braking torque is **proportional to speed** — high at high speed, vanishes as motor stops.

**3. Plugging (Counter-Current) Braking:**

The armature terminals are reversed (with a current-limiting resistor). The supply voltage and back-EMF now add up:

$$
I_a = \frac{-V - E_b}{R_a + R_{add}}
$$

$$
T_b = -\frac{k\phi(V + k\phi\omega)}{R_a + R_{add}}
$$

**Comparison of Torque-Speed Characteristics:**

| Method | Torque-Speed Relationship | Maximum Torque |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Regenerative | $T_b \propto$ small, near rated | Moderate |
| Dynamic | $T_b \propto \omega$ (linear) | High at start |
| Plugging | $T_b$ very high across range | Very High |

**(7 marks — distribute marks for circuit, equations, and comparison)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Regenerative Braking of 3-Phase IM (7 marks):**

**Given:** 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, full-load slip $s_{fl} = 0.04$, $N_{over} = 1560$ RPM, $R_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$.

**Step 1: Calculate Synchronous Speed**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 2: Part (i) — Slip at 1560 RPM**

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1560}{1500} = \frac{-60}{1500} = -0.04 \text{ or } -4\%
$$

**Step 3: Part (ii) — Mode of Operation**

Since slip is **negative** ($s = -0.04$), the rotor is rotating **faster than the synchronous speed**. Therefore, the machine is operating as an **induction generator** (regenerative braking mode). The electromagnetic torque is **negative**, opposing the rotation, and power flows from rotor to stator to the supply. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Part (iii) — Effective Rotor Resistance**

The effective resistance referred to the stator is:

$$
R_{2,eff} = \frac{R_2}{s} = \frac{0.5}{-0.04} = -12.5 \, \Omega
$$

The **negative sign** is mathematically significant — it represents the fact that the rotor is delivering power (acting as a source) rather than absorbing it. **(3 marks)**

**Final Answers:**
- Slip: $s = -0.04$ (or $-4\%$)
- Mode: **Induction generator / regenerative braking**
- Effective rotor resistance: $-12.5 \, \Omega$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** During regenerative braking of DC motors, students forget that $I_a$ and $T$ are **both negative**. Failing to indicate this loses 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Plugging Equation Confusion:** Writing $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ instead of $I_a = -(V + E_b)/R_a$ is a **very common error**. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Series Motor Dynamic Braking:** Forgetting to discuss the **stability of self-excitation** in DC series motor dynamic braking leads to incomplete answers.
> 4. **Induction Motor Slip Sign:** In regenerative braking of an induction motor, students often write $s = +4\%$ when it should be $s = -4\%$. The negative sign is essential.
> 5. **DC Injection Braking Detail:** Students forget to mention the **zero-speed sensing** required to disconnect the DC supply; otherwise, the rotor gets heated.
> 6. **Units & Numerical Verification:** Always include units in final answers. Mark deduction is common when only numerical values are given.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Braking of Electrical Motors:**

**Universal Concepts:**
- ☐ Three braking modes: **Regenerative, Dynamic, Plugging** (applies to all motor types with modifications).
- ☐ During braking, kinetic energy must go somewhere: **back to supply** (regen), **into a resistor** (dynamic), or **into the motor + supply** (plugging).
- ☐ Braking torque is always **opposite to direction of rotation**.

**DC Shunt Motor:**
- ☐ **Regenerative** = field weakening or supply voltage reduction.
- ☐ **Dynamic** = armature to $R_b$, field energized.
- ☐ **Plugging** = reverse armature, add $R_{add}$, cut at zero speed.

**DC Series Motor:**
- ☐ **Dynamic braking is complex** due to self-excitation stability issues.
- ☐ **Plugging** is most common — reverse armature (NOT field).
- ☐ **Regenerative** requires parallel mode conversion.

**DC Compound Motor:**
- ☐ Watch the **polarity of the series field** during dynamic braking.
- ☐ Differential compound is more forgiving for dynamic braking.

**Three-Phase Induction Motor:**
- ☐ **Regenerative** = super-synchronous operation ($s < 0$).
- ☐ **Dynamic (DC Injection)** = disconnect AC, inject DC, stationary field.
- ☐ **Plugging** = reverse two stator leads, slip becomes $(2 - s)$.
- ☐ Energy dissipated in DC injection: $E = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2$ (no return to supply).

**Single-Phase Induction Motor:**
- ☐ **DC injection** is the most widely used braking method.
- ☐ **Plugging is ineffective** without auxiliary windings.
- ☐ Used in washing machines, fans, and small appliances.

**Key Equations to Memorize:**
- ☐ $E_b = k\phi\omega$
- ☐ $T_b = k\phi I_a$
- ☐ $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$
- ☐ $s_{new} = 2 - s$ (plugging of IM)
- ☐ $E_k = \frac{1}{2} J\omega^2$
- ☐ $t_b = J\omega / (T_b + T_L)$

**Industrial Applications to Remember:**
- ☐ Hoists/Cranes → Regenerative
- ☐ Electric Trains → Regenerative
- ☐ Elevators → Plugging / DC injection
- ☐ Washing Machines → DC injection
- ☐ Paper Mills → Dynamic braking
- ☐ CNC Machines → All three methods used in different sequences

**Examiner's Golden Rule:** Always state the **direction of energy flow** and the **braking torque equation** with proper signs in your answer. Marks are awarded for clear, complete reasoning, not just the final number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of D.C. Motor Starters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of D.C. Motor Starters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **D.C. Motor Starter** is an electro-mechanical protective switching device inserted in series between the supply and the armature of a D.C. motor. Its primary functions are to:

1. **Limit the inrush starting current** to a safe value (typically 1.5 to 2 times the full-load current) by inserting a calibrated external resistance that is progressively cut out as the motor accelerates.
2. **Provide back-up protection** to the motor against sustained overloading, no-voltage (supply failure), and over-current conditions through electromagnetic release mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The KTU Module 3 specifically requires the student to understand the **construction, working, and protection features** of the 2-point, 3-point, and 4-point starters. Differential understanding of the *no-voltage coil* and *holding coil* connections is a high-frequency exam topic.

The starting current of a D.C. motor without a starter is given by:

$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

This value is dangerously high (often **8 to 10 times** the full-load current) because at standstill, the back EMF $E_b = 0$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Manual Clutch in a Car"

Imagine a heavy loaded truck parked on a steep slope. If you suddenly engage first gear and release the clutch instantly, the engine will stall due to the shock load. Instead, you **gradually release the clutch** while increasing the engine RPM. The clutch pedal acts as a *variable resistance coupler* that smoothly transfers torque to the wheels.

A D.C. motor starter performs the **exact same function electrically**:

| Car Clutch (Mechanical Analogy) | DC Motor Starter (Electrical) |
|---|---|
| Initial high resistance to motion | Maximum external resistance $R_{ext}$ in circuit |
| Gradual release = less resistance | Step-by-step cutting out of $R_{ext}$ |
| Engine picks up speed → load shared | Motor accelerates → back EMF $E_b$ builds up |
| Clutch fully engaged at cruising | Starter handle reaches the RUN position, $R_{ext} = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metric to Remember:** The standard safe starting current is **1.5 $I_{FL}$** to **2.0 $I_{FL}$** (Full Load Current). The starter design is always based on this KTU-recommended limit.

## 1.3 Why Starters Are Absolutely Necessary

At the moment of switching ON a D.C. motor:
- Armature speed $N = 0$ ⇒ Back EMF $E_b = k\phi N = 0$
- Therefore, the armature sees the full supply voltage $V$ across only the tiny armature resistance $R_a$ (which is in milliohms for fractional kW motors).
- The commutator segments, brushes, and winding insulation cannot tolerate such a surge.

A starter, therefore, is **not optional** — it is a mandatory protective interlock mandated by Indian Standard **IS 13947-4-1** for industrial D.C. motor installations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Voltage Equation of a D.C. Motor

The fundamental relationship that drives the entire starter design is the **armature voltage equation**:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Rearranged to express the armature current:

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a}$$

The back EMF itself is a function of speed and flux:

$$E_b = k \phi N$$

where $k$ is the machine construction constant, $\phi$ is the flux per pole, and $N$ is the speed in RPM.

## 2.2 Logic Flow — Step-by-Step Working of a Starter

1. **Initial State (t = 0):** Motor is at rest, $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0$. Full resistance $R_{ext}$ is connected in series with $R_a$.
2. **Inrush Limitation:** The starter limits current to a safe value:
$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}}$$
3. **Acceleration Phase:** As the motor picks up speed, $E_b$ rises linearly with $N$. This causes $I_a$ to drop automatically.
4. **Resistance Cut-Out:** When $I_a$ falls to the lower safe threshold, the operator (or an automatic relay) cuts out one stud of resistance. The current briefly jumps up but is re-limited.
5. **Steady State:** All resistance cut out, $R_{ext} = 0$, and the motor runs on its natural characteristic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Successive Cut-Outs:** The resistance is **not linear** but **non-linear (resistive ladder)**. The first stud removes the largest resistance value, and successive studs remove progressively smaller values. This gives roughly constant acceleration torque.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | Armature current equation at any speed | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | $E_b = k\phi N$ | Back EMF is proportional to flux × speed | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $I_{st} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | Starting current *without* starter (DANGEROUS) | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $I_{st,lim} = \dfrac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}}$ | Limited starting current *with* starter | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $R_{ext} = \dfrac{V}{1.5\,I_{FL}} - R_a$ | External resistance required for 1.5 $I_{FL}$ start | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | $T \propto \phi I_a$ | Torque proportional to flux × armature current | N·m |
| 7 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\phi}$ | Speed-load characteristic of a D.C. motor | RPM |
| 8 | $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{k\phi}$ | No-load (ideal) speed at $I_a = 0$ | RPM |
| 9 | $E_b \approx V$ at rated load | At full load, armature drop is small (~5%) | Volts (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulae are used by **industrial drive design engineers** to size the starter studs, calculate heat dissipation in the resistance box, and design soft-start circuits for high-inertia loads such as rolling mills, elevators, and printing presses.

## 2.4 Classification of D.C. Motor Starters

D.C. motor starters are broadly classified into **three types** based on the number of connection terminals between the starter and the motor:

| Type | Connection Points | Auxiliary Coil | Field Current Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2-Point Starter** | A and F (2 terminals) | Overload coil in series with armature | Series-wound only |
| **3-Point Starter** | A, F, and L (3 terminals) | No-volt coil in parallel with field | Shunt-wound (with series interpole) |
| **4-Point Starter** | A, A1, F, and L (4 terminals) | No-volt coil in independent parallel circuit | Shunt / Compound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "L" Terminal:** In a 3-point and 4-point starter, the terminal **L** is connected to the **positive supply line**. The starter handle is spring-loaded and held in the ON position by the magnetic pull of the no-volt coil. If supply fails, the spring returns the handle to OFF — a vital protection feature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Required External Starter Resistance

**Given Data (Typical KTU Numerical):**
- Supply Voltage: $V = 220\text{ V}$
- Full-Load Current: $I_{FL} = 40\text{ A}$
- Armature Resistance: $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$
- Motor Type: D.C. Shunt

**Objective:** Calculate the total external resistance required so that the starting current does not exceed **1.5 $I_{FL}$**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Safe Starting Current Limit

$$I_{st,lim} = 1.5 \times I_{FL} = 1.5 \times 40 = 60\ \text{A}$$

We take 1.5 as the conventional factor (KTU 2024 convention).

### Step 2 — Write the Starting Current Equation

At the instant of starting, the back EMF $E_b = 0$. The total circuit resistance is $R_a + R_{ext}$.

$$I_{st,lim} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Known Values

$$60 = \frac{220}{0.4 + R_{ext}}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $R_{ext}$

$$0.4 + R_{ext} = \frac{220}{60}$$

$$0.4 + R_{ext} = 3.6667\ \Omega$$

$$R_{ext} = 3.6667 - 0.4 = 3.2667\ \Omega$$

### Step 5 — Verifying the Result

Substituting $R_{ext} = 3.2667\ \Omega$ back into the starting equation:

$$I_{st} = \frac{220}{0.4 + 3.2667} = \frac{220}{3.6667} \approx 60\ \text{A} \;\;\checkmark$$

This satisfies our 1.5 $I_{FL}$ criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Conversion Note:** The key conceptual step is realizing that *at $t = 0$*, the motor behaves like a pure resistor because no rotation → no back EMF. Once rotation starts, $E_b$ opposes $V$ and the current drops naturally, allowing the external resistance to be progressively removed.

## 3.2 Derivation — Speed at the End of a Starter Stud

**Concept:** When the motor has accelerated such that the back EMF is $E_b$, the current becomes:

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a + R_{ext}}$$

Suppose the starter has $n$ resistance studs. The current is allowed to swing between a **maximum of 1.5 $I_{FL}$** (just after cutting out a stud) and a **minimum of $I_{FL}$** (just before cutting out the next stud). The speed ratio between successive stud cut-outs is:

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V - I_{FL} R_{total,1}}{V - 1.5 I_{FL} R_{total,2}}$$

This recursive relationship is used in KTU problems to find the **number of starter studs** required for smooth acceleration.

### Step-by-Step Numerical Illustration

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $I_{FL} = 40$ A, $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$, $R_{ext} = 3.27\ \Omega$ (total).

**At Stud 1 (All resistance in):** Current just after connection is 60 A. The motor accelerates; current falls to 40 A. Speed at this instant:

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{FL} \times (R_a + R_{ext}) = 220 - 40 \times 3.67 = 220 - 146.8 = 73.2\ \text{V}$$

**Speed (as a fraction of no-load):**

$$N_1 = N_0 \times \frac{73.2}{220} = 0.333\ N_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** When all the resistance is in, the motor reaches only **33.3% of no-load speed** at full-load current. This tells us the starter must have multiple studs so that each cut-out pushes the motor into the next speed band.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Universal Starter Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **interactions** between the supply, starter, motor, and protection circuits, mapped as a functional flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUP[DC Supply V] --> LINELINE[L Terminal - Line]
    LINELINE --> OVL[Overload Release Coil OL]
    OVL --> NV[No Volt Holding Coil NV]
    NV --> ARMTERM[A Terminal - Armature]
    ARMTERM --> ARM[Motor Armature Ra]
    ARM --> EB[Back EMF Source Eb]
    EB --> RET[Return Path to Supply]
    LINELINE --> HANDLE[Starter Handle with Studs]
    HANDLE --> RESBOX[Resistance Box Rext]
    RESBOX --> ARM
    HANDLE --> SPRING[Spring Return Mechanism]
    SPRING --> OFF[OFF Position]
    NV -.magnetic pull.- HANDLE
```

## 4.2 Three-Point Starter — Detailed Architecture

The 3-point starter is the **most frequently tested** starter in KTU exams. The Mermaid block representation of its working is as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY[DC Supply Rails]
        POS[Positive Bus L]
        NEG[Negative Bus]
    end
    POS --> NVCOIL[No-Volt Coil NV]
    NVCOIL --> HANDLE1[Stud 1 Max Resistance]
    HANDLE1 --> HANDLE2[Stud 2]
    HANDLE2 --> HANDLE3[Stud 3 Min Resistance]
    HANDLE3 --> ZERO[Stud 4 - Direct]
    ZERO --> ATERM[A Terminal]
    ATERM --> ARM[Motor Armature]
    ARM --> INTERPOLE[Interpole Winding]
    INTERPOLE --> OLC[Overload Coil OL]
    OLC --> NEG
    POS --> FTERM[F Terminal - Shunt Field]
    FTERM --> SHUNT[Shunt Field Winding]
    SHUNT --> NEG
    NVCOIL -.pulls.- SPRING1[Spring Loaded Handle]
    OLC -.trips on overload.- SPRING1
```

**Operational Summary in 3 Stages:**

1. **START Position:** Handle is at the first stud, all external resistance is in series, current is limited to 60 A.
2. **ACCELERATION:** Handle moves stud-by-stud, $E_b$ builds, and resistance is reduced.
3. **RUN Position:** Handle reaches the last stud, NV coil holds the handle magnetically against the spring.

## 4.3 Four-Point Starter — Architecture and Key Difference

The 4-point starter is fundamentally different from the 3-point in **where the no-volt coil gets its supply from**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POS[Positive L] --> NV[No-Volt Coil NV]
    NV --> N1[Node N1]
    N1 --> A1[Terminal A1]
    A1 --> ARM[Armature Ra]
    ARM --> NEG[Negative Supply]
    POS --> AT[Terminal A via Studs]
    AT --> RESB[Resistance Box Rext]
    RESB --> ARM
    POS --> FT[Terminal F]
    FT --> FLD[Shunt Field]
    FLD --> NEG
    N1 -.parallel coil path.- NEG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Difference (KTU 2024 Hot Topic):** In a 4-point starter, the no-volt coil is **not** in series with the shunt field. Therefore, even if the field rheostat is varied to weaken the field for speed control, the holding coil current remains essentially constant. This eliminates the **"field weakening trip problem"** that plagues the 3-point starter.

## 4.4 Two-Point Starter — Series Motor Application

Used for **series-wound D.C. motors** (traction, cranes). The no-volt coil and the motor armature are in **series** with the supply.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POS[Positive] --> NV[No-Volt Coil]
    NV --> OLC[Overload Coil]
    OLC --> A[Motor Armature]
    A --> S[Series Field]
    S --> HANDLE[Starter Studs Resistance]
    HANDLE --> NEG[Negative]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is a starter necessary for a D.C. motor?**

**Model Answer:**
At the moment of starting a D.C. motor, the armature is at rest, so the back EMF $E_b = 0$. The armature current is therefore $I_{st} = V / R_a$, which is **8 to 10 times** the full-load current. This very high current can:
- Damage the commutator and brush assembly
- Overheat the armature winding
- Cause a severe voltage dip in the supply
- Produce a destructive mechanical shock torque

A starter inserts an external resistance in series with the armature to limit the starting current to a safe value of **1.5 to 2 times** the full-load current, and progressively removes it as the motor speeds up and develops back EMF. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a 3-point and a 4-point starter.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 3-Point Starter | 4-Point Starter |
|---|---|---|
| No. of terminals | 3 (A, F, L) | 4 (A, A1, F, L) |
| NV coil connection | In series with shunt field | In independent parallel circuit |
| Field weakening effect | Coil current drops if field weakened → handle may release | Field variation does not affect NV coil |
| Speed control above rated | Not possible (handle returns to OFF) | Possible up to 50% above base speed |
| Application | Where speed control above base is not needed | Where wide speed range is required |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a 3-point starter** for a D.C. shunt motor. Mention the functions of the no-volt coil and overload coil. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V D.C. shunt motor has an armature resistance of $0.5\ \Omega$ and a field resistance of $220\ \Omega$. The full-load armature current is 20 A. Calculate:
1. The total external resistance required at starting to limit the starting current to 1.5 times full-load current. **(4 marks)**
2. The back EMF developed at full load. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Construction:** A 3-point starter consists of (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2):

- An **insulated resistance box** with $n$ tappings brought out as **studs** along an arc.
- A **movable copper handle (starter arm)** with a brass arc that slides over the studs.
- A **spring-loaded mechanism** that pulls the handle back to the OFF position when released.
- A **No-Volt Coil (NV)** connected between terminal **F** and the negative line, in series with the shunt field.
- An **Overload Coil (OL)** in series with the armature, which on excessive current attracts a soft iron plunger to short-circuit the NV coil and trip the handle.

**Working:**

1. With handle at OFF, the circuit is open. Moving the handle to the first stud inserts the **maximum external resistance $R_{ext}$**, limiting the initial current to:
$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}}$$
2. As the motor accelerates, $E_b$ builds, and the current drops. The handle is moved to the next stud, **cutting out one section** of resistance.
3. The cycle repeats until the handle reaches the **RUN position**, where all resistance is cut out and the motor runs at its natural speed.
4. The **NV coil** is energized and magnetically holds the handle against the spring tension.
5. On **supply failure**, the NV coil de-energizes, and the spring returns the handle to OFF — automatically disconnecting the motor.
6. On **overload**, the OL coil attracts a plunger, which short-circuits the NV coil through a low resistance, weakening its magnetic pull and releasing the handle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 220\ \Omega$
- $I_{FL,a} = 20$ A
- Safe starting current: $I_{st} = 1.5 \times 20 = 30$ A

**Part (1): External Resistance Calculation (4 marks)**

At starting, $E_b = 0$:

$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}}$$

$$30 = \frac{220}{0.5 + R_{ext}}$$

$$0.5 + R_{ext} = \frac{220}{30} = 7.333\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{ext} = 7.333 - 0.5 = 6.833\ \Omega}$$

**Part (2): Back EMF at Full Load (3 marks)**

The field current is:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{220} = 1\ \text{A}$$

Applying the armature voltage equation:

$$E_b = V - I_{FL,a} \times R_a$$

$$E_b = 220 - 20 \times 0.5 = 220 - 10 = 210\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 210\ \text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Loss:**
> - **MISTAKE 1:** Some students use $I_a = I_{FL}$ directly in the starting equation. This is **wrong**; use $I_a = 1.5\,I_{FL}$ as the design limit.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** Forgetting that the back EMF at the **rated operating point** uses the *armature current* only, not the *line current*. The shunt field current must be calculated separately.
> - **MISTAKE 3:** In a 3-point starter explanation, students often forget to mention that the NV coil is in **series with the field** — this is precisely what makes it susceptible to field weakening and the *handle-release problem* at high speeds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **construction, working, and advantages** of a **4-point starter** with a suitable diagram. Why is it preferred over a 3-point starter in applications requiring wide speed ranges? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 400 V D.C. shunt motor draws a no-load current of 5 A and a full-load current of 50 A. The armature and shunt field resistances are $0.2\ \Omega$ and $200\ \Omega$ respectively. Calculate:
1. The armature current at no load. **(3 marks)**
2. The back EMF at full load. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Construction:** The 4-point starter has **four terminals**:
- **A** → connected to one end of the armature via studs and resistance box
- **A1** → connected to the other end of the armature (return path)
- **F** → connected to the shunt field winding
- **L** → connected to the positive supply line

The **No-Volt (NV) coil** is connected in an **independent parallel circuit** between terminals **L** and a separate point, completely isolated from the shunt field. The **Overload (OL) coil** is in series with the armature.

**Working:** The starting and accelerating sequences are identical to the 3-point starter. However, the NV coil current is **independent of the shunt field current** because it draws its magnetizing current from a separate parallel branch.

**Advantages over 3-point starter:**
1. The NV coil current is **not affected** by variations in the shunt field rheostat.
2. **Wide speed range** above base speed is possible via field weakening, without the handle releasing.
3. The starter can be used for **compound motors** with greater reliability.
4. Better suited for **Ward-Leonard** and **industrial process control** applications.

**Why preferred for wide speed range:** In a 3-point starter, when the field is weakened to achieve speeds above rated, the current through the NV coil (which is in series with the field) drops below the holding threshold. The spring then releases the handle — an undesirable *trip* in normal operation. The 4-point starter eliminates this. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 400$ V
- $I_{NL,line} = 5$ A
- $I_{FL,line} = 50$ A
- $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 200\ \Omega$

**Part (1): Armature current at no load (3 marks)**

Shunt field current (constant in all operating conditions):

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{400}{200} = 2\ \text{A}$$

By KCL at the no-load condition:

$$I_{a,NL} = I_{NL,line} - I_{sh} = 5 - 2 = 3\ \text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{a,NL} = 3\ \text{A}}$$

**Part (2): Back EMF at full load (4 marks)**

Full-load armature current:

$$I_{a,FL} = I_{FL,line} - I_{sh} = 50 - 2 = 48\ \text{A}$$

Applying the armature voltage equation:

$$E_b = V - I_{a,FL} \times R_a$$

$$E_b = 400 - 48 \times 0.2 = 400 - 9.6 = 390.4\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{b,FL} = 390.4\ \text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Loss of Marks Alert:**
> - The line current $I_L$ on the motor nameplate includes the **shunt field current**. Always subtract $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ to get the **armature current** $I_a$.
> - In a 4-point starter diagram, missing the **fourth terminal A1** (separate armature return) is a 2-mark deduction — examiners specifically look for this differentiation.
> - The phrase *"NV coil in series with the field"* applies ONLY to the 3-point starter. Writing it for a 4-point starter is a definitive 1-mark cut.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Principles
- ✅ Starter is **mandatory** for all D.C. motors above 0.5 kW rating.
- ✅ At $t = 0$ (start), $E_b = 0 \Rightarrow I_a = V/R_a$ is dangerously high.
- ✅ Safe limit: $I_{st,lim} = 1.5$ to $2.0 \times I_{FL}$.
- ✅ External resistance required: $R_{ext} = \dfrac{V}{1.5\,I_{FL}} - R_a$.

### 2-Point Starter
- ✅ Two terminals: **A** and **F** (line and field shorted together).
- ✅ NV coil in **series** with the motor armature.
- ✅ Used exclusively for **series D.C. motors** (traction, cranes, hoists).
- ✅ Cannot be used for shunt motors (NV coil cannot hold handle under low armature current).

### 3-Point Starter
- ✅ Three terminals: **A**, **F**, **L**.
- ✅ NV coil in **series with the shunt field** (this is its main drawback).
- ✅ Standard for fixed-speed D.C. shunt motors in industry.
- ✅ Suffers from *"field weakening trip"* during above-base-speed control.

### 4-Point Starter
- ✅ Four terminals: **A**, **A1**, **F**, **L**.
- ✅ NV coil in **independent parallel branch** — isolated from field.
- ✅ Preferred for **wide speed range** applications via field weakening.
- ✅ Used in **Ward-Leonard drives, paper mills, rolling mills, machine tools**.

### Protection Features (Common to All Starters)
- ✅ **No-Volt Protection (NVP):** NV coil de-energizes on supply failure → handle returns to OFF.
- ✅ **Overload Protection (OL):** OL coil lifts plunger → NV coil short-circuited → handle releases.
- ✅ **Spring-Loaded Handle:** Ensures fail-safe return to OFF position.

### KTU-Favourite Numerical Patterns
- ✅ Calculate $R_{ext}$ for a given $I_{st,lim}$ (most common: 7-mark problem).
- ✅ Calculate $E_b$ at full load using $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.
- ✅ Separate $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ from line current before using it in armature equations.
- ✅ Sometimes a problem asks for the **number of starter studs** given a current-swing ratio — use the recursive formula in Section 3.2.

### Examiner's Mental Model
- ✅ If the question says **"above base speed"** → answer is **4-point starter**.
- ✅ If the question says **"series motor"** → answer is **2-point starter**.
- ✅ If the question says **"simple industrial shunt motor"** → answer is **3-point starter**.
- ✅ If the question says **"why cannot we start a D.C. motor directly?"** → start with $E_b = 0$ at $N = 0$ and the $V/R_a$ current spike.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Control Circuits for Shunt and Series Motors - Three Phase Squirrel Cage and Slip Ring Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Typical Control Circuits for Shunt and Series Motors - Three Phase Squirrel Cage and Slip Ring Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **motor control circuit** is an arrangement of switching, protection, and signalling components interconnected to perform the safe, sequential, and reliable operation of an electric motor. In industrial practice, control circuits are categorized into two functional levels:

- **Power Circuit (Main Circuit):** Carries the actual load current to the motor armature/stator.
- **Control Circuit (Auxiliary Circuit):** Carries low-current signals that actuate contactors, relays, and timers; it decides *when* and *how* the power circuit operates.

For the four machines under study:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Definition Set**
> - **DC Shunt Motor Control:** A constant-field, variable-armature-voltage type control using a **DC contactor** (DCC) and a starting resistor bank cut out in steps.
> - **DC Series Motor Control:** A series-field motor starter (using **starting resistance** and a **hold-on coil**) with overload (OL) and no-volt (NV) protection.
> - **Squirrel Cage Induction Motor Control:** Starters such as **Direct-On-Line (DOL)**, **Star-Delta (Y-Δ)**, and **Autotransformer starter** are used. KTU emphasises the **Star-Delta starter** and its **automatic transition using a timer relay**.
> - **Slip Ring Induction Motor Control:** Uses a **rotor resistance starter** (liquid or metallic) to add external resistance in the rotor circuit during start, then cuts it out in steps.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a motor control circuit as the **electrical equivalent of a car's ignition, gear-shift, and dashboard combined into one**:

- The **contactor** is the *ignition key* — it physically connects the battery (supply) to the engine (motor).
- The **overload relay** is the *temperature warning light* — it disconnects the system before damage occurs.
- The **timer relay** in a Star-Delta starter is the *automatic gear-shift* — it holds the car in "low gear" (Star) long enough to build momentum, then smoothly shifts to "high gear" (Delta).
- The **no-volt coil** is the *dead-man switch* — if you fall off (power fails), the machine stops; it will not restart by itself.

> [!NOTE]
> The student should always remember: **Power Circuit = muscles; Control Circuit = brain.** Power circuit handles current, control circuit handles decisions.

### 1.3 Standard Symbols & Legend

| Symbol | Component | Standard Used |
|---|---|---|
| **→○** | Push-button (NO) | IEC 60617 |
| **→●** | Push-button (NC) | IEC 60617 |
| **╫** (line with cross) | Contactor / Power contact | IEC |
| **⏚** (semicircle) | Overload (thermal) relay | IEC |
| **⌒** (half circle) | No-volt / Hold-on coil | IEC |
| **T or t** | Timer contact (on-delay) | IEC |
| **R₁, R₂, Rₙ** | Starting resistors | – |
| **M** | Motor (universal symbol) | – |

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics Used in Industrial Practice

- **Control circuit voltage:** **110 V AC, 230 V AC, 24 V DC, 110 V DC** (most common in industry for safe handling and noise immunity).
- **Power circuit voltage for induction motors:** **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz** (India standard).
- **Contact rating of contactor:** Specified in **AC-3 duty** (for squirrel-cage motors) as per IEC 60947-4-1.
- **Starting current of squirrel-cage motor at DOL:** Typically **5 to 7 times** the full-load current.
- **Standard starting torque ratios:** Star-Delta gives **1/3 of DOL starting torque**; Autotransformer at 70 % tap gives **0.49 of DOL torque**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Shunt Motor – Typical Control Circuit

**Operating Principle:** The field winding is connected **across the supply (shunt)**, hence receives a nearly constant voltage. The armature current is large, so a **step-starting resistor** is included in series with the armature to limit the starting inrush (otherwise it would be **10 to 20 times** the rated current).

**Sequence of Operation:**
1. **START push-button** is pressed → energises the **DC contactor coil (DCC)**.
2. **DCC main contacts close** → power is applied to the armature through the starting resistor bank (R₁ + R₂ + R₃).
3. The **holding (auxiliary NO) contact** of DCC bypasses the START button (latching).
4. The **armature accelerates**; back-EMF builds, current drops.
5. **Accelerating contactors / relays (1A, 2A)** are energised by current-sensitive relays (or by timed sequence) to progressively short-circuit R₁, then R₂, then R₃.
6. **STOP push-button (NC)** breaks the holding circuit → DCC de-energises → motor coasts to rest.
7. **Overload relay (OL)** — a bimetallic strip — opens its NC contact in the control circuit on sustained overcurrent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why start resistors?** At standstill, back-EMF $E_b = 0$, so $I_a = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$. With $R_a$ very small, the inrush is enormous. Adding external resistance brings this down to a safe value (typically **1.5 to 2 times** rated current).

### 2.2 DC Series Motor – Typical Control Circuit

**Special Considerations:**
- The field is in **series with the armature** — current is common.
- Must **never be started without load** (will run away to dangerous speeds).
- Uses a **No-Volt Release (NVR) coil** instead of a holding contact in some designs.
- Has a **field weakening** arrangement (series-parallel switch) for speed control above base speed.

**Sequence:**
1. Press START → **NVR coil** energised → its plunger closes the line contactor.
2. The plunger is **mechanically held** in the closed position.
3. Starting resistors are progressively cut out by **timer / current relays**.
4. On power failure, the NVR coil de-energises, the plunger drops, and the contactor opens (fail-safe).
5. Pressing STOP mechanically releases the plunger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **NVR (No-Volt Release) coil** is the hallmark of the series-motor starter. In shunt-motor starters, a **NO holding contact** performs the equivalent function electrically.

### 2.3 Three-Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor – Starters

#### A) Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter

Simplest, cheapest. Connects the stator directly to the 3-phase line.

**Drawback:** Starting current = **5 to 7 × FLC**; starting torque ≈ **1.5 to 2 × rated torque**. Not allowed for large motors (> **5 HP / 3.7 kW** in many Indian industrial regulations).

#### B) Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter

**Principle:** During start, the stator is connected in **Star** so each phase receives $V_L / \sqrt{3}$, hence the starting current and torque become **1/3 of DOL values**.

**Sequence (Automatic type using Timer):**
1. Press START → **Main Contactor (K1)** and **Star Contactor (K2)** energise. Motor runs in Star.
2. After preset time (timer T), K2 opens and **Delta Contactor (K3)** closes. Motor runs in Delta.
3. A **mechanical / electrical interlock** between K2 and K3 prevents both from closing simultaneously (which would cause a short circuit).
4. Overload relay (OL) in the control circuit protects against sustained overcurrent.

#### C) Autotransformer Starter

Uses a **3-phase autotransformer** with multiple taps (typically **50 %, 65 %, 80 %**). During start, motor receives reduced voltage; after acceleration, transfer is made to full voltage (bypassing the autotransformer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Korndörfer connection** is a special autotransformer starter in which the motor is momentarily disconnected (open transition) — KTU frequently asks to compare **closed transition (Korndörfer)** vs **open transition**.

### 2.4 Three-Phase Slip-Ring Induction Motor – Rotor Resistance Starter

**Why needed?** At start, slip $s = 1$, so rotor induced frequency and reactance are maximum. By adding **external rotor resistance**:

- Starting current is **reduced**.
- Starting torque is **increased** (in fact, maximum torque can be obtained at start by suitable resistance value).

**Starter Construction:**
- **Liquid type:** Uses an electrolyte (e.g., sodium carbonate solution) in a tank; electrodes are lowered/raised to vary resistance.
- **Metallic (resistance) type:** Uses a bank of resistors with a face-plate controller.

**Sequence:**
1. Rotor resistance is **maximum** at start.
2. As motor accelerates, the **starter handle / contactor** progressively cuts out resistance in steps.
3. At full speed, all resistance is cut out and rotor winding is short-circuited (or connected to a slip-ring shorting device).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{st,\,DOL} = 5 \text{ to } 7 \times I_{FL}$ | DOL starting current of squirrel-cage motor | A |
| 2 | $I_{st,\,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \, I_{st,\,DOL}$ | Star starting current (line current) | A |
| 3 | $T_{st,\,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \, T_{st,\,DOL}$ | Star starting torque | N·m |
| 4 | $T_{st,\,auto} = x^{2} \, T_{st,\,DOL}$ | Torque at autotransformer tap ratio $x$ | N·m |
| 5 | $I_{st,\,auto} = x^{2} \, I_{st,\,DOL}$ | Current at autotransformer tap ratio $x$ | A |
| 6 | $T \propto \dfrac{s \, E_2^{\,2}}{R_2^{\,2} + (sX_2)^{2}}$ | Torque of induction motor | N·m |
| 7 | $s_{max\,T} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | Slip at maximum torque | – |
| 8 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{k \, V^{2}}{2 X_2}$ | Maximum torque (independent of $R_2$) | N·m |
| 9 | $I_{a,\,start} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | DC motor starting current (no back-EMF) | A |
| 10 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back-EMF equation (running) | V |
| 11 | $E_b = k \phi \omega$ | Back-EMF proportional to speed | V·s/rad |
| 12 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\phi}$ | Speed of DC motor (key speed equation) | rpm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** "**Y** gives **Y**ou **T**hird — current **and** torque both become **1/3**" — applies to Star-Delta. For autotransformer, **both** scale with $x^{2}$, NOT 1/3.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cranes and hoists** use DC series motors for high starting torque.
- **Lathes and machine tools** use DC shunt motors for nearly constant speed.
- **Pumps, fans, compressors** use squirrel-cage motors with Star-Delta or autotransformer starters.
- **High-inertia loads (mills, kilns)** use slip-ring motors with rotor resistance starters.
