## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Star-Delta Reduces Starting Current to 1/3

**Given:** A 3-phase, Y-connected motor has line voltage $V_L$ and per-phase impedance $Z_{ph}$.

**Step 1 — Direct-On-Line connection in Delta:**

In delta, phase voltage = line voltage. So:

$$V_{ph,\,\Delta} = V_L$$

Phase current:

$$I_{ph,\,\Delta} = \dfrac{V_{ph,\,\Delta}}{Z_{ph}} = \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

Line current in delta is $\sqrt{3}$ times phase current:

$$I_{L,\,\Delta} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,\,\Delta} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 2 — Star connection during start:**

In star, line voltage = $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ ⟹ $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. Phase current = line current in star.

$$I_{L,\,Y} = I_{ph,\,Y} = \dfrac{V_{ph,\,Y}}{Z_{ph}} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$\dfrac{I_{L,\,Y}}{I_{L,\,\Delta}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}}$$

Simplify numerator and denominator:

$$\dfrac{I_{L,\,Y}}{I_{L,\,\Delta}} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{ph}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L}$$

Cancel $V_L$ and $Z_{ph}$:

$$\dfrac{I_{L,\,Y}}{I_{L,\,\Delta}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{I_{L,\,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot I_{L,\,\Delta}}$$

**Step 4 — Torque ratio:**

For an induction motor, $T \propto V^{2}$ (at constant slip during start, $s = 1$).

$$\dfrac{T_{Y}}{T_{\Delta}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{ph,\,Y}}{V_{ph,\,\Delta}}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{T_{Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot T_{\Delta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both **current and torque** are reduced to **1/3** of DOL values in Star-Delta. This is the key KTU result.

### 3.2 Derivation: Autotransformer Tap Relation

**Given:** Autotransformer tap ratio $x$ (e.g., $x = 0.7$ for 70 % tap). Motor sees voltage $x V_L$.

**Step 1 — Motor starting current at reduced voltage** (treated as a transformer-coupled load):

$$I_{motor} = \dfrac{x V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 2 — Line current on supply side** (autotransformer transforms by $x$ on the input side):

$$I_{line} = x \cdot I_{motor} = x \cdot \dfrac{x V_L}{Z_{ph}} = x^{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with DOL line current** ($V_L / Z_{ph}$ in delta equivalent):

$$\dfrac{I_{line,\,auto}}{I_{line,\,DOL}} = x^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Torque ratio** ($T \propto V^{2}$ at start):

$$\dfrac{T_{auto}}{T_{DOL}} = x^{2}$$

**Numerical example (KTU typical):** $x = 0.65$

$$I_{auto} = 0.65^{2} \cdot I_{DOL} = 0.4225 \, I_{DOL}$$

$$T_{auto} = 0.4225 \, T_{DOL}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **same $x^{2}$ factor** applies to both current AND torque for autotransformer — this is **different** from Star-Delta where both are 1/3. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Rotor Resistance on Starting Torque (Slip-Ring Motor)

**Starting torque of induction motor:**

$$T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^{2} \cdot (R_2 / s)}{(R_2/s)^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

**At start, $s = 1$:**

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \, V^{2}}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_2^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

**Add external rotor resistance $R_{ext}$ so that $R_2' = R_2 + R_{ext}$:**

$$T_{st}' = \dfrac{3 \, V^{2}}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{(R_2 + R_{ext})^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

**For maximum starting torque**, we need $R_2' = X_2$ at $s = 1$, i.e.:

$$R_2 + R_{ext} = X_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{ext} = X_2 - R_2$$

**Conclusion:** By choosing $R_{ext}$ appropriately, we make the **starting torque equal to the maximum (pull-out) torque** — a unique advantage of slip-ring motors.

### 3.4 Derivation: Why DC Shunt Motor Needs Starting Resistor

At standstill, $E_b = k \phi \omega = 0$ (since $\omega = 0$). Hence:

$$I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$$

If $V = 220$ V and $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ (typical small motor):

$$I_a = \dfrac{220}{0.5} = 440 \text{ A}$$

Rated current is, say, 20 A. So starting current is **22 times** rated — destructive. Adding $R_{ext} = 10 \, \Omega$:

$$I_a^{new} = \dfrac{220}{10 + 0.5} = \dfrac{220}{10.5} \approx 20.95 \text{ A}$$

This is close to rated current. As motor speeds up, $E_b$ rises and $I_a$ falls; the external resistance is cut out step by step.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Starters

| Feature | DOL | Star-Delta | Autotransformer | Slip-Ring (Rotor R) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Starting current | $5\text{–}7 \times I_{FL}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3} \times I_{DOL}$ | $x^{2} \times I_{DOL}$ | Lower (depends on $R_{ext}$) |
| Starting torque | $1.5\text{–}2 \times T_{FL}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3} \times T_{DOL}$ | $x^{2} \times T_{DOL}$ | Up to $T_{max}$ (adjustable) |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | High | High (rings + resistors) |
| Smooth start | No (jerk) | Open transition (jerk) | Closed transition possible | Yes (very smooth) |
| Suitable motor | Small SCIM | Medium SCIM | Large SCIM | Slip-ring (wound rotor) |
| Starting power loss | – | Reduced | Reduced | High in $R_{ext}$ (resistive loss) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
**List the protective devices used in a typical DC shunt motor starter. State the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A typical DC shunt motor starter incorporates the following protective devices:

1. **Overload Relay (OL):** A bimetallic strip or solenoid-based device. On sustained overcurrent (typically **115 % to 125 %** of rated), the strip heats, bends, and opens the NC contact in the control circuit, de-energising the line contactor.

2. **No-Volt / Under-Voltage Relay (NVR/UVR):** Monitors the supply voltage; if voltage falls below a threshold (usually **80 %** of rated), the contactor is de-energised to prevent the motor from stalling or operating at unsafe torque.

3. **Field Failure Relay (FFR):** Specifically for shunt motors. If the field current collapses (field circuit open), the motor may run away to dangerous speeds. FFR detects this and trips the starter.

4. **Single-Phase Preventer (SPP):** In 3-phase systems, loss of one phase causes negative-sequence currents and overheating; SPP disconnects the motor.

5. **Short-Circuit Protection (HRC Fuse / MCB):** Provides instantaneous clearance on dead short circuits.

6. **Earth-Fault Relay (EFR):** Detects leakage to earth and disconnects the system.

> **Memory tip (KTU): "O L U V F S E"** – **O**verload, **L**ocked rotor, **U**nder-voltage, **V**oltage asymmetry, **F**ield failure, **S**ingle phasing, **E**arth fault.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]**
**Why is a slip-ring induction motor preferred over a squirrel-cage motor for high-inertia loads like cement mills and hoists?**

**Model Answer:**

A slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motor is preferred for high-inertia loads for the following reasons:

1. **High starting torque with low starting current:** By inserting external resistance in the rotor circuit at start, the starting current is reduced and the starting torque can be made **equal to the maximum (pull-out) torque**, which is impossible with a squirrel-cage motor.

2. **Smooth acceleration:** The external resistance is cut out in steps, giving a stepless (gradual) rise in speed — no mechanical jerk on the driven load.

3. **Adjustable speed under load:** By keeping some rotor resistance in circuit, the operating slip can be increased, giving speed control (though not as efficient as a VFD).

4. **Reduced starting stress on supply:** Since starting current is much lower (typically **1.5 × FLC** versus **5–7 × FLC** for squirrel cage), voltage dips in the supply grid are avoided.

**Conclusion:** For high-inertia loads (mills, hoists, crushers, conveyors with loaded start), the slip-ring motor provides the necessary starting torque without the supply-side disturbances that a squirrel-cage DOL start would cause.

---

### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

---

**Part B – Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **automatic Star-Delta starter** for a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. Mention the role of the timer relay and the interlock.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A three-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 10 HP, 6-pole induction motor is started using a Star-Delta starter. The motor has a full-load current of **15 A** and a starting current of **75 A** in DOL condition. The starting torque in DOL condition is **40 N·m**. Determine: (i) starting current in star, (ii) starting torque in star, and (iii) comment on the suitability of this starter for a load that requires **30 N·m** to start.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to (a) — Automatic Star-Delta Starter:**

**Components used:**
- **K1** – Main contactor (connects line to motor).
- **K2** – Star contactor (shorts the three stator terminals together at the star point).
- **K3** – Delta contactor (connects the three stator terminals to the line in delta).
- **T** – On-delay timer relay (typically **5 to 30 seconds**).
- **OL** – Overload relay.
- **START** (NO) and **STOP** (NC) push buttons.
- **Interlock contacts** between K2 and K3 (NC of K2 in series with K3 coil and vice-versa).

**Power Circuit (Brief Description):**
- L1, L2, L3 → K1 main contacts → motor terminals U1, V1, W1.
- The opposite ends (W2, U2, V2) are connected to K3 delta contactor (for delta run) and to K2 star contactor (for star start).

**Control Circuit Sequence:**

1. **Initial State:** K1, K2, K3 all de-energised. Timer T is reset. Motor is OFF.

2. **Press START (NO):** The control circuit is completed → **K1 coil** and **K2 coil** energise.
   - K1 main contacts close → supply reaches the motor.
   - K2 main contacts close → motor stator is connected in **Star** (W2, U2, V2 shorted).
   - K1's NO holding contact latches across START.
   - The **timer T begins counting**.
   - K2's NC contact in the K3 line opens (interlock) — K3 cannot energise.

3. **After preset time (t = 5 to 30 s):** Timer T contact changes over.
   - T's NC contact in K2 line **opens** → K2 de-energises → Star contactor opens. Motor momentarily disconnects from supply (open transition).
   - T's NO contact in K3 line **closes** → K3 coil energises → Delta contactor closes → motor now runs in **Delta** at full voltage.
   - K3's NC contact in K2 line opens (interlock) → even if K2 tries to re-energise, it cannot.

4. **Press STOP (NC):** Holding circuit breaks → K1, K3 de-energise → motor coasts to rest.

5. **Overload:** If sustained overcurrent occurs, the **OL relay's NC contact opens** → K1 de-energises → motor stops.

**Significance of Timer Relay:** Provides the necessary acceleration time in Star so that the motor builds up speed and reduces the inrush current before transition to Delta — the **time delay is usually set to the motor's acceleration time** (5 to 10 s for small motors, 15 to 30 s for large ones).

**Significance of Interlock:** Mechanically and electrically prevents K2 and K3 from closing simultaneously. If both close, a **dead short circuit** occurs across two phases → catastrophic failure.

**Diagram (textual):**
```
         L1      L2      L3
          |       |       |
         [K1]    [K1]    [K1]   ← Main contactor K1
          |       |       |
         U1      V1      W1   ← Motor terminals (line side)
          |       |       |
          +-------+-------+
          |   Stator Windings  |
          |                    |
         W2      U2      V2    ← Motor terminals (delta side)
          |       |       |
   +------+       |       +------+
   |              |              |
  [K2 Star]    [K3 Delta]    [K3 Delta]   ← Bottom: K2 shorts all three (Star)
   shorts                              ↑
   W2 U2 V2                            K3 connects W2 to L1, U2 to L2, V2 to L3 (Delta)
```

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $V_L = 400$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 10$ HP (≈ **7.46 kW**)
- $I_{FL} = 15$ A
- $I_{st,\,DOL} = 75$ A
- $T_{st,\,DOL} = 40$ N·m

**(i) Starting current in Star:**

Using the proven relation (Section 3.1):

$$I_{st,\,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times I_{st,\,\Delta} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 75 = \mathbf{25 \text{ A}}$$

**(ii) Starting torque in Star:**

$$T_{st,\,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times T_{st,\,\Delta} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 40 = \mathbf{13.33 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iii) Suitability for load requiring 30 N·m to start:**

Available starting torque in Star = **13.33 N·m**
Required starting torque = **30 N·m**

Since $13.33 < 30$, the motor **cannot start the load** in Star mode. The load will stall. Hence, **this starter is NOT suitable** for this application.

**Recommendation:** Use an **autotransformer starter at 80 % tap** ($x = 0.8$):

$$T_{st,\,auto} = x^{2} \times T_{st,\,DOL} = 0.8^{2} \times 40 = 0.64 \times 40 = 25.6 \text{ N·m}$$

Still not enough. At 100 % tap (DOL), $T = 40$ N·m — sufficient. So we need an **autotransformer with 90 % tap** for smoother start, or a **different starting method** (VFD or soft-starter).

> **Answer:** $I_{st,\,Y} = 25$ A, $T_{st,\,Y} = 13.33$ N·m, **Starter NOT suitable** (insufficient starting torque).

---

### Part B – Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the **rotor resistance starter** for a 3-phase slip-ring induction motor. What is the role of the **face-plate controller**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole slip-ring induction motor has rotor resistance per phase $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance per phase $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$. Calculate: (i) the slip at maximum torque, (ii) the external resistance required to obtain maximum torque at starting (slip = 1), and (iii) the ratio of starting torque (with external resistance) to the torque at slip 0.05 without external resistance.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to (a) — Rotor Resistance Starter:**

**Construction:**

A rotor resistance starter consists of the following parts:

1. **Stator Contactor (S):** Connects the 3-phase stator to the supply.

2. **Three Slip Rings:** Mounted on the rotor shaft, each connected to one end of the rotor winding. Carbon brushes ride on these rings.

3. **External Resistance Bank:** Three star-connected banks of resistors (R₁, R₂, R₃ per phase) connected to the slip rings via the brushes.

4. **Face-Plate Controller:** A manually operated rotary switch with moving contacts that progressively short-circuit sections of the resistance bank as the handle is moved from the "Start" position to the "Run" position.

5. **Overload Relay (OL):** Protects against sustained overcurrent.

6. **No-Volt Release (NVR):** Ensures the starter returns to "OFF" on power failure.

**Working:**

1. **Initial position (handle at Start):** All external resistance (R₁ + R₂ + R₃) is **in series** with the rotor winding. Motor is at standstill.

2. **Stator is energised** by pressing START. Motor starts to rotate. With high rotor resistance, starting current is limited and starting torque is high (close to maximum).

3. **As motor picks up speed**, the operator (or an automatic timer/current relay) **moves the face-plate handle** through positions 1, 2, 3, … cutting out resistance step by step.

4. **Final position (handle at Run):** All external resistance is short-circuited. The slip rings may be **short-circuited** by a separate device. Brushes may be lifted in some designs to reduce wear.

**Role of Face-Plate Controller:**

The face-plate controller is the **mechanism** that allows progressive, controlled cutting out of the rotor resistance. It:

- Provides **manual or motorised** step-by-step resistance reduction.
- Has **clearly marked positions** (Start → 1 → 2 → 3 → Run) for repeatable operation.
- Ensures the resistance cut-out is **in synchronisation with rotor acceleration**.
- In **automatic versions**, the same function is performed by **contactors controlled by timers or current relays**.

**Diagram (textual):**
```
   L1 L2 L3 → Stator Contactor → Stator (3-phase winding)
                                  Rotor (slip rings)
                                  |    |    |
                                 B1   B2   B3   ← Brushes
                                  |    |    |
                              ┌───┴────┴────┴───┐
                              │ External R Bank │
                              │  R₁   R₂   R₃  │
                              └───┬────┬────┬───┘
                                  ↑ Moving handle (face-plate)
                                  |
                            Final position shorts all resistors
                            and the slip rings are shorted
```

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$
- Number of poles = 6, $f = 50$ Hz

**(i) Slip at maximum torque:**

The standard relation is:

$$s_{max\,T} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2} = \dfrac{0.2}{1.0} = \mathbf{0.2 \;\; (or\ 20\,\%)}$$

**(ii) External resistance for maximum torque at starting ($s = 1$):**

At $s = 1$, the effective rotor resistance per phase is $R_2' = R_2 + R_{ext}$.
For maximum torque at start, $R_2' = X_2$:

$$R_2 + R_{ext} = X_2$$
$$0.2 + R_{ext} = 1.0$$
$$\boxed{R_{ext} = 0.8 \; \Omega \text{ per phase}}$$

**(iii) Ratio of starting torque (with $R_{ext}$) to torque at $s = 0.05$ without $R_{ext}$:**

The torque equation (per phase basis, simplified form):

$$T \propto \dfrac{s \, R_2'}{R_2'^{2} + (s X_2)^{2}}$$

We need to compare:
- **Starting torque with $R_{ext}$:** $s = 1$, $R_2' = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$
- **Torque at $s = 0.05$ without $R_{ext}$:** $R_2' = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $s = 0.05$

Since $T_{max}$ itself is **independent of $R_2$**, the starting torque with $R_{ext}$ (which equals $R_2' = X_2$) is the **maximum torque** $T_{max}$.

**Calculate $T_{st,\,with}$ (numerator):**

$$T_{st,\,with} \propto \dfrac{1 \times 1.0}{(1.0)^{2} + (1 \times 1.0)^{2}} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.0 + 1.0} = \dfrac{1.0}{2.0} = 0.5$$

**Calculate $T_{s=0.05,\,without}$ (denominator):**

$$T_{0.05} \propto \dfrac{0.05 \times 0.2}{(0.2)^{2} + (0.05 \times 1.0)^{2}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$0.05 \times 0.2 = 0.010$$

Compute denominator:

$$(0.2)^{2} = 0.04 \quad ; \quad (0.05)^{2} = 0.0025$$

$$0.04 + 0.0025 = 0.0425$$

So:

$$T_{0.05} \propto \dfrac{0.010}{0.0425} = 0.2353$$

**Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{T_{st,\,with}}{T_{0.05,\,without}} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.2353} = \mathbf{2.125}$$

> **Answer:** $s_{max\,T} = 0.2$; $R_{ext} = 0.8 \, \Omega$; Ratio = **2.125** (starting torque with external resistance is about **2.1 times** the running torque at $s = 0.05$ without external resistance).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Mistakes – Read carefully before the exam!**
> 1. **Interlock symbol confusion:** Many students draw the interlock between K2 (Star) and K3 (Delta) but forget that **TWO** interlocks are needed (NC of K2 in K3 coil line AND NC of K3 in K2 coil line). Single interlock is **incomplete** — loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Star-Delta numerical mistake:** Some students compute $I_{st,Y} = I_{st,\Delta} / \sqrt{3}$. This is WRONG. The correct factor is **1/3**, not $1/\sqrt{3}$. The $1/\sqrt{3}$ is the **voltage** factor; current has an additional $\sqrt{3}$ from delta-to-star topology, giving overall **1/3**.
> 3. **Forgetting timer setting rationale:** Just saying "timer is set to 10 s" loses marks. State the **principle** — "set equal to the motor's acceleration time in star to a value where the transition surge is acceptable".
> 4. **Confusing NVR with overload:** The NVR (No-Volt Release) protects against **loss of supply voltage**; the OL relay protects against **overcurrent**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting the "open transition" issue in Star-Delta:** During the brief K2-off-K3-on interval, the motor is momentarily disconnected → a current surge occurs at re-closure in Delta. **Mention this** for full marks.
> 6. **Series motor cannot start on no-load:** Frequently tested. Always state this explicitly when describing the series motor starter.
> 7. **Drawing the slip-ring rotor resistance bank as delta-connected:** The rotor external resistance bank is **star-connected** (always). One of the most common drawing errors.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **DC shunt motor starter** has a **step-starting resistor** cut out by current/timer relays, a **holding contact** for latching, and an **overload relay** in the control circuit.
- A **DC series motor starter** uses a **No-Volt Release (NVR) coil** with mechanical plunger hold. The motor must **never start on no load** (runaway speed risk).
- A **DOL starter** is the simplest but draws **5–7 × FLC** — suitable only for small motors (≤ 5 HP typically).
- A **Star-Delta starter** reduces both current and torque to **1/3 of DOL values** — derivation from basic line/phase voltage relations is essential.
- An **autotransformer starter** at tap $x$ reduces both current and torque by factor **$x^{2}$** — NOT 1/3. Tap 70 % → 0.49 of DOL.
- A **slip-ring motor starter** uses **external rotor resistance** to obtain high starting torque with low starting current. For maximum starting torque, $R_2 + R_{ext} = X_2$.
- **Slip at maximum torque:** $s_{max\,T} = R_2 / X_2$ — independent of voltage.
- **Maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance** — this is a KTU-favourite concept.
- **Interlocks** between star and delta contactors are **mandatory** for safety.
- **Timers** in automatic starters are set to the motor's acceleration time.
- **Overload relay** always sits in the **control circuit** (low current), even though it senses the **power circuit** (high current) via bimetallic heaters or CTs.
- **Back-EMF equation:** $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ — at start, $E_b = 0$, hence high $I_a$.
- **Speed equation of DC motor:** $N \propto (V - I_a R_a)/\phi$ — fundamental for understanding speed control.
- **Standard control voltage in industry:** 110 V AC (for safety) or 24 V DC (for electronics). Supply 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz is the Indian standard for industrial motors.
- **Contactors are rated by AC-3 duty** (induction motor starting and stopping) as per IEC 60947-4-1.
- **KTU most-asked:** Difference between DOL, Star-Delta, and Autotransformer — be ready with the table from Section 3.5.
- **KTU second-most-asked:** Rotor resistance starter — circuit diagram and slip-ring connections.
- **KTU third-most-asked:** Why DC series motor cannot start on no-load.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional and Solid-State Speed Control of D.C and A.C Drives - Speed Control of DC Series and Shunt Motors - Armature and Field Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conventional and Solid-State Speed Control of D.C and A.C Drives - Speed Control of DC Series and Shunt Motors - Armature and Field Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Electrical Drive** is a system employed for motion control comprising an electric motor, a power electronic controller/modulator, a sensor-feedback assembly, and a mechanical load. **Speed Control of a DC Motor** is the deliberate regulation of the rotational speed of a DC machine by varying one or more of its input electrical parameters (armature voltage, field flux, or armature resistance) to meet a desired operating point defined by the load torque–speed characteristic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 4**
> The scope here covers two parallel taxonomies of DC motor speed control:
> 1. **Conventional Methods** — Resistive/electromechanical (rheostatic control, field diverters, series–parallel grouping).
> 2. **Solid-State Methods** — Power-electronic converters (linear amplifiers, choppers, controlled rectifiers, dual converters).

The fundamental **speed equation** of a DC motor (valid for both shunt and series machines in the linear magnetic region) is:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

where the symbols follow standard KTU notation: $N$ = speed in rpm, $V$ = applied armature voltage, $I_a$ = armature current, $R_a$ = armature resistance, $\Phi$ = flux per pole, and $k$ = machine construction constant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tap" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world intuition:** Imagine a ceiling fan. To make it spin faster, you either
> (a) increase the **electrical pressure** (voltage) pushing current into the motor — like turning a water tap harder,
> or (b) reduce the **magnetic grip** of the field winding — like loosening a brake on a rotating wheel.
> Both actions cause more revolutions per minute, but they affect torque differently. This is the heart of **armature voltage control** vs **field flux control**.

**Key physical constants / metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
- **Rated Speed (N₀)**: Manufacturer-specified base speed, typically **1500 rpm** or **3000 rpm** for industrial DC machines.
- **Base Speed Region (Constant Torque Region)**: $0 \le N \le N_0$, where armature voltage control is applied.
- **Field Weakening Region (Constant Power Region)**: $N > N_0$, achieved by reducing $\Phi$ below its rated value.
- **Speed Regulation**: $(N_{NL} - N_{FL})/N_{FL} \times 100\%$, typically **5%–15%** for a well-designed DC shunt motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pearl:** The KTU examiner expects the student to explicitly distinguish between the **constant-torque region** (below base speed, armature control) and the **constant-power region** (above base speed, field weakening). Confusing these regions is the single largest source of 2–3 mark deductions in ESE answers.

## 1.3 Why Speed Control Matters in Industrial Drives

Speed control is NOT merely about "going faster or slower". It is the primary means of:
- **Matching motor speed to process speed** (e.g., rolling mills, paper machines, elevators).
- **Optimising energy consumption** — DC drives at partial speed via field weakening can save **20%–40%** input power.
- **Providing soft-start and dynamic braking** — eliminating mechanical stress.
- **Achieving four-quadrant operation** — Forward motoring, Forward braking, Reverse motoring, Reverse braking (e.g., CNC, robotic arms).
]<]minimax[>[</section1>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Equations of a DC Motor

A DC machine is governed by three coupled electromechanical equations that the student must internalise before tackling any speed-control problem.

### 2.1.1 Voltage (KVL around the armature loop)

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

The applied voltage $V$ is balanced by the back-EMF $E_b$ and the ohmic drop $I_a R_a$. This is the most-cited equation in KTU 2-mark and 7-mark problems.

### 2.1.2 Back-EMF (Faraday's Law for a rotating armature)

$$
E_b = k \Phi N \quad \text{(with N in appropriate units)}
$$

or, in SI form:

$$
E_b = k \Phi \omega_m
$$

where $\omega_m$ is the mechanical angular speed in rad/s.

### 2.1.3 Electromagnetic Torque (Lorentz force on armature conductors)

$$
T_e = k \Phi I_a
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for speed control:** Combining the three equations, we obtain $N \propto (V - I_a R_a) / \Phi$. Therefore, **speed can be controlled by manipulating exactly three physical quantities** — $V$, $R_a$, or $\Phi$. This is the conceptual foundation of every method in Module 4.

## 2.2 Speed Control of a DC Shunt Motor

A shunt motor has its field winding connected **in parallel** with the armature. Because the shunt field draws only a small current and sees a near-constant terminal voltage, the flux $\Phi$ is approximately **constant** (neglecting armature reaction). This gives a near-linear speed–armature-voltage relationship.

### 2.2.1 Armature Voltage Control (Below Base Speed)

Here the field is held at rated flux and the armature voltage $V$ is varied from 0 to rated $V_{rated}$.

$$
N = \frac{V}{k \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{k \Phi} I_a = N_0 - \Delta N
$$

**Operational characteristics:**
- Torque capability at any speed is **constant** (equal to rated torque), since $\Phi = \Phi_{rated}$ and $I_a$ can be pushed to its rated value.
- Speed drop from no-load to full-load is small → good **speed regulation**.
- Below base speed, this is the **preferred method** for constant-torque loads (conveyors, hoists).

**Conventional (electromechanical) implementation:** Insert a variable resistor $R_{ext}$ in series with the armature. The total series resistance becomes $R_a + R_{ext}$, so:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{ext})}{k \Phi}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Energy inefficiency:** Rheostatic armature control dissipates $I_a^2 R_{ext}$ as heat. For a 10 kW motor running at half speed, you can lose up to 5 kW in the external resistor. This is why solid-state choppers replaced rheostats in modern drives.

**Solid-state implementation (Chopper / DC–DC Converter):** A chopper switches the armature at high frequency ($1\text{ kHz}$ to $20\text{ kHz}$) with a duty cycle $\alpha = T_{on}/T$. The effective average armature voltage is:

$$
V_a = \alpha V_s
$$

where $V_s$ is the fixed DC supply. This gives **lossless, continuous, and precise** speed control from 0 to rated voltage.

### 2.2.2 Field Flux Control (Above Base Speed)

Here the armature receives rated voltage and the field current is reduced by adding resistance in the field circuit, weakening $\Phi$.

$$
N = \frac{V_{rated} - I_a R_a}{k \Phi} \quad \text{with} \quad \Phi < \Phi_{rated}
$$

**Operational characteristics:**
- As $\Phi$ decreases, $N$ increases — but $T_e = k \Phi I_a$ **decreases** for the same armature current.
- Since $P = T_e \omega$, the **power** remains approximately **constant**.
- Therefore field weakening operates in the **constant-power region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical limit:** Field flux cannot be reduced below ~30% of rated flux because armature reaction becomes severe, commutation deteriorates, and the speed may "run away" if the field circuit opens. KTU board questions often probe this **open-field hazard** for the series motor.

## 2.3 Speed Control of a DC Series Motor

A series motor carries the **same current** through armature and field: $I_a = I_{se} = I_L$. Its flux is a saturable function of armature current:

$$
\Phi \propto I_a \quad \text{(linear region)}
$$

### 2.3.1 Armature (Rheostatic) Control

A resistor $R_{ext}$ is inserted in series with the series combination of armature and series field.

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se} + R_{ext})}{k \Phi} \quad \text{with} \quad \Phi \propto I_a
$$

**Characteristic:** For a series motor, speed varies **inversely with armature current** in the linear region, leading to the famous hyperbolic N–I curve.

### 2.3.2 Field Divertor / Tapped-Field Control

A **divertor** is a variable resistor placed in **parallel with the series field winding**. Diverting some current away from the field reduces $\Phi$, raising $N$ without inserting any resistance in the armature circuit (hence lower losses than rheostatic control).

### 2.3.3 Series–Parallel Grouping (for multiple series motors)

For traction applications (e.g., two motors), connecting the motors in **series** at low speeds (half voltage across each → slow) and in **parallel** at high speeds (full voltage across each → fast) gives two discrete speed steps with very high efficiency — the **classical tram/EMU scheme**.

### 2.3.4 Solid-State Control of Series Motors

| Converter | Application |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Single-phase half-controlled / fully-controlled bridge** | Low-power traction, forklift drives |
| **Three-phase fully-controlled bridge** | Medium-power industrial drives |
| **Chopper (Buck / Class-A)** | DC traction, battery-powered vehicles |
| **Dual converter (back-to-back SCR bridge)** | Four-quadrant operation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Boundary Condition | Unit |
|---|----------|------------------------------------|------|
| 1 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL; valid for all DC motors | V |
| 2 | $E_b = k \Phi N$ | Back-EMF induced in armature (linear magnetic region) | V |
| 3 | $T_e = k \Phi I_a$ | Developed electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 4 | $N = (V - I_a R_a)/(k \Phi)$ | **Master speed equation** — basis of all control methods | rpm |
| 5 | $N_0 = V / (k \Phi_{rated})$ | Ideal no-load speed (no armature drop) | rpm |
| 6 | $N \propto V$ (shunt, $\Phi = $ const) | Armature voltage control relation | — |
| 7 | $N \propto 1/\Phi$ (shunt, $V = $ const) | Field flux control relation | — |
| 8 | $N \propto 1/I_a$ (series, linear region) | Series motor speed-current relation | — |
| 9 | $V_a = \alpha V_s$ (chopper) | Average armature voltage with duty cycle $\alpha$ | V |
| 10 | $V_a = (V_m/\pi)(1 + \cos \alpha)$ | Single-phase fully-controlled bridge average voltage | V |
| 11 | $V_a = (3\sqrt{3} V_m/\pi) \cos \alpha$ | Three-phase fully-controlled bridge average voltage | V |
| 12 | $\omega_m = 2\pi N / 60$ | Conversion between rpm and rad/s | rad/s |
| 13 | $P_{conv} = T_e \omega_m$ | Developed (air-gap) power in W | W |
| 14 | $\eta_{mech} = N_{NL}/N_{FL}$ | Mechanical speed regulation ratio | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Equation (10) and (11) note:** $\alpha$ is the firing angle of the SCRs (0 to $\pi$ for full-control). These appear frequently in KTU Part B questions (14 marks) on converter-fed DC drives.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Methods

- **Rolling mills (steel plants):** Ward-Leonard system historically used armature voltage control with a separate DC generator — eliminated by static rectifiers/choppers in modern plants.
- **Electric locomotives (Indian Railways):** Series–parallel grouping + chopper control, transitioning to 3-phase AC drives.
- **Battery electric vehicles:** Chopper-fed DC series motor (older models like Mahindra Reva) → now mostly BLDC/PMSM, but the DC chopper principle is still taught in KTU.
- **Paper mills and textile mills:** Ward-Leonard and dual-converter systems for precise tension control.
]<]minimax[>[</section2>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation: Speed Equation from First Principles

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the armature circuit.**
For any DC motor, the supply voltage equals the back-EMF plus the armature ohmic drop:

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the back-EMF.**
This is the EMF available to drive the rotation:

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 3 — Express back-EMF in terms of speed and flux.**
Faraday's law for a rotating armature gives the induced EMF proportional to flux and speed:

$$
E_b = k \Phi N
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for $E_b$.**

$$
k \Phi N = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for the speed $N$.**

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**
Speed $N$ is increased by:
- Increasing $V$ (armature voltage control),
- Decreasing $R_a$ (impractical, fixed by design),
- Decreasing $\Phi$ (field flux / field weakening control).

This single equation **unifies** all four methods in Module 4.

## 3.2 Derivation: Speed of a Shunt Motor under Armature Voltage Control

**Step 1 — In a shunt motor, the field is connected across a near-constant voltage, so $\Phi \approx \Phi_{rated} = $ constant.**

**Step 2 — Apply the master equation.**

$$
N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

**Step 3 — At no-load, $I_a \to 0$ and the no-load speed is:**

$$
N_{0} = \frac{V_a}{k \Phi}
$$

**Step 4 — At full-load, $I_a = I_{a,FL}$ and:**

$$
N_{FL} = \frac{V_a - I_{a,FL} R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

**Step 5 — The drop in speed from no-load to full-load (speed regulation) is:**

$$
\Delta N = N_0 - N_{FL} = \frac{I_{a,FL} R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

**Step 6 — In terms of the no-load speed, the regulation ratio is:**

$$
\frac{\Delta N}{N_0} = \frac{I_{a,FL} R_a}{V_a}
$$

This shows that a shunt motor has intrinsically **good speed regulation** — a key reason it is preferred where constant speed is required.

## 3.3 Derivation: Field Weakening in a Shunt Motor (Constant-Power Region)

**Step 1 — Hold the armature voltage at rated value $V_{rated}$ and let the field current $I_f$ be reduced below rated $I_{f,rated}$, so $\Phi < \Phi_{rated}$.**

**Step 2 — The master speed equation becomes:**

$$
N = \frac{V_{rated} - I_a R_a}{k \Phi} \approx \frac{V_{rated}}{k \Phi} \quad (\text{ignoring the small } I_a R_a \text{ drop})
$$

**Step 3 — Expressing $N$ in terms of the base speed $N_0$ (at $\Phi = \Phi_{rated}$):**

$$
\frac{N}{N_0} = \frac{\Phi_{rated}}{\Phi}
$$

**Step 4 — Power developed in the air gap:**

$$
P = T_e \omega_m = (k \Phi I_a)(\omega_m)
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $\omega_m \propto 1/\Phi$ (from step 2):**

$$
P = (k \Phi I_a) \cdot \frac{C}{\Phi} = C \, k \, I_a = \text{constant (for constant } I_a \text{)}
$$

Hence, the **constant-power region** is established.

## 3.4 Derivation: Speed of a Series Motor in the Linear Magnetic Region

**Step 1 — In the unsaturated region, flux is proportional to field current, which equals armature current:**

$$
\Phi = K_{\Phi} I_a
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the master speed equation:**

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k (K_{\Phi} I_a)}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
N = \frac{V}{k K_{\Phi} I_a} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k K_{\Phi}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define machine constants $A = V / (k K_{\Phi})$ and $B = (R_a + R_{se})/(k K_{\Phi})$:**

$$
N = \frac{A}{I_a} - B
$$

**Step 5 — This is the famous **hyperbolic** N–I characteristic of a series motor. As $I_a \to \infty$ (short-circuit / stalled), $N \to -B$ (theoretically negative — physically the motor stalls). As $I_a \to 0$ (no load), $N \to \infty$ — hence the **danger of no-load operation** (mechanical destruction).**

## 3.5 Derivation: Chopper-Average Voltage for a Buck Converter

**Step 1 — A Class-A chopper feeds the DC motor from a fixed supply $V_s$ via a controllable switch (IGBT/MOSFET).**

**Step 2 — When the switch is ON for time $T_{on}$, the motor sees $V_s$. When OFF for $T_{off}$, the motor sees 0 V (with a freewheeling diode carrying the current).**

**Step 3 — The average armature voltage over one switching period $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$ is:**

$$
V_a = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral explicitly:**

$$
V_a = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Carry out the integration:**

$$
V_a = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s T_{on} + 0 \right] = \frac{T_{on}}{T} V_s
$$

**Step 6 — Define the duty cycle $\alpha = T_{on}/T$ (some textbooks use $D$ or $k$):**

$$
\boxed{\,V_a = \alpha V_s\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Substituting back into the master speed equation gives chopper-controlled speed:**

$$
N = \frac{\alpha V_s - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

The duty cycle $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ provides **continuous, stepless** speed control from 0 to base speed.

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix: Conventional vs Solid-State Speed Control

| Aspect | Conventional (Rheostatic / Field Divertor) | Solid-State (Chopper / Rectifier) |
|--------|---------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Efficiency** | Low (30%–60%) — power lost in resistors | High (85%–95%) — switching losses only |
| **Control** | Discrete steps / manual rheostat | Continuous, stepless via $\alpha$ |
| **Size & Weight** | Bulky resistors & switches | Compact IGBT/MOSFET modules |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Significant — cooling needed | Minimal — small heatsinks |
| **Maintenance** | High (moving contacts, wear) | Very low (no moving parts) |
| **Response Speed** | Slow (mechanical inertia) | Fast ($\mu$s to ms response) |
| **Cost (Initial)** | Low | Moderate to high |
| **Cost (Lifetime)** | High (energy + maintenance) | Low (energy efficient) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Theory, definitions | Numerical problems, derivations |
]<]minimax[>[</section3>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of DC Motor Speed Control Methods (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DC MOTOR SPEED CONTROL"] --> B["ARMATURE SIDE CONTROL"]
    A --> C["FIELD SIDE CONTROL"]
    A --> D["SUPPLY SIDE CONTROL"]

    B --> B1["Rheostatic Control: Rext in series with armature"]
    B --> B2["Chopper Control: DC-DC buck converter"]
    B --> B3["Controlled Rectifier: 1-phase / 3-phase SCR bridge"]

    C --> C1["Field Weakening: Rheostat in field circuit"]
    C --> C2["Field Divertor: R parallel to series field"]
    C --> C3["Tapped Field: Discrete tap on field winding"]
    C --> C4["Series-Parallel Grouping: Multiple series motors"]

    D --> D1["Ward-Leonard: Separate M-G set for V control"]
    D --> D2["Dual Converter: Back-to-back SCRs for 4-Q operation"]
    D --> D3["Chopper on DC Bus: Common in traction"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chopper-Fed DC Shunt Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUP["DC SUPPLY VS"] --> CHOP["CHOPPER POWER STAGE IGBT SWITCH WITH GATE DRIVER"]
    CHOP --> ARM["ARMATURE WINDING OF SHUNT MOTOR RA AND LA"]
    FLD["FIELD WINDING FROM DC SUPPLY"] --> MTR["EXCITATION FLUX PHI"]
    MTR --> ARM
    ARM --> FWD["FREEWHEELING DIODE PATH DURING TOFF"]
    FWD --> CHOP
    ARM --> SH["SHUNT CURRENT SENSOR FOR IA FEEDBACK"]
    SH --> CTRL["CONTROL UNIT GENERATES DUTY CYCLE ALPHA"]
    SPD["SPEED SENSOR TACHOMETER OR ENCODER"] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> CHOP
    LOAD["MECHANICAL LOAD"] -.-> ARM
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Three-Phase Converter-Fed DC Series Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AC3["3-PHASE AC MAINS 415V 50HZ"] --> RECT["3-PHASE FULLY CONTROLLED SCR BRIDGE"]
    CTRL["FIRING ANGLE ALPHA GENERATED BY GATE PULSE GENERATOR"] --> RECT
    RECT --> DC["SMOOTHING INDUCTOR L AND DC LINK"]
    DC --> SER["DC SERIES MOTOR ARMATURE AND FIELD IN SERIES"]
    SER --> FBK["CURRENT AND SPEED FEEDBACK VIA CT AND TACHOMETER"]
    FBK --> CTRL
    SER --> MECH["MECHANICAL LOAD WITH TACHOMETER COUPLED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Whenever a question asks for a "block diagram of a DC drive", present the **power circuit** (rectifier → smoothing → motor) and the **control circuit** (sensors → controller → firing unit) as two distinct functional blocks. This earns full marks and demonstrates the closed-loop thinking expected at the B.Tech level.
]<]minimax[>[</section4>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**"Why is the speed of a DC shunt motor considered approximately constant with varying load? Justify with the relevant equation."**

**Model Answer:**

The speed of a DC shunt motor is given by:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

In a shunt motor, the field winding is connected directly across the supply, so $\Phi$ is approximately constant (neglecting armature reaction). As load increases, $I_a$ increases, but $I_a R_a$ is very small compared to $V$ (typically **3%–5%** of $V$). Therefore $N$ drops only slightly, and the motor exhibits good **speed regulation**, behaving as an approximately constant-speed machine. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"State any three differences between armature control and field control methods of a DC shunt motor."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Armature Control | Field Control |
|--------|------------------|---------------|
| **Region of operation** | Below base speed | Above base speed |
| **Parameter varied** | Armature voltage $V_a$ | Field flux $\Phi$ |
| **Torque characteristic** | Constant torque region | Constant power region |
| **Flux** | Held constant at rated value | Reduced below rated value |
| **Direction of speed change** | Speed increases with $V_a$ | Speed increases as $\Phi$ decreases |

**(3 marks — one mark each for any three crisp differences)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE full question** from a choice of two. Each question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the speed equation of a DC motor from the basic voltage and torque equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Armature circuit KVL:**

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 3 — Faraday's law for rotating armature:**

$$
E_b = k \Phi N
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for N:**

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

**Step 5 — Note the developed torque is:**

$$
T_e = k \Phi I_a
$$

**Conclusion:** From $N = (V - I_a R_a)/(k \Phi)$, speed can be controlled by varying $V$, $R_a$, or $\Phi$ — these are the three fundamental methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a chopper-controlled DC shunt motor drive. State the expression for average armature voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Class-A chopper** is a DC–DC buck converter that switches the armature voltage at a high frequency. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.2) comprises:
1. **DC Supply** $V_s$ feeding the chopper switch.
2. **Chopper power stage** (IGBT/MOSFET with freewheeling diode).
3. **DC shunt motor** with field held at rated flux.
4. **Control unit** generating gate pulses of duty cycle $\alpha = T_{on}/T$.

**Working:** When the chopper is ON for $T_{on}$, the motor sees $V_s$. When OFF for $T_{off}$, the freewheeling diode carries the armature current, and the motor sees 0 V. The average armature voltage is:

$$
V_a = \frac{T_{on}}{T} V_s = \alpha V_s
$$

Substituting in the speed equation:

$$
N = \frac{\alpha V_s - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

By varying $\alpha$ from 0 to 1, the motor speed is **continuously varied from 0 to base speed**, with very high efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the armature control and field control methods of speed control of a DC series motor. Compare their performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In a DC series motor, armature and field windings carry the same current $I_a = I_{se}$.

**1. Armature (Rheostatic) Control:**
A variable resistor $R_{ext}$ is inserted in series with the armature. The speed equation becomes:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se} + R_{ext})}{k \Phi}
$$

As $R_{ext}$ increases, the voltage drop across it increases, reducing the back-EMF and hence the speed. **Drawback:** High power loss in $R_{ext}$ — $I_a^2 R_{ext}$ wasted as heat. Used in low-power intermittent applications like cranes, hoists.

**2. Field Control Methods:**
- **(a) Field Divertor:** A resistor $R_d$ in parallel with the series field diverts part of the current, reducing $\Phi$ and increasing $N$. More efficient than rheostatic control.
- **(b) Tapped Field:** Discrete taps on the field winding alter the effective turns, changing $\Phi$.
- **(c) Series–Parallel Grouping:** Two series motors are connected in series (low speed) or parallel (high speed). Used in electric traction.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Armature Control | Field Control (Divertor) |
|--------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Efficiency | Low (heat loss in $R_{ext}$) | Higher (no series resistance) |
| Speed range | Wide, below base | Limited, above base |
| Smoothness | Continuous (if variable $R_{ext}$) | Depends on method |
| Application | Cranes, winches | Traction, industrial drives |

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field winding resistance of 110 Ω. At no-load, the motor draws 4 A from the supply and runs at 1500 rpm. At full load, the motor draws 50 A. Calculate the full-load speed and the speed regulation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.5 \ \Omega$, $R_f = 110 \ \Omega$.
- $I_{L,NL} = 4$ A, $N_{NL} = 1500$ rpm.
- $I_{L,FL} = 50$ A.

**Step 1 — Field current (constant for a shunt motor):**

$$
I_f = \frac{V}{R_f} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — No-load armature current:**

$$
I_{a,NL} = I_{L,NL} - I_f = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — No-load back-EMF:**

$$
E_{b,NL} = V - I_{a,NL} R_a = 220 - (2)(0.5) = 220 - 1 = 219 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Full-load armature current:**

$$
I_{a,FL} = I_{L,FL} - I_f = 50 - 2 = 48 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Full-load back-EMF:**

$$
E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 220 - (48)(0.5) = 220 - 24 = 196 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — Since $\Phi$ is constant in a shunt motor, $E_b \propto N$:**

$$
N_{FL} = N_{NL} \times \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}} = 1500 \times \frac{196}{219}
$$

$$
N_{FL} = 1500 \times 0.89497 = 1342.46 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 7 — Speed regulation:**

$$
\% \text{ Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1342.46}{1342.46} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Regulation} = \frac{157.54}{1342.46} \times 100 \approx 11.73\%
$$

**Result:** Full-load speed $\approx$ **1342 rpm**, Speed regulation $\approx$ **11.73%**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $I_f$:** When line current is given, students compute $I_a = I_L$ directly. **Always subtract** the shunt field current: $I_a = I_L - I_f$.
> 2. **Using $E_b \propto V$ instead of $E_b \propto N$:** Back-EMF is proportional to **speed**, not voltage. The voltage equation is $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.
> 3. **Confusing no-load and full-load in regulation formula:** Speed regulation is $\dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}}$, not $\dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{NL}}$. Using the wrong denominator costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring saturation in series motor:** The linear relation $\Phi \propto I_a$ holds only in the unsaturated region. If the problem specifies a saturation curve, use it.
> 5. **Mixing up chopper and rectifier equations:** Chopper uses duty cycle $\alpha = T_{on}/T$. Rectifier uses firing angle $\alpha$ (different symbol, different range). The KTU examiner will deliberately interchange these to test understanding.
> 6. **Skipping units in final answer:** Always write "N = 1342 rpm" or "η = 87%", not just "1342" or "87". Marks are deducted for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 (DC Drives Speed Control)**

- **Master equation:** $N = (V - I_a R_a)/(k \Phi)$ — know this by heart, derivable from KVL and Faraday's law.
- **Three control levers:** $V \uparrow \Rightarrow N \uparrow$ (armature control), $\Phi \downarrow \Rightarrow N \uparrow$ (field control), $R_a \uparrow \Rightarrow N \downarrow$ (rheostatic, lossy).
- **Shunt motor:** Flux ≈ constant → linear speed–voltage relation, good regulation (5%–15%).
- **Series motor:** Flux ∝ $I_a$ (linear region) → hyperbolic N–I curve, **never run at no-load** (will destroy itself).
- **Constant-torque region** (below base speed): use armature voltage control — $\Phi$ = rated.
- **Constant-power region** (above base speed): use field weakening — $\Phi$ < rated.
- **Chopper:** $V_a = \alpha V_s$, with $\alpha \in [0, 1]$; efficient, stepless, compact.
- **Rectifier:** $V_a = (V_m/\pi)(1 + \cos\alpha)$ (1-φ) or $V_a = (3\sqrt{3} V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ (3-φ); firing angle $\alpha \in [0, \pi]$.
- **Ward-Leonard:** M–G set for smooth, wide-range armature voltage control — historical benchmark.
- **Series–parallel grouping:** Two series motors → 2 speed steps; used in electric traction.
- **Field divertor:** Parallel resistor across series field; reduces $\Phi$ without armature losses.
- **Speed regulation formula:** $\% \text{ Reg} = (N_{NL} - N_{FL})/N_{FL} \times 100$.
- **Efficiency ranking:** Solid-state (chopper/rectifier) > Field divertor > Series-parallel > Rheostatic armature control.
- **Practical limit of field weakening:** $\Phi_{min} \approx 0.3 \Phi_{rated}$ to avoid commutation failure and mechanical instability.
- **Tapped-field method:** Discrete speed steps by selecting different field-winding taps; used in traction.
- **Closed-loop DC drive:** Speed sensor + controller + chopper → fast dynamic response, precise regulation.
- **Energy savings:** A chopper drive at half speed can save up to **40%** energy compared to a rheostatic drive.
- **Applications to remember:** Rolling mills (Ward-Leonard), traction (series motor + chopper), battery EVs (chopper), paper mills (dual converter).
- **Common KTU 14-mark problem pattern:** (a) Derivation of speed equation (7 marks) + (b) Numerical on chopper/rectifier control (7 marks).
- **SI units to be used:** $N$ in **rpm**, $\omega_m$ in **rad/s**, $T_e$ in **N·m**, $P$ in **W**, $\Phi$ in **Wb**, $E_b$ in **V**.
- **Conversion:** $N (\text{rpm}) \times (2\pi/60) = \omega_m (\text{rad/s})$.
- **Armature reaction:** Always assume negligible in a shunt motor unless explicitly told otherwise; reduces $\Phi$ slightly and increases speed.
- **Commutation:** A concern during deep field weakening — limit $\Phi_{min}$ to avoid flashover at the commutator.
]<]minimax[>[</section5>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ward-Leonard Control System Using Controlled Rectifiers and DC Choppers - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ward-Leonard Control System Using Controlled Rectifiers and DC Choppers - Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Ward-Leonard Control System Using Controlled Rectifiers and DC Choppers – Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Ward-Leonard Control System** is a classic variable-voltage, variable-speed control method for **separately excited DC motors** in which the voltage applied to the motor armature is varied smoothly from zero to rated value (and beyond) to achieve a wide range of speed control both below and above base speed. In its **modern solid-state form**, the rotating Motor-Generator (M-G) set that originally produced this variable DC voltage is replaced by:

- **Controlled Rectifiers (Phase-controlled AC–DC converters)**, when the supply is AC, or
- **DC Choppers (DC–DC switching converters)**, when the supply is DC (e.g., battery).

This static realization of the Ward-Leonard principle is often called the **Static Ward-Leonard Drive** or **Solid-State Ward-Leonard System**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The static Ward-Leonard system using controlled rectifiers (single-phase / three-phase fully controlled bridge) and DC choppers (step-down/step-up) is a **direct application of power electronic converters** for DC drive speed control. It is one of the most frequently asked topics in KTU University Examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine driving a car and the only way to control your speed is by **pressing the accelerator pedal**. Pressing the pedal is like increasing the voltage applied to the motor. Now imagine that the petrol tank pressure (the "supply pressure") is fixed, but you have a **smart valve** that lets you adjust the fuel flow smoothly. That smart valve is what the Ward-Leonard system does:

- **Old Ward-Leonard:** A separate motor rotates a generator whose field you adjust. The generator's output voltage is your "smart valve."
- **Modern Ward-Leonard:** That bulky generator is replaced by **silicon thyristors or IGBTs** that chop or rectify the supply electronically.

So essentially, you are electronically "throttling" the voltage reaching the DC motor, just like a smart fuel valve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to remember:**
> - Rated DC motor speed: typically **1500 rpm** or **3000 rpm**
> - Base speed $N_b$ corresponds to rated armature voltage
> - Speed range achievable: **20:1 or wider** with proper feedback
> - Power range: from **fractional kW to several MW**
> - Supply frequencies: **50 Hz (India)**, **60 Hz (US)**

### 1.3 Block Diagram Representation of the Static Ward-Leonard System

A static Ward-Leonard system consists of:

1. **AC supply** (3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz) → **Controlled Rectifier** → **DC Motor Armature** (with constant field)
2. **DC supply** (Battery / DC bus) → **DC Chopper** → **DC Motor Armature**

In both cases, the field winding of the motor is fed from a separate constant-voltage source (or a small field converter for field weakening above base speed).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle – Step-by-Step Logic

The Ward-Leonard system operates on the following fundamental equations of a separately excited DC motor:

1. **Armature voltage equation:**
$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

2. **Back-EMF equation:**
$$E_b = K \Phi \omega = \frac{2\pi K \Phi N}{60}$$

3. **Speed equation:**
$$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$

4. **Torque equation:**
$$T = K \Phi I_a$$

### Operational Logic

- The **armature voltage $V_a$** is varied externally (by the M-G set, controlled rectifier, or chopper).
- The **field flux $\Phi$** is held constant for speeds **below base speed** (constant-torque region).
- The **field flux $\Phi$** is weakened (reduced) for speeds **above base speed** (constant-power region).
- The motor speed $N$ varies **linearly** with $V_a$ when $\Phi$ is constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Ward-Leonard system so widely used?**
> Because the same motor can be operated in **all four quadrants** of the torque-speed plane (forward motoring, forward braking, reverse motoring, reverse braking) by simply controlling the polarity and magnitude of the armature voltage. Regenerative braking is naturally obtained when $V_a < E_b$.

## 2.2 Static Ward-Leonard Using Controlled Rectifiers

### Circuit Topology

A **three-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier** (Graetz bridge) fed from a 3-phase AC supply provides variable DC voltage to the DC motor armature. The firing angle $\alpha$ of the thyristors is varied to control $V_a$.

### Average Output Voltage (3-Phase Full Converter)

For a 3-phase fully controlled bridge feeding a highly inductive load (DC motor):

$$V_0 = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \, V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

where:
- $V_m$ = peak phase voltage
- $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \, V_m$ = peak line-to-line voltage
- $V_{LL}$ = RMS line voltage
- $V_m = \sqrt{2} \, V_{phase,rms}$

For a single-phase fully controlled bridge:

$$V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

### Quadrant Operation with Controlled Rectifier

- **Forward motoring:** $\alpha < 90°$, $V_0$ positive → motor rotates forward
- **Forward braking (regenerative):** $\alpha > 90°$, $V_0$ negative → current reverses, energy fed back to AC supply
- **Reverse motoring:** $\alpha$ in region 90°–180° with appropriate firing sequence
- **Reverse braking:** similar with reversed polarity

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip:** For complete 4-quadrant operation, two back-to-back converters (dual converter) are used – one for forward, one for reverse. A single fully controlled bridge gives only 2-quadrant operation.

## 2.3 Static Ward-Leonard Using DC Choppers

When the supply is **DC** (battery, DC bus, traction supply), a **DC chopper** acts as the variable DC voltage source.

### Step-Down (Buck) Chopper – Motoring Mode

The chopper periodically connects and disconnects the DC supply to the motor.

**Average output voltage:**
$$V_0 = \alpha V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T} V_s$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = duty cycle = $T_{on}/T$
- $V_s$ = DC supply voltage
- $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$ = chopping period

### Step-Up (Boost) Chopper – Regenerative Braking

During regenerative braking, the motor acts as a generator, and a boost chopper steps up the motor's back-EMF to feed energy back to the DC source.

**Output voltage of boost chopper:**
$$V_0 = \frac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}$$

### Four-Quadrant Chopper (Class E) for Full Reverse Operation

Two choppers connected back-to-back allow bidirectional armature current and bidirectional voltage → **true 4-quadrant operation**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC motor speed | $N = (V_a - I_a R_a)/(K\Phi)$ | rpm |
| 2 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K\Phi\omega$ | Volts |
| 3 | Torque | $T = K\Phi I_a$ | N·m |
| 4 | Single-phase full converter output | $V_0 = (2 V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ | Volts (continuous conduction) |
| 5 | Three-phase full converter output | $V_0 = (3\sqrt{3}\,V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ | Volts |
| 6 | DC chopper (step-down) output | $V_0 = \alpha V_s$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| 7 | DC chopper (step-up) output | $V_0 = V_s/(1-\alpha)$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| 8 | RMS chopper output (step-down) | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\alpha} \cdot V_s$ | For resistive load |
| 9 | Motor armature current | $I_a = (V_a - E_b)/R_a$ | Amps |
| 10 | Power to armature | $P_a = V_a I_a$ | Watts |
| 11 | Constant-torque region | $N < N_b$, $\Phi = \Phi_{rated}$ | Below base speed |
| 12 | Constant-power region | $N > N_b$, $\Phi$ weakened | Above base speed |
| 13 | Speed ratio | $N/N_b = V_a/V_{rated}$ | At no-load, $\Phi$ constant |
| 14 | Regenerative condition | $V_a < E_b$ | Current reverses |
| 15 | Dual converter firing boundary | $\alpha_f + \alpha_r = 180°$ | For smooth transition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This system is the workhorse of heavy industries. A 5 MW Ward-Leonard drive in a steel rolling mill can hold motor speed to within $\pm 0.1\%$ of setpoint using a simple tacho-feedback loop. Modern CNC machines, paper machines, and mine winders all rely on variations of this principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a 3-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier

### Step 1 – Define the input phase voltages

For a balanced 3-phase supply with phase voltage $v_{an} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$v_{an} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$v_{bn} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$
$$v_{cn} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$

### Step 2 – Identify the line-to-line voltage

The line-to-line voltage $v_{ab} = v_{an} - v_{bn}$:

$$v_{ab} = V_m [\sin(\omega t) - \sin(\omega t - 120°)]$$

Using the identity $\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos(\frac{A+B}{2}) \sin(\frac{A-B}{2})$:

$$v_{ab} = 2 V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) \sin(60°)$$

$$\sin(60°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$v_{ab} = \sqrt{3} \, V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) = V_{mL} \cos(\omega t - 60°)$$

where $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \, V_m$ is the peak line voltage.

### Step 3 – Average output over one firing interval

For a fully controlled 3-phase bridge, each thyristor conducts for 120°, and the average output is computed over a 60° interval:

$$V_0 = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} V_{mL} \cos(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

### Step 4 – Evaluate the integral

Let $\theta = \omega t$:

$$V_0 = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

$$V_0 = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} [\sin\theta]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3}$$

### Step 5 – Apply the limits

$$V_0 = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[\sin(\alpha + \pi/3) - \sin(\alpha)\right]$$

Using $\sin(A+B) - \sin A = 2 \cos(A + B/2) \sin(B/2)$:

$$\sin(\alpha + \pi/3) - \sin(\alpha) = 2 \cos(\alpha + \pi/6) \sin(\pi/6)$$

Since $\sin(\pi/6) = 1/2$:

$$V_0 = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot \cos(\alpha + \pi/6)$$

### Step 6 – Final simplified form

The standard textbook expression (with $\alpha$ measured from the natural commutation point, i.e., $\alpha = 0$ when $\omega t = \pi/6$):

$$V_0 = \frac{3 \sqrt{3} \, V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** When $\alpha = 0°$, $V_0$ is maximum positive; when $\alpha = 90°$, $V_0 = 0$ (used for free-wheeling); when $\alpha > 90°$, $V_0$ becomes negative, enabling regenerative operation.

## 3.2 Derivation: DC Chopper Output Voltage

### Step 1 – Chopper operation

A chopper switch closes for time $T_{on}$ and opens for $T_{off}$. Period $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$.

**During $T_{on}$:** $v_0 = V_s$
**During $T_{off}$:** $v_0 = 0$

### Step 2 – Average output voltage

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_0 \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \left[\int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt\right]$$

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T} \cdot V_s \cdot T_{on} = \frac{T_{on}}{T} V_s = \alpha V_s$$

where $\alpha = T_{on}/T$ is the duty cycle.

### Step 3 – RMS output voltage (for ripple and heating calculations)

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s^2 \, dt = \frac{V_s^2 T_{on}}{T} = \alpha V_s^2$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\alpha} \cdot V_s$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Motor Speed vs. Armature Voltage

For a separately excited DC motor with constant field:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$
$$E_b = K \Phi \omega$$

At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $V_a \approx E_b$:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K\Phi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_0 = \frac{60 \, V_a}{2\pi K\Phi}$$

Therefore, the no-load speed varies **linearly** with $V_a$:

$$\frac{N}{N_{base}} = \frac{V_a}{V_{rated}}$$

At full load, the speed drops by the armature reaction, giving:

$$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** A 220 V, 1000 rpm, separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $\Phi$ constant. Armature current at full load = 20 A. Armature voltage is reduced to 110 V by controlled rectifier.

**Find:** Motor speed at full load with $V_a = 110$ V.

**Solution:**

$$E_{b,\text{old}} = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - (20)(0.5) = 210\ \text{V at 1000 rpm}$$

Back-EMF constant:

$$K\Phi = \frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{210}{2\pi(1000/60)} = \frac{210}{104.72} = 2.005\ \text{V·s/rad}$$

New back-EMF at the same load current (assuming linear magnetization):

$$E_{b,\text{new}} = V_{a,\text{new}} - I_a R_a = 110 - (20)(0.5) = 100\ \text{V}$$

New speed:

$$\omega_{\text{new}} = \frac{E_{b,\text{new}}}{K\Phi} = \frac{100}{2.005} = 49.88\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$N_{\text{new}} = \frac{49.88 \times 60}{2\pi} = 476.4\ \text{rpm}$$

**Observation:** Halving the armature voltage approximately halves the speed — this is the essence of Ward-Leonard control.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | M-G Ward-Leonard | Controlled Rectifier (Static) | DC Chopper (Static) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variable voltage source | DC generator | Thyristor bridge | IGBT/MOSFET switch |
| Speed range | Wide (50:1) | Wide (20:1–100:1) | Very wide (>100:1) |
| Efficiency | 60–70% (2 machines) | 90–95% | 92–97% |
| Maintenance | High (brushes, bearings) | Low | Very low |
| Size & weight | Bulky | Compact | Very compact |
| Noise | High (rotating machines) | Low (mains hum) | Very low (high-freq) |
| Response | Slow (rotor inertia) | Fast (firing angle) | Very fast (PWM) |
| Regenerative braking | Yes (reverse field) | Yes (inverter mode) | Yes (boost chopper) |
| Initial cost | Moderate | Moderate–High | High (but falling) |
| Typical application | Rolling mills, elevators | Industry, mills, hoists | EVs, traction, battery drives |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Static Ward-Leonard System Using Controlled Rectifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC3[(3-Phase AC Supply\n415 V, 50 Hz)] --> CB[Circuit Breaker / MCB]
    CB --> T1[Three-Phase Fully\nControlled Bridge Rectifier\nThyristor Module]
    T1 -->|Variable DC Voltage Va| DCM[Separately Excited\nDC Motor Armature]
    DCM -->|Speed N, Torque T| LD[Mechanical Load\nRoll Mill / Hoist / Crane]
    FS[Field Supply\nConstant DC] --> FLD[DC Motor Field Winding]
    SH[Shaft-mounted Tacho\nGenerator] --> CTR[Speed / Current\nController Unit]
    CTR -->|Firing Pulses alpha| T1
    DCM -.->|Feedback Is, N| CTR
    REG[Regenerative Energy\nFed Back to AC Mains] --> AC3
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Static Ward-Leonard System Using DC Chopper (Battery / EV Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BAT[(DC Battery Pack\n48V to 800V DC)] --> F1[Protection Fuse /\nPre-charge Circuit]
    F1 --> CHP[DC Chopper\nIGBT / MOSFET Bridge]
    CHP -->|Variable DC Va\nPWM Duty alpha| DCM2[Separately Excited\nDC Traction Motor]
    DCM2 -->|Drive Shaft| WHL[Vehicle Wheels /\nTraction Load]
    FS2[Field Supply\nChopper 2] --> FLD2[Motor Field Winding]
    TC2[Tacho / Encoder\non Motor Shaft] --> ECU[Motor Controller ECU]
    ACC[Accelerator / Brake\nPedal Input] --> ECU
    ECU -->|PWM Gate Signals| CHP
    ECU -->|Field Current\nReference| FS2
    REG2[Boost Mode:\nRegenerative Energy\nto Battery] --> BAT
```

## 4.3 Quadrant Operation Chart (Torque vs. Speed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1[Quadrant I - Forward Motoring]
        Q1N[Speed Positive\nTorque Positive]
    end
    subgraph Q2[Quadrant II - Forward Braking]
        Q2N[Speed Positive\nTorque Negative]
    end
    subgraph Q3[Quadrant III - Reverse Motoring]
        Q3N[Speed Negative\nTorque Negative]
    end
    subgraph Q4[Quadrant IV - Reverse Braking]
        Q4N[Speed Negative\nTorque Positive]
    end
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In **Quadrant I (forward motoring)**, the motor absorbs electrical power and delivers mechanical power. In **Quadrant II (forward braking)**, the motor acts as a generator — kinetic energy of the load is converted to electrical energy and either dissipated or returned to the source (regenerative braking). Quadrants III and IV are the mirror cases for reverse rotation.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Closed-Loop Speed Control of Static Ward-Leonard Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REFS[Speed Reference\nNref from Potentiometer] --> SUM[Summer / Comparator]
    TAC[Tacho Generator\nmeasures N] --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error Signal| PI[PI Controller\nProportional + Integral]
    PI --> FF[Firing Circuit /\nGate Pulse Generator]
    FF -->|Pulses alpha| CONV[Controlled Rectifier /\nDC Chopper]
    CONV -->|Variable Va| MOT[DC Motor Armature]
    MOT --> SHAFT[Mechanical Load]
    MOT -.->|Back EMF Eb| CONV
    SHAFT -.-> TAC
```
