# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Ward-Leonard control system. List any two of its advantages.

**Model Answer:**

The Ward-Leonard control system is a method of obtaining smooth and wide-range speed control of a separately excited DC motor by **varying the voltage applied to the armature** while keeping the field flux constant (for below-base-speed operation). In its original form, the variable voltage was obtained from a **DC generator driven by a constant-speed AC motor** (M-G set). In modern static form, **controlled rectifiers or DC choppers** replace the M-G set.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Wide and smooth speed control range** — from near zero to above base speed, with excellent regulation.
2. **Regenerative braking is inherent** — when the armature voltage is reduced below the back-EMF, the motor acts as a generator and feeds energy back to the supply.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between motoring and regenerative braking modes of a separately excited DC motor controlled by a Ward-Leonard system.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Motoring | Regenerative Braking |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $V_a > E_b$ | $V_a < E_b$ |
| Armature current direction | Supply → Motor | Motor → Supply |
| Power flow | Electrical → Mechanical | Mechanical → Electrical |
| Polarity of torque | Same as speed | Opposite to speed |
| In Ward-Leonard | $\alpha$ in 0°–90° (rectifier) | $\alpha$ in 90°–180° (inverter) |
| Chopper equivalent | Step-down chopper | Step-up chopper |

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of a **static Ward-Leonard drive using a 3-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier** for a separately excited DC motor. Discuss its four-quadrant operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 1500 rpm, 50 A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$. The motor is fed from a 3-phase fully controlled rectifier with $V_{LL} = 400$ V, 50 Hz. Compute the firing angle required to obtain a speed of **(i)** 1000 rpm at no-load, and **(ii)** 750 rpm at full-load torque. Assume linear magnetization and field current is constant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Block Diagram:**

A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz AC supply is fed through an MCB to a **three-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier** (six thyristors in a Graetz configuration). The DC output of the rectifier is applied to the **armature terminals of a separately excited DC motor**. The **field winding** of the motor is fed from a separate, constant DC source (or a small controlled rectifier). A **tachogenerator** mounted on the motor shaft provides speed feedback to the **firing-angle controller**, which generates the gate pulses for the thyristors with a variable firing angle $\alpha$.

**Operation:**

The average output voltage of the rectifier is:

$$V_0 = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

where $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{phase,rms} = \sqrt{2}(V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}) = \sqrt{2/3} \cdot V_{LL}$.

By varying $\alpha$ from 0° to 180°, $V_0$ varies from positive maximum through zero to negative maximum.

**Four-Quadrant Operation:**

To obtain full four-quadrant operation, **two back-to-back fully controlled converters** (a **dual converter**) are used:
- **Converter 1** (forward): provides positive $V_0$ when $\alpha_f < 90°$, negative $V_0$ when $\alpha_f > 90°$.
- **Converter 2** (reverse): provides negative $V_0$ (with reverse current path) when $\alpha_r < 90°$.

The boundary condition for smooth, circulating-current-free transition is:

$$\alpha_f + \alpha_r = 180°$$

- **Quadrant I (forward motoring):** Converter 1 in rectifier mode, $\alpha_f < 90°$, current positive.
- **Quadrant II (forward braking):** Converter 1 in inverter mode, $\alpha_f > 90°$, current negative.
- **Quadrant III (reverse motoring):** Converter 2 active in rectifier mode.
- **Quadrant IV (reverse braking):** Converter 2 in inverter mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that **field polarity must also be reversed** (or armature polarity reversed) to truly reverse rotation. The dual converter reverses the **armature current** while the **field remains in the same direction**. Just reversing the armature voltage is not enough for continuous 4-quadrant operation with a single converter — it gives only 2 quadrants.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- $V_{rated} = 220$ V, $N_{rated} = 1500$ rpm, $I_{a,FL} = 50$ A, $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$
- 3-phase supply: $V_{LL} = 400$ V
- $K\Phi$ constant (field current constant, linear magnetization)

**Step 1 – Compute $K\Phi$ from rated conditions:**

At rated load, $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - (50)(0.4) = 220 - 20 = 200$ V.

$$\omega_{rated} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$K\Phi = \frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{200}{157.08} = 1.273\ \text{V·s/rad}$$

**Step 2 – Compute peak phase voltage:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} V_{LL} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \times 400 = 0.8165 \times 400 = 326.6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 – (i) Speed 1000 rpm at no-load:**

At no-load, $I_a = 0$, so $E_b = V_a$.

$$E_{b,1000} = K\Phi \cdot \omega_{1000} = 1.273 \times \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 1.273 \times 104.72 = 133.3\ \text{V}$$

Therefore, $V_a = 133.3$ V.

From the rectifier equation:

$$V_0 = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = 1.654 \times 326.6 \times \cos\alpha = 540.2 \cos\alpha$$

Setting $V_0 = 133.3$:

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{133.3}{540.2} = 0.2468$$

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.2468) = 75.71° \approx 75.7°$$

**Step 4 – (ii) Speed 750 rpm at full-load torque:**

Full-load torque requires $I_a = 50$ A (since $T = K\Phi I_a$).

$$E_{b,750} = K\Phi \cdot \omega_{750} = 1.273 \times \frac{2\pi \times 750}{60} = 1.273 \times 78.54 = 100.0\ \text{V}$$

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a = 100 + (50)(0.4) = 100 + 20 = 120\ \text{V}$$

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{120}{540.2} = 0.2221$$

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.2221) = 77.16° \approx 77.2°$$

**Results:**
- (i) $\alpha \approx 75.7°$
- (ii) $\alpha \approx 77.2°$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to use $V_{LL} = 400$ V directly in the formula $V_0 = (3\sqrt{3}V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ without converting to $V_m = \sqrt{2/3}\,V_{LL}$. This gives a numerical error of about 13% and **costs 2–3 marks**. Always check: is the formula using $V_m$ (phase peak) or $V_{mL}$ (line peak)?

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **DC chopper-fed separately excited DC motor** as a static Ward-Leonard system. With the help of waveforms, describe the principle of **regenerative braking using a step-up (boost) chopper**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 240 V, 1500 rpm, 100 A separately excited DC motor is controlled by a step-down chopper from a 300 V DC source. Compute the **duty cycle** required to operate the motor at **(i)** 1000 rpm, full load; and **(ii)** 500 rpm, full load. Take $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$. State any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Chopper-fed DC motor — principle:**

In a static Ward-Leonard system using a DC chopper, the **DC supply** (e.g., a battery or a DC link) is connected to the **armature of the DC motor** through a high-speed electronic switch (IGBT/MOSFET). The chopper switches ON and OFF rapidly. During the ON state ($T_{on}$), the full supply voltage is applied to the motor. During the OFF state ($T_{off}$), the motor is disconnected and its current freewheels through a freewheeling diode.

**Average armature voltage:**

$$V_a = \alpha V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T} V_s$$

By varying the duty cycle $\alpha$ between 0 and 1, $V_a$ is varied from 0 to $V_s$, providing smooth speed control.

**Waveforms (Step-Down Chopper – Motoring):**

- Gate pulse: rectangular, width $T_{on}$, period $T$
- Output voltage: rectangular pulses of amplitude $V_s$
- Armature current: rising exponential during $T_{on}$, decaying exponential during $T_{off}$ (continuous conduction for high-inertia load)

**Regenerative Braking Using Boost Chopper:**

When the motor must be braked, the chopper is reconfigured as a **boost converter**:

- The motor's back-EMF $E_b$ acts as the **input** to the boost chopper.
- The chopper inductor stores energy from the motor when the switch is ON.
- When the switch is OFF, the inductor's energy plus $E_b$ is delivered to the DC source at a higher voltage.

**Boost chopper output:**

$$V_0 = \frac{E_b}{1 - \alpha}$$

The motor's kinetic energy is converted to electrical energy and **pumped back** into the DC source. This is **regenerative braking**.

**Waveforms (Boost Chopper – Regenerative Braking):**
- Switch current: pulses during $T_{on}$ (rising), drops to motor current during $T_{off}$
- Diode current: flows only during $T_{off}$
- Output voltage at the supply side: constant $V_s$ (battery voltage) — the chopper boosts $E_b$ up to $V_s$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the direction of power flow. In **motoring**, power flows from supply to motor. In **regenerative braking**, power flows from motor (acting as generator) back to supply. The boost chopper **does not generate** the braking energy; it merely **channels** the motor's mechanical energy back to the source.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- $V_{rated} = 240$ V, $N_{rated} = 1500$ rpm, $I_{a,FL} = 100$ A, $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$
- DC supply: $V_s = 300$ V
- Step-down chopper, $V_a = \alpha V_s$

**Assumption:** Linear magnetization (constant $K\Phi$ at all speeds).

**Step 1 – Compute $K\Phi$ at rated conditions:**

$$E_{b,rated} = V_{rated} - I_{a,FL} R_a = 240 - (100)(0.2) = 240 - 20 = 220\ \text{V}$$

$$\omega_{rated} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$K\Phi = \frac{220}{157.08} = 1.4006\ \text{V·s/rad}$$

**Step 2 – (i) Speed = 1000 rpm, full load:**

$$\omega_{1000} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$E_{b,1000} = K\Phi \cdot \omega_{1000} = 1.4006 \times 104.72 = 146.69\ \text{V}$$

$$V_{a,1000} = E_b + I_a R_a = 146.69 + (100)(0.2) = 146.69 + 20 = 166.69\ \text{V}$$

$$\alpha_{1000} = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = \frac{166.69}{300} = 0.5556 \approx 0.556$$

**Step 3 – (ii) Speed = 500 rpm, full load:**

$$\omega_{500} = \frac{2\pi \times 500}{60} = 52.36\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$E_{b,500} = 1.4006 \times 52.36 = 73.34\ \text{V}$$

$$V_{a,500} = 73.34 + 20 = 93.34\ \text{V}$$

$$\alpha_{500} = \frac{93.34}{300} = 0.3111 \approx 0.311$$

**Results:**
- (i) Duty cycle $\alpha = 0.556$ (or **55.6%**)
- (ii) Duty cycle $\alpha = 0.311$ (or **31.1%**)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common mistake is to use $I_a = 0$ (no-load) in the voltage equation. The problem explicitly says "full load," so use $I_{a,FL} = 100$ A. Marks will be deducted for ignoring the armature drop term $I_a R_a$.

---

## 5.3 Real-World Applications of Ward-Leonard Drives (KTU-relevant Industrial Examples)

| # | Industry / Application | Why Ward-Leonard? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rolling mills (steel)** | Very wide speed range, high starting torque, regenerative braking during deceleration between passes |
| 2 | **Paper mills** | Smooth speed regulation over a 10:1 range is critical for paper quality |
| 3 | **Mine winders** | Precise control during lowering (regenerative) and hoisting (motoring) of heavy loads |
| 4 | **Cranes & hoists** | Frequent start/stop, need regenerative braking when lowering load |
| 5 | **Elevators / lifts** | Smooth acceleration/deceleration, energy recovery in modern regenerative elevators |
| 6 | **Electric traction (locomotives, trams)** | Battery / DC line-fed chopper drives give high efficiency, regenerative braking |
| 7 | **Electric vehicles (EVs)** | Chopper-fed DC traction motors; modern versions use BLDC/PMSM but the Ward-Leonard principle persists in the inverter-fed drives |
| 8 | **Machine tools (lathes, milling)** | Infinitely variable speed for optimal cutting |
| 9 | **Printing presses** | Synchronized multi-motor drives with precise speed matching |
| 10 | **Textile mills** | Smooth speed control for yarn quality |
| 11 | **Cable laying / winding** | Constant tension winding requires precise torque/speed control |
| 12 | **Battery-operated industrial trucks** | Forklifts, automated guided vehicles (AGVs) use chopper-fed DC drives |

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Ward-Leonard System**

- [x] **Ward-Leonard system = variable armature voltage** control of separately excited DC motor.
- [x] **Original form:** M-G set (AC motor + DC generator). **Modern form:** Controlled rectifier or DC chopper.
- [x] **Speed equation:** $N = (V_a - I_a R_a)/(K\Phi)$ — speed ∝ $V_a$ when $\Phi$ is constant.
- [x] **Below base speed:** constant $\Phi$, variable $V_a$ → **constant-torque region**.
- [x] **Above base speed:** rated $V_a$, weakened $\Phi$ → **constant-power region**.
- [x] **3-phase full converter output:** $V_0 = (3\sqrt{3} V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$, where $V_m = \sqrt{2/3}\,V_{LL}$.
- [x] **Single-phase full converter output:** $V_0 = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$.
- [x] **Step-down chopper output:** $V_0 = \alpha V_s$, where $\alpha$ is duty cycle.
- [x] **Step-up chopper output:** $V_0 = V_s/(1-\alpha)$ — used in regenerative braking.
- [x] **Regenerative condition:** $V_a < E_b$ — current reverses, energy fed back to supply.
- [x] **Four-quadrant operation:** Needs **dual converter** (controlled rectifier) or **two-quadrant / four-quadrant chopper**.
- [x] **Firing-angle boundary for smooth dual-converter operation:** $\alpha_f + \alpha_r = 180°$.
- [x] **Ward-Leonard advantages:** wide speed range, smooth control, inherent regenerative braking, good regulation.
- [x] **Ward-Leonard disadvantages (M-G):** low efficiency (~65%), bulky, high maintenance, noisy.
- [x] **Static Ward-Leonard advantages:** high efficiency (>90%), compact, fast response, low maintenance.
- [x] **Typical applications:** rolling mills, paper mills, mine winders, cranes, elevators, EVs, machine tools, printing presses.
- [x] **Modern evolution:** Ward-Leonard principle survives in **VFD-fed induction motors** and **PMSM drives** — the variable-voltage-variable-frequency (VVVF) idea is a direct descendant.
- [x] **Key exam formula traps:** (1) Use $V_m$ (phase peak) correctly, (2) Remember $I_a R_a$ drop, (3) Don't confuse $\alpha$ (firing angle) with $\alpha$ (duty cycle) — same symbol, different meaning!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Control of Three Phase Induction Motor - Voltage Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speed Control of Three Phase Induction Motor - Voltage Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stator Voltage Control** is a method of speed control of a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor in which the **stator (input) voltage** is varied while the **supply frequency is held constant**. Because the synchronous speed $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ depends only on frequency and poles, $N_s$ remains fixed, and the rotor speed is reduced by allowing the motor to operate at a **higher slip**. The method relies on the fact that the air-gap torque of an induction motor is proportional to the **square of the stator voltage**.

In a three-phase induction motor, the torque developed is governed by the approximate relation:

$$T \;\propto\; \frac{V_1^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}}{ \left( R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2 }$$

When $V_1$ is reduced, the available torque at any given slip $s$ falls in proportion to $V_1^2$, so the load line intersects the torque-slip curve at a **higher value of slip**, giving a **lower running speed**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Riding a Bicycle Up a Slope**
> Imagine you are cycling up a small hill. If you keep pedalling at the same cadence (frequency is constant), but you are riding a heavier, weaker rider (i.e., the **load torque is fixed**), then to climb a steeper grade you must slow down. Voltage control works the same way: the **slope (load) is fixed**, and by **reducing the push (voltage)**, the motor cannot maintain its no-load speed, so it slips more, and the shaft slows down.

The synchronous speed is the "speed limit" set by the road (frequency), and the rotor is always chasing that limit. Reducing the stator voltage reduces the "muscle power" the motor can deliver, so for a given mechanical load, the rotor must slip further behind to extract the torque it needs.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Slip $s$**: dimensionless, defined as $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$
- **Synchronous speed $N_s$** in **rpm**: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$
- **Slip at maximum torque $s_m$**: $s_m = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ — **independent of $V_1$**
- **Maximum torque $T_{max}$**: $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ — **proportional to $V_1^2$**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** In stator voltage control, $s_m$ does **not** change, but $T_{max}$ decreases rapidly as $V_1$ is reduced. The control range is therefore **narrow and limited to the high-slip (unstable) region** of the torque-slip curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Speed Equation — Why Rotor Speed Cannot Be Set Directly

The speed of an induction motor is given by:

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = \frac{120 f}{P} (1 - s)$$

Since $f$ and $P$ are practically fixed for a given motor, **speed control must be implemented by varying $s$**. Three broad approaches exist:

1. **Vary the voltage $V_1$** (this module) → increases $s$
2. **Vary the frequency $f$** ($V/f$ control) → varies $N_s$
3. **Vary the rotor resistance $R_2$** (wound rotor only) → changes $s_m$

### 2.2 Torque–Slip Curve Behaviour Under Voltage Reduction

When stator voltage is reduced from $V_1$ to $V_1'$:

| Quantity | Effect of reducing $V_1$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Synchronous speed $N_s$ | **Unchanged** (depends only on $f, P$) |
| Slip at max torque $s_m$ | **Unchanged** |
| Maximum torque $T_{max}$ | Decreases as $V_1^2$ |
| Starting torque $T_{st}$ | Decreases as $V_1^2$ |
| No-load speed $N_{0}$ | Practically unchanged |
| Operating slip $s_{op}$ (for fan-type load) | **Increases** |
| Operating speed $N_r$ | **Decreases** |

### 2.3 Operating Region — Why Voltage Control Has a Narrow Range

- For a **constant-torque load**, voltage control is **practically impossible** because reducing $V_1$ reduces $T_{max}$ below the load torque and the motor stalls.
- For a **fan / pump-type load** ($T_L \propto N^2$), the load torque grows with speed, and the operating point slides to a higher slip — useful speed reduction is obtained.
- The **stable operating region** for voltage control is $s > s_m$ (high-slip region), which is inherently less stable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always draw torque-slip curves at $V_1$, $0.8 V_1$, and $0.6 V_1$ on the same graph, mark $s_m$ on the slip axis to show it is unchanged, and superimpose the **load line** to identify the new operating points.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Notes | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | **rpm** |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | Rotor speed | **rpm** |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}}{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | Full torque equation (per phase equivalent) | **N·m** |
| 5 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ | Maximum (pull-out) torque | **N·m** |
| 6 | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ | Slip at max torque (independent of $V_1$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T \propto V_1^2$ (at any fixed slip) | Direct proportionality — the foundation of voltage control | — |
| 8 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous angular speed | **rad/s** |
| 9 | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | Starting torque ($s = 1$) | **N·m** |
| 10 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^2$ | Ratio of torques at same slip for two voltages | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 10** is a frequent KTU short-question: *"If stator voltage is reduced from 400 V to 320 V, by what factor does the available torque change?"* Answer: $(320/400)^2 = 0.64$, i.e., torque becomes **64 %** of its original value.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Voltage Control

- **Low-cost drives** for fans, blowers, centrifugal pumps, and small compressors.
- **Soft starters** for smooth acceleration of large induction motors (also serve as inrush current limiters).
- **Energy-saving schemes** for lightly-loaded motors (e.g., in fans where load varies with ambient temperature).
- **Domestic appliance control**: washing machines, ceiling fans (with TRIAC dimmer-style controllers).
- Used where a **narrow speed range (typically 80–100 % of $N_s$)** is acceptable.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Torque is Proportional to $V_1^2$

**Step 1 — Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit.**
Refer all rotor quantities to the stator. The air-gap voltage $E_1 \approx V_1$ (neglecting stator impedance drop at moderate loads).

**Step 2 — Rotor Current Per Phase.**
The rotor branch impedance is:

$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2'}{s} + j X_2'$$

Hence the rotor current referred to the stator is:

$$I_2' = \frac{V_1}{\left( R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s} \right) + j(X_1 + X_2')}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of $I_2'$.**

$$\vert I_2' \vert = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left( R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Air-Gap Power.**
The air-gap power per phase is the power dissipated in the equivalent rotor resistance $\dfrac{R_2'}{s}$:

$$P_{g,\text{phase}} = \vert I_2' \vert^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} = \frac{V_1^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}}{\left( R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Power Developed.**
Total air-gap power for 3 phases is $P_g = 3 P_{g,\text{phase}}$. The mechanical power developed is $P_m = (1 - s) P_g$. The electromagnetic torque is $T = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_m}$ where $\omega_m = (1 - s) \omega_s$. Therefore:

$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_m} = \frac{(1 - s) P_g}{(1 - s) \omega_s} = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s}$$

**Step 6 — Final Torque Expression.**

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}}{\left( R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Recognising the $V_1^2$ Dependence.**
For any fixed slip $s$ and any given motor (where $R_1, R_2', X_1, X_2', \omega_s$ are constants), the only variable is $V_1^2$. Therefore:

$$T \;=\; (\text{constant at given }s) \cdot V_1^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T \propto V_1^2 \;\text{at constant }s$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Slip at Maximum Torque is Independent of $V_1$

**Step 1** — Differentiate the torque expression with respect to $s$ and set $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$.
Let $a = R_1$, $b = R_2'$, $c = X_1 + X_2'$. Then:

$$T = \frac{3 V_1^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{b/s}{a^2 + 2a(b/s) + (b/s)^2 + c^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^2$:

$$T = \frac{3 V_1^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{b s}{a^2 s^2 + 2 a b s + b^2 + c^2 s^2}$$

**Step 2** — Differentiating and equating to zero yields the classical result (after algebraic manipulation):

$$s_m = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 3** — Observation: $s_m$ contains only the motor's **own circuit parameters** $R_1, R_2', X_1, X_2'$. The supply voltage $V_1$ **does not appear**, confirming that the location of $T_{max}$ on the slip axis is **fixed** regardless of stator voltage.

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Torque Proportional to $V_1^2$

Substitute $s = s_m$ back into the torque expression:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_1^2}{\omega_s \left[ R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2} \right]}$$

For a typical motor where $R_1 \ll (X_1 + X_2')$, the approximation $T_{max} \approx \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s (X_1 + X_2')}$ holds, and clearly:

$$T_{max} \;\propto\; V_1^2$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole, $Y$-connected induction motor has $R_1 = 0.5 \,\Omega$, $R_2' = 0.5 \,\Omega$, $X_1 = X_2' = 1.5 \,\Omega$. It runs at full-load with a slip of 4 %. Calculate: (a) the synchronous speed, (b) the full-load speed, (c) the slip at maximum torque, (d) the ratio of maximum torque to full-load torque.

**Solution — Step by Step.**

**Step (a) — Synchronous Speed.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (b) — Full-Load Speed.**

$$N_r = N_s(1 - s) = 1500 (1 - 0.04) = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (c) — Slip at Maximum Torque.**

$$s_m = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 3.0^2}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{0.25 + 9.00}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{9.25}} = \frac{0.5}{3.0414}$$

$$s_m \approx 0.1644 \;\;(\text{or } 16.44\%)$$

**Step (d) — Ratio $T_{max}/T_{fl}$.**

Using the standard formula:

$$\frac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \frac{(a^2 + b^2 s_m^2) \cdot s_{fl}}{b^2 \cdot s_m}$$

where $a = R_1$, $b = \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$, $s_{fl} = 0.04$, and $s_m = 0.1644$.

$$b^2 = R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2 = 0.25 + 9.00 = 9.25$$

$$a^2 + b^2 s_m^2 = 0.25 + 9.25 \times (0.1644)^2 = 0.25 + 9.25 \times 0.02703 = 0.25 + 0.2500 = 0.5000$$

Numerator $= 0.5000 \times 0.04 = 0.02000$.

Denominator $= 9.25 \times 0.1644 = 1.5207$.

$$\frac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \frac{0.02000}{1.5207} \approx 0.01316$$

**Interpretation:** Hmm, this is the ratio at the same voltage. Note that the *true* formula is:

$$\frac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \frac{(R_1 + R_2'/s_{fl})^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}{(R_1 + R_2'/s_m)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2} \cdot \frac{s_{fl}}{s_m}$$

Let me recompute correctly:

$$R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s_{fl}} = 0.5 + \frac{0.5}{0.04} = 0.5 + 12.5 = 13.0 \,\Omega$$

$$R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s_m} = 0.5 + \frac{0.5}{0.1644} = 0.5 + 3.042 = 3.542 \,\Omega$$

$$(13.0)^2 + (3.0)^2 = 169 + 9 = 178$$

$$(3.542)^2 + (3.0)^2 = 12.546 + 9 = 21.546$$

$$\frac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \frac{178}{21.546} \times \frac{0.04}{0.1644} = 8.262 \times 0.2433 \approx 2.01$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} \approx 2.0 \cdot T_{fl}}$$

This is a typical, healthy induction-motor design value (the pull-out torque is roughly twice the full-load torque).

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Voltage Control vs. Other Methods

| Parameter | Stator Voltage Control | V/f Control | Rotor Resistance Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed range | **Narrow** (10–20 %) | **Wide** (5–100 %+ above base) | Moderate (40–100 %) |
| Starting torque | Poor (falls as $V_1^2$) | Good (constant) | **Excellent** (high at start) |
| Efficiency at low speed | **Poor** (high slip losses) | Good | Poor (rotor I²R losses) |
| Motor type | Squirrel cage | Squirrel cage | **Wound rotor** only |
| Cost | **Low** | High (inverter) | Moderate |
| Smoothness of control | Stepped (autotransformer) or continuous (thyristor) | Excellent | Stepped |
| Transient behaviour | Acceptable | Excellent | Acceptable |
| Suitability for fan/pump | **Excellent** | Excellent | Good |
| Suitability for constant-torque load | Poor | **Excellent** | Good |

### 4.4 Mermaid — Conceptual Torque-Slip Curves at Three Voltages

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Axes[Slip s vs Torque T]
        A1["Slip Axis s"] --> A2["Origin"]
        A2 --> A3["Stable Region s less than sm"]
        A2 --> A4["Unstable Region s greater than sm"]
    end

    subgraph Curves[Family of Torque-Slip Curves]
        C1["Curve V1 100 percent  Tmax highest  sm same"]
        C2["Curve 0.8 V1  Tmax lower  sm same"]
        C3["Curve 0.6 V1  Tmax lowest  sm same"]
    end

    subgraph LoadLines[Load Lines]
        L1["Constant Torque Load  poor for voltage control"]
        L2["Fan Load  Torque proportional to N squared  good"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **shape** of all three torque-slip curves is identical — only the **vertical scale** changes (by $V_1^2$). The slip at maximum torque, $s_m$, lies at the **same horizontal location** on every curve. The intersection of the fan-load line (parabola) with each curve occurs at progressively higher slip values as voltage falls, yielding the desired speed reduction.
