## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`
**"Why does stator voltage control give a narrow speed range in a three-phase induction motor?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a three-phase induction motor, the electromagnetic torque is proportional to the **square of the stator voltage** ($T \propto V_1^2$). When $V_1$ is reduced, the **maximum torque** $T_{max}$ decreases rapidly (also as $V_1^2$), while the **slip at maximum torque** $s_m$ remains **unchanged** because it depends only on the motor's own parameters $R_1, R_2', X_1, X_2'$. As a result, the motor can only be operated in the **high-slip region** beyond $s_m$, which is **inherently unstable**. The motor stalls as soon as the load torque exceeds the reduced $T_{max}$. Hence the speed control range is restricted typically to 80–90 % of synchronous speed. **[3 marks]**

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`
**"A 3-phase induction motor has a maximum torque of 2.5 times the full-load torque. If the stator voltage is reduced to 70 % of rated value, find the new maximum torque as a percentage of the original full-load torque."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Since maximum torque is proportional to the square of the stator voltage:

$$T_{max,\text{new}} = T_{max,\text{old}} \times \left( \frac{V_1'}{V_1} \right)^2 = 2.5 \cdot T_{fl} \times (0.7)^2$$

$$T_{max,\text{new}} = 2.5 \times 0.49 \cdot T_{fl} = 1.225 \cdot T_{fl}$$

**New maximum torque = 122.5 % of full-load torque.** Since the fan/pump load torque at this speed is less than this, operation is possible, but the margin is small. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024):** *Answer the following. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **torque-slip characteristics** of a three-phase induction motor for **different stator voltages**. State the relationship between stator voltage and torque developed. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator:
$R_1 = 0.3 \,\Omega$, $R_2' = 0.25 \,\Omega$, $X_1 = 0.6 \,\Omega$, $X_2' = 0.6 \,\Omega$.
The motor runs at full load with a slip of 3 %. Calculate:
(i) Synchronous speed,
(ii) Full-load speed,
(iii) Slip at maximum torque,
(iv) Maximum torque developed (in N·m),
(v) Starting torque.
Assume the motor is star-connected to a 415 V, 50 Hz supply. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Torque-Slip Characteristics at Different Voltages (7 marks):**

- The synchronous speed $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ is **fixed** for a given supply.
- As the stator voltage is reduced ($V_1 \to 0.8 V_1 \to 0.6 V_1$), the torque at any given slip is reduced in proportion to $V_1^2$.
- The slip at maximum torque $s_m$ is **independent of $V_1$**.
- The family of curves retains the **same shape** but is **scaled down vertically**.

A typical curve family should be drawn (described in text here):

- $X$-axis: slip $s$ from 0 to 1.
- $Y$-axis: torque $T$ in N·m.
- Three curves labelled $V_1 = 100\,\%$, $V_1 = 80\,\%$, $V_1 = 60\,\%$.
- All three curves cross the slip axis at the same $N_s$ (mirror of slip axis).
- All three curves have peak torque at the same $s = s_m$.
- Peak torques satisfy $T_{max,100} : T_{max,80} : T_{max,60} = 1 : 0.64 : 0.36$.
- The load line (e.g., fan load: $T \propto N^2$) intersects each curve at a different point — these are the operating points.
- The operating point moves towards higher slip (lower speed) as voltage is reduced.

**Relationship:** $T \propto V_1^2$ at any given slip. **[7 marks: 3 for diagram explanation, 2 for relationship, 2 for stability discussion]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (ii) — Full-Load Speed:**

$$N_r = N_s(1 - s) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.03) = 970 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (iii) — Slip at Maximum Torque:**

$$s_m = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} = \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{0.3^2 + (0.6 + 0.6)^2}} = \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{0.09 + 1.44}} = \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{1.53}}$$

$$s_m = \frac{0.25}{1.2369} \approx 0.2021 \;\;(\text{or } 20.21\%)$$

**Step (iv) — Maximum Torque:**

First, find the synchronous angular speed:

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

Per-phase stator voltage for star connection:

$$V_{ph} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \text{ V}$$

Maximum torque formula:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{(239.6)^2}{0.3 + \sqrt{1.53}} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{57408.16}{0.3 + 1.2369}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{57408.16}{1.5369} = \frac{3 \times 37350.6}{104.72} = \frac{112051.8}{104.72} \approx 1070 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} \approx 1070 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step (v) — Starting Torque (at $s = 1$):**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{57408.16 \times 0.25}{(0.3 + 0.25)^2 + (0.6 + 0.6)^2} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{14352.04}{0.3025 + 1.44}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{14352.04}{1.7425} = \frac{3 \times 8235.5}{104.72} = \frac{24706.5}{104.72} \approx 235.9 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{st} \approx 236 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Note on Voltage Control Application:**
If $V_1$ were reduced to 70 % of rated value, both $T_{max}$ and $T_{st}$ would reduce to $(0.7)^2 = 0.49$ of their original values: $T_{max}' \approx 524$ N·m and $T_{st}' \approx 115.6$ N·m. **[7 marks: 1 each for parts (i), (ii); 2 for (iii); 2 for (iv); 1 for (v) + comment]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain with a **circuit diagram** the operation of a **3-phase AC voltage controller** used for speed control of a squirrel-cage induction motor. Discuss the effect of firing angle on the stator voltage and torque. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4 % and a maximum torque of **1.8 times** the full-load torque. The motor drives a **fan load** ($T_L \propto N^2$). If the stator voltage is reduced to 75 % of rated value, estimate the new operating speed. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) 3-Phase AC Voltage Controller (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description:**
- Each phase of the motor stator is fed through an **anti-parallel pair of SCRs** (or a single TRIAC). Three such pairs are required — one per phase.
- The SCRs are connected between the 3-phase AC mains and the motor terminals.
- Gate pulses to each SCR are derived from a **firing circuit** that determines the firing angle $\alpha$ measured from the respective phase-voltage zero-crossing.
- The firing angle is varied from a controller (potentiometer / microcontroller / PLC).

**Modes of Conduction:**
- $0 \leq \alpha \leq 30°$: **Two-thyristor conduction mode** — at any instant two phases conduct and one is off.
- $30° \leq \alpha \leq 90°$: **Two-three mode** — both two-SCR and three-SCR conduction intervals exist.
- $90° \leq \alpha \leq 150°$: **Three-SCR conduction mode** only.
- $\alpha > 150°$: **No conduction** — motor is disconnected.

**Effect of Firing Angle on Voltage:**

The fundamental RMS output voltage per phase is approximately:

$$V_{o,rms} = V_{s,rms} \sqrt{1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}}$$

As $\alpha$ increases, the conduction angle decreases and $V_o$ decreases.

**Effect on Torque:** Since $T \propto V_1^2$, the torque at any slip reduces in proportion to the square of the reduced voltage. For a fan load, the operating slip increases and the speed reduces. **[7 marks: 2 for circuit, 2 for conduction modes, 1 for voltage equation, 2 for torque discussion]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Known Data:**
- $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $s_{fl} = 0.04$, $T_{max}/T_{fl} = 1.8$.
- $V_1' = 0.75 V_1$.

**Step 2 — Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Full-Load Speed at Rated Voltage:**

$$N_{fl} = 1500(1 - 0.04) = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — New Maximum Torque at Reduced Voltage:**

$$T_{max}' = T_{max} \times (0.75)^2 = 1.8 \cdot T_{fl} \times 0.5625 = 1.0125 \cdot T_{fl}$$

**Step 5 — New Full-Load Torque (load line) at Reduced Voltage:**

For a fan, $T_L \propto N^2$. The new operating point must satisfy:

- $T_L' = T_{\text{motor}}$ (developed torque)
- $T_L'/T_{fl} = (N'/N_{fl})^2$ (fan law)

**Step 6 — Using Torque Equation Ratio:**

The developed torque at slip $s$ is:

$$T = T_{max} \cdot \frac{2}{\frac{s}{s_m} + \frac{s_m}{s}}$$

At rated voltage, $s_{fl} = 0.04$ and $T_{fl} = T_{max}/1.8$:

$$\frac{T_{fl}}{T_{max}} = \frac{1}{1.8} = 0.5556 = \frac{2}{\frac{0.04}{s_m} + \frac{s_m}{0.04}}$$

Solving: $\frac{0.04}{s_m} + 25 s_m = 3.6$.

Let $x = s_m$. Then $0.04/x + 25 x = 3.6 \Rightarrow 25 x^2 - 3.6 x + 0.04 = 0$.

$$x = \frac{3.6 \pm \sqrt{12.96 - 4.00}}{50} = \frac{3.6 \pm \sqrt{8.96}}{50} = \frac{3.6 \pm 2.993}{50}$$

Acceptable root (small slip region): $s_m = \dfrac{3.6 - 2.993}{50} = \dfrac{0.607}{50} = 0.0121$.

**Step 7 — Apply Voltage Reduction:**

With $V_1' = 0.75 V_1$, $s_m$ is unchanged (still 0.0121), and $T_{max}' = 1.0125 \cdot T_{fl}$. The new operating slip $s'$ satisfies:

$$\frac{T'}{T_{max}'} = \frac{2}{\frac{s'}{0.0121} + \frac{0.0121}{s'}}$$

Since the load torque is the fan torque at the new speed:

$$T' = T_{fl} \cdot \left(\frac{N'}{N_{fl}}\right)^2 = T_{fl} \cdot (1 - s')^2 / (1 - 0.04)^2$$

Combined with the torque-slip curve, this requires a numerical/iterative solution. Setting up the equation:

$$\frac{(1 - s')^2}{(0.96)^2} = 1.0125 \cdot \frac{2}{\frac{s'}{0.0121} + \frac{0.0121}{s'}}$$

$$\frac{(1 - s')^2}{0.9216} = \frac{2.025}{\frac{s'}{0.0121} + \frac{0.0121}{s'}}$$

By iteration (try $s' = 0.05$):

- LHS: $(0.95)^2/0.9216 = 0.9025/0.9216 = 0.9793$
- RHS: $2.025/(0.05/0.0121 + 0.0121/0.05) = 2.025/(4.132 + 0.242) = 2.025/4.374 = 0.4630$

RHS < LHS → $s'$ should be **higher** (motor torque must drop to match higher slip on the falling side).

Try $s' = 0.12$:

- LHS: $(0.88)^2/0.9216 = 0.7744/0.9216 = 0.8403$
- RHS: $2.025/(0.12/0.0121 + 0.0121/0.12) = 2.025/(9.917 + 0.1008) = 2.025/10.018 = 0.2022$

Still RHS < LHS. Try $s' = 0.18$:

- LHS: $(0.82)^2/0.9216 = 0.6724/0.9216 = 0.7296$
- RHS: $2.025/(0.18/0.0121 + 0.0121/0.18) = 2.025/(14.876 + 0.0672) = 2.025/14.943 = 0.1355$

Still LHS > RHS — operating point has moved into the unstable region beyond $s_m$, where the equations begin to fail because the motor is on the **falling** part of the curve. This indicates that at 75 % voltage, the fan load cannot be supported at any operating point in the stable region — the motor **stalls**.

**Step 8 — Finding the Critical Voltage:**

For the motor to just support the fan load, $T_{max}'$ must equal the load torque at the operating slip. The minimum value of $T_{max}'$ needed occurs at the load line — since $T_{max}'$ falls as $V_1^2$, the **critical voltage** is found by setting $T_{max}' = T_{fl,peak}$. The fan load at $s = s_m = 0.0121$ is:

$$T_{fl,at\_sm} = T_{fl} \cdot \left(\frac{1 - 0.0121}{0.96}\right)^2 = T_{fl} \cdot (1.0249)^2 \approx 1.05 \cdot T_{fl}$$

So $T_{max}' \geq 1.05 \cdot T_{fl}$. The required voltage ratio:

$$\left(\frac{V_1'}{V_1}\right)^2 \geq \frac{1.05}{1.8} = 0.5833 \Rightarrow \frac{V_1'}{V_1} \geq 0.7637$$

**Conclusion:** At $V_1' = 0.75 V_1$, the motor **just barely stalls**; a small increase in load would cause it to stop. For stable operation, the voltage must be at least **76.4 %** of rated. If the load is slightly less than rated fan load, the operating point is at a slip just below $s_m$ (say $s' \approx 0.012$ — i.e., speed $\approx$ 1482 rpm).

**Final Answer (KTU 2024 expected):**

$$\boxed{N' \approx 1482 \text{ rpm (operating near the limit of stable region)}}$$

Or, if interpreted as the maximum possible fan load that the motor can still drive, the **speed reduction is very small** (from 1440 rpm to ~1482 rpm is actually slightly higher, indicating the motor is on the verge of stalling — meaning 75 % voltage is **insufficient** to support the rated fan load).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *starting torque* with *maximum torque* in the $V_1^2$ proportionality — both follow the same square law, but they occur at **different slips** ($s = 1$ vs $s = s_m$).
> 2. **Do not** state that $s_m$ changes with voltage — it does **not**. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Forgetting** to convert line voltage to phase voltage for a star-connected motor is a **classic 1-mark loss** in numerical questions.
> 4. In AC voltage controller questions, **failing to mention the conduction mode boundaries** (2-thyristor, 2-3 mode, 3-thyristor) costs at least **2 marks**.
> 5. **Wrong units** in the final answer (rpm vs rad/s vs Hz) is a frequent deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ⚡ The synchronous speed $N_s$ is **set by frequency and poles only** — voltage does **not** change it.
- ⚡ **Core equation:** $T \propto V_1^2$ at any fixed slip.
- ⚡ **Slip at maximum torque $s_m$** depends on motor parameters only — **independent of $V_1$**.
- ⚡ **Maximum torque $T_{max}$** is proportional to $V_1^2$ — drops rapidly as voltage is reduced.
- ⚡ Voltage control gives a **narrow speed range** (typically 80–90 % of $N_s$).
- ⚡ Best suited for **fan / pump / blower loads** (torque $\propto N^2$) — **not** for constant-torque loads.
- ⚡ Three practical implementations: **auto-transformer tap changer**, **series reactor/resistor**, **AC voltage controller (thyristor/TRIAC)**.
- ⚡ **Soft starter** is a by-product of voltage control and is widely used to limit starting inrush current.
- ⚡ **Thyristor AC voltage controller** operates in three conduction modes depending on firing angle $\alpha$: 2-thyristor, 2-3 mixed, 3-thyristor mode.
- ⚡ Firing angle is measured from the **zero-crossing of the phase voltage**, not the line voltage.
- ⚡ **Power electronics switches** (SCRs, TRIACs, IGBTs in chopper circuits) have made voltage control smoother and more energy-efficient.
- ⚡ The torque formula to memorise:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}}{\left( R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

- ⚡ Typical KTU derivations: show that $s_m = R_2'/\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ by differentiating $T$ w.r.t. $s$.
- ⚡ Typical KTU short questions: ratio $T_1/T_2 = (V_1/V_2)^2$ at the same slip; effect of voltage reduction on starting torque.
- ⚡ Typical KTU long questions: full numerical with 5 sub-parts on synchronous speed, full-load speed, slip at max torque, max torque, starting torque; plus a circuit-diagram question on AC voltage controller.
- ⚡ **Stable operating region** is $s < s_m$ — voltage control pushes the operating point towards this boundary, hence the narrow range.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage/Frequency Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Voltage/Frequency Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Voltage/Frequency (V/f) Control**, also universally referred to as **V/Hz Control** or **Scalar Control**, is the foundational open-loop speed control strategy employed for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors (SCIM) using a variable-frequency Voltage Source Inverter (VSI). 

The technique mandates that the ratio between the stator RMS line voltage and the supply frequency is maintained at a constant nominal value across the operating speed range, thereby preserving the air-gap flux of the machine at its rated value.

Mathematically, the governing scalar invariant is expressed as:

$$\frac{V}{f} = \text{Constant} = \frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** The examiner almost always allocates marks for explaining **WHY** the V/f ratio must be held constant. Your answer MUST link the V/f ratio directly to the air-gap flux ($\Phi_g$) using the standard EMF equation, otherwise the answer is deemed incomplete.

## 1.2 The Physical Root Cause: Why V/f Control is Necessary

To understand *why* we manipulate both voltage AND frequency simultaneously, we must examine the equivalent circuit of an induction motor. The induced air-gap EMF ($E_g$) per phase is governed by Faraday's Law and the machine design equation:

$$E_g = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_g \, K_w$$

Where:
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz** (typically **50 Hz** in India)
- $N$ = Number of turns per phase
- $\Phi_g$ = Air-gap flux per pole in **Webers (Wb)**
- $K_w$ = Winding factor (typically **0.95 to 0.98**)

If the motor is supplied directly from the fixed mains (constant $V$ and constant $f$), the flux $\Phi_g$ becomes a fixed design constant. However, when a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) changes the frequency $f$ to alter the synchronous speed ($N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$), we must simultaneously scale the voltage $V$ in exact proportion. If we increase $f$ while leaving $V$ constant, $\Phi_g$ collapses, destroying the torque-producing capability of the machine.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Pressure" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation for First-Time Learners**

Imagine a centrifugal water pump (analogous to an induction motor) designed to operate at an optimal water pressure (analogous to magnetic flux). 

- The **water flow rate** through the pipe is your motor's **rotor speed**.
- The **pipe diameter** is the **frequency** controlling the rotational momentum.
- The **water pressure** is the **stator voltage** generating the magnetic field.

If you suddenly widen the pipe to increase flow (raise frequency) without boosting the pump's pressure (voltage), the pressure per unit area collapses, and the pump loses its ability to push water against resistance. The pump stalls. 

**V/f control is exactly the discipline of "widening the pipe and boosting the pressure proportionally"** so the magnetic "pressure" inside the motor remains identical whether it is running at **10% speed** or **100% speed**.

## 1.4 Operational Zones of V/f Control

A complete industrial VFD operates the motor in three distinct zones, each requiring a different mathematical law:

| Zone | Frequency Range | V/f Law | Torque Capability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Speed Region** | $0$ to $f_{\min}$ (approx. **0–3 Hz**) | **Voltage Boost (Constant V)** | Boosted starting torque |
| **Constant Torque Region** | $f_{\min}$ to $f_{\text{rated}}$ | **Linear Law: $\frac{V}{f} = k$** | **Rated torque maintained** |
| **Constant Power Region** | $f_{\text{rated}}$ to $f_{\max}$ | **Field Weakening: V held constant** | Torque falls as $\frac{1}{f}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** At very low frequencies (Zone 1), the stator ohmic drop ($I_s R_s$) becomes a significant fraction of the applied voltage and cannot be ignored. The drive firmware injects an extra "voltage boost" (typically **2% to 10%** of rated voltage) to compensate for this $IR$ drop and maintain flux. This boost is the reason why you see a non-linear "kink" in the V/f curve near the origin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operating Regions — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### Region I: Voltage Boost Region (0 Hz to ~3 Hz)

When an induction motor operates at very low frequencies (approaching zero), the synchronous speed $N_s$ is extremely low. The back-EMF generated in the stator ($E_g = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_g K_w$) becomes negligibly small because it is directly proportional to $f$. 

Consequently, almost the entire applied stator voltage is dropped across the stator resistance:
$$V_s \approx I_s R_s + j I_s X_s$$

Since $X_s = 2\pi f L_s \to 0$ as $f \to 0$, the resistive drop dominates and degrades the flux. To restore rated flux, the VFD deliberately injects a constant offset voltage $V_{\text{boost}}$ (often user-programmable in industrial drives like the **ABB ACS150**, **Siemens SINAMICS V20**, or **Delta MS300**). 

The modified V/f characteristic becomes:
$$V_s(f) = V_{\text{boost}} + k \cdot f$$

### Region II: Constant Flux / Constant Torque Region ($f_{\min}$ to $f_{\text{rated}}$)

This is the heart of V/f control. Both $V$ and $f$ are varied in strict linear proportionality:
$$V_s = k \cdot f, \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}$$

**Why does this preserve torque?** The electromagnetic torque of an induction motor in the linear magnetic regime is:
$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot \frac{R_r'}{s}}{\left( R_s + \frac{R_r'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

Because $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \frac{4\pi f}{P}$ is proportional to $f$, and $V_s$ is also proportional to $f$, the ratio $\frac{V_s^2}{\omega_s}$ remains invariant. Thus, for any given slip $s$, the torque $T_e$ is preserved — hence "**constant torque region**".

### Region III: Field Weakening / Constant Power Region ($f > f_{\text{rated}}$)

The stator voltage cannot be increased beyond the rated voltage $V_{\text{rated}}$ because of the **DC bus voltage limitation** (the DC bus is rectified from the AC mains and is approximately $V_{DC} = \sqrt{2} \times V_{LL}$). Therefore, beyond base frequency $f_{\text{rated}}$, the voltage is **clamped** at $V_{\text{rated}}$ while the frequency continues to increase.

The flux now begins to fall inversely with frequency:
$$\Phi_g \propto \frac{V}{f} \propto \frac{1}{f}$$

Substituting this into the torque equation yields:
$$T_e \propto \Phi_g^2 \propto \frac{1}{f^2}$$

However, because the power output $P = T_e \cdot \omega_m \propto T_e \cdot f$ remains roughly constant in this region, the drive is said to operate in the **constant power region**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Mandatory)

> [!NOTE]
> **Important LaTeX Encoding Note:** All absolute-value or divider bars inside the table below use the LaTeX command `\vert` instead of the literal keyboard pipe `\vert` to ensure the markdown table renders cleanly without breaking column alignment.

| # | Formula / Identity | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Sets the no-load speed ceiling |
| 2 | Synchronous Angular Velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | Used in torque equations |
| 3 | Air-Gap EMF (per phase) | $E_g = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_g \, K_w$ | Proves $V/f$ invariance preserves $\Phi_g$ |
| 4 | V/f Invariant | $\dfrac{V}{f} = k = \dfrac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}$ | **Core control law** |
| 5 | Low-Frequency Boost Law | $V_s(f) = V_{\text{boost}} + k f$ | Compensates $I_s R_s$ drop near zero Hz |
| 6 | Mechanical Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Determines operating point on Torque-Slip curve |
| 7 | Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \, \frac{R_r'}{s}}{\left( R_s + \frac{R_r'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$ | Torque at any slip $s$ |
| 8 | Pull-Out (Maximum) Torque | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2}{2 \, (R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2})}$ | Determines overload margin |
| 9 | Slip at Pull-Out | $s_{\max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}}$ | Slip giving $T_{\max}$ |
| 10 | Air-Gap Power | $P_g = \dfrac{T_e \cdot \omega_s}{1}$ | Power crossing the air gap |
| 11 | Rotor Copper Loss | $P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g$ | Slip losses — critical in V/f drives |
| 12 | Shaft Power | $P_{\text{shaft}} = (1-s) \, P_g$ | Useful mechanical output |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

V/f control is the **workhorse** of modern industry. It is deployed in:

1. **HVAC systems** in commercial buildings (fan and pump control) — typically operating in the constant torque region.
2. **Conveyor belts** in packaging and bottling plants — where soft-start capability eliminates mechanical shock.
3. **Elevators and escalators** — where smooth acceleration profiles are mandatory.
4. **Machine tool spindle drives** in the low-to-medium power range.
5. **Water and wastewater treatment pumps** — V/f drives save up to **50% energy** compared to throttle-valve flow control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why V/f control is preferred over direct voltage control for fan/pump applications:** Because the load torque of a centrifugal fan/pump varies as the **square of speed** ($T_L \propto N^2$), reducing speed by 20% reduces the load torque to 64% — drastically lowering energy consumption (cubic law: $P \propto N^3$). This is the basis of the **Affinity Laws** used in energy-efficient pumping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Proof That Constant V/f Preserves Air-Gap Flux

We begin with the standard per-phase EMF equation of an induction machine operating at rated conditions:

$$E_{g,\text{rated}} = 4.44 \, f_{\text{rated}} \, N \, \Phi_{g,\text{rated}} \, K_w$$

**Step 1:** Assume the stator terminal voltage $V_s$ is approximately equal to the air-gap EMF $E_g$ (a valid approximation when the motor operates near rated load, since $E_g \gg I_s(R_s + jX_s)$ drops).

$$V_s \approx E_g = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_g \, K_w$$

**Step 2:** Solve this equation explicitly for the air-gap flux $\Phi_g$:

$$\Phi_g = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f \, N \, K_w}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the constant values ($4.44$, $N$, $K_w$) into a single design constant $k_m$:

$$\Phi_g = \frac{1}{k_m} \cdot \frac{V_s}{f}$$

where $k_m = 4.44 \, N \, K_w$.

**Step 4:** Now apply the V/f control law. The drive scales both variables such that:

$$\frac{V_s}{f} = \frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}} = k_{\text{Vf}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Step 4 into Step 3:

$$\Phi_g = \frac{1}{k_m} \cdot k_{\text{Vf}} = \frac{k_{\text{Vf}}}{k_m} = \Phi_{g,\text{rated}}$$

**Conclusion:** The air-gap flux $\Phi_g$ remains locked at its rated design value, independent of the operating frequency. This is the mathematical bedrock of V/f control.

$$\boxed{\therefore \quad \Phi_g = \Phi_{g,\text{rated}} \quad \text{for all} \quad f \in [f_{\min}, f_{\text{rated}}]}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Proof That Constant V/f Preserves Maximum (Pull-Out) Torque

The maximum torque of an induction motor is given by the standard textbook formula:

$$T_{\max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2 \, [R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}]}$$

**Step 1:** Note that the synchronous reactances are directly proportional to frequency:

$$X_s = 2\pi f L_s \quad \text{and} \quad X_r' = 2\pi f L_r'$$

Thus the sum reactance becomes:

$$X_s + X_r' = 2\pi f (L_s + L_r') = X_{\text{total}}$$

**Step 2:** Now assume the motor operates in the **high-frequency regime** where $f$ is large enough that $R_s \ll X_s + X_r'$. Under this approximation:

$$\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2} \approx X_s + X_r' = 2\pi f (L_s + L_r')$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the approximation back into the pull-out torque expression:

$$T_{\max} \approx \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2 \, (X_s + X_r')} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{4\pi f (L_s + L_r')}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\omega_s = \frac{4\pi f}{P}$ into the equation:

$$T_{\max} \approx \frac{3 \cdot P}{4\pi f} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{4\pi f (L_s + L_r')} = \frac{3 P}{16 \pi^2 f^2 (L_s + L_r')} \cdot V_s^2$$

**Step 5:** Apply the V/f invariant law $V_s = k_{\text{Vf}} \cdot f$:

$$T_{\max} \approx \frac{3 P}{16 \pi^2 f^2 (L_s + L_r')} \cdot (k_{\text{Vf}})^2 \, f^2$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $f^2$ from numerator and denominator:

$$T_{\max} \approx \frac{3 P \, k_{\text{Vf}}^2}{16 \pi^2 (L_s + L_r')}$$

**Conclusion:** The pull-out torque becomes independent of frequency — it is a **constant** throughout the constant-torque region of V/f control.

$$\boxed{\therefore \quad T_{\max} = \text{Constant} \quad \text{for all} \quad f \in [f_{\min}, f_{\text{rated}}]}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque-Speed Characteristics at Different Frequencies

We can numerically demonstrate how the torque-speed envelope shifts as frequency changes. Consider a 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor with the following parameters:

$R_s = 1.0 \, \Omega, \quad R_r' = 0.9 \, \Omega, \quad X_s = X_r' = 2.5 \, \Omega \text{ at 50 Hz}$

**Step 1:** Evaluate the reactances at any arbitrary frequency $f$ (note: $X \propto f$):

$$X_s(f) = 2.5 \cdot \frac{f}{50}, \quad X_r'(f) = 2.5 \cdot \frac{f}{50}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the V/f invariant to find the voltage at any frequency:

$$V_s(f) = 400 \cdot \frac{f}{50} \quad \text{(assuming 400 V rated line-to-line)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the slip at maximum torque at each frequency:

$$s_{\max}(f) = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + [X_s(f) + X_r'(f)]^2}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the corresponding maximum torque at each frequency:

$$T_{\max}(f) = \frac{3}{\omega_s(f)} \cdot \frac{V_s^2(f)}{2 [R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + [X_s(f) + X_r'(f)]^2}]}$$

**Step 5:** Tabulate the results for a typical VFD operating in the constant-torque region:

| $f$ (Hz) | $V_s$ (V) | $N_s$ (RPM) | $T_{\max}$ (relative) | Operating Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **50** | 400 | 1500 | 1.00 (Reference) | Rated point |
| **40** | 320 | 1200 | ≈ 1.00 | Constant Torque |
| **30** | 240 | 900 | ≈ 1.00 | Constant Torque |
| **20** | 160 | 600 | ≈ 1.00 | Constant Torque |
| **10** | 80 | 300 | ≈ 0.95 | Constant Torque (with $I_s R_s$ droop) |
| **5** | 40 | 150 | ≈ 0.85 | Voltage Boost Required |

The motor is said to exhibit a **family of parallel torque-speed curves** sliding along the speed axis as frequency changes.

## 3.4 Slip Speed and Stiffness of V/f Control

A subtle but important characteristic: although V/f control preserves $T_{\max}$ and $\Phi_g$, the **absolute slip speed** $N_{\text{slip}} = s \cdot N_s$ does change with frequency for a given load torque.

For a constant load torque $T_L$, operating at a slip $s_1$:

$$N_{\text{slip,1}} = s_1 \cdot N_{s,1} = \frac{120 \, f_1}{P} \cdot s_1$$

The percentage speed regulation across the constant-torque region stays roughly the same, but the **absolute RPM droop** scales linearly with frequency. This means V/f drives have good but not perfect speed regulation — typically requiring **slip compensation** (an automatic boost in $V_s$ proportional to measured $I_s$) in precision applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a V/f Controlled VFD

The following diagram illustrates the signal flow inside an industrial VFD implementing V/f control. The architecture follows the classical **rectifier → DC bus → inverter → motor** topology with a closed-loop V/f generator at the control core.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AC Mains Supply 3 Phase 50 Hz 400 V"] --> B["Diode Bridge Rectifier"]
    B --> C["DC Link Filter Capacitor Bank Vdc approx 565 V"]
    C --> D["Three Phase VSI Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor Bridge"]
    D --> E["PWM Gate Signal Generator"]
    E --> F["V by f Profile Generator with Voltage Boost"]
    F --> G["Speed Reference Input RPM or Hz"]
    G --> F
    D --> H["Three Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor"]
    H --> I["Rotating Air Gap Flux Constant Phi g"]
    H --> J["Variable Rotor Speed N r"]
    F --> K["Slip Compensator Optional Feedback Loop"]
    H --> K
    K --> F
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

1. **AC Mains → Rectifier**: The 3-phase, 50 Hz utility supply is converted into a pulsating DC voltage by an uncontrolled 6-pulse diode bridge rectifier.
2. **DC Bus Filter**: A bank of electrolytic capacitors (typically **$2200 \, \mu F$ to $4700 \, \mu F$** rated at **$800 \, V$ DC**) smooths the rectified DC into a stiff DC link voltage of approximately $V_{DC} = \sqrt{2} \times 400 = 565 \, V$.
3. **V/f Profile Generator**: This is the brain of the drive. It receives a frequency command (e.g., $25 \, \text{Hz}$ for half-speed operation) and outputs a proportional voltage command (e.g., $200 \, V$) according to the linear V/f law. It also injects the low-frequency voltage boost.
4. **PWM Modulator**: Converts the voltage/frequency command into high-frequency switching signals (typically **$2 \, \text{kHz}$ to $16 \, \text{kHz}$** carrier frequency) using **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** or **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)**.
5. **IGBT Inverter**: The 6-IGBT bridge chops the DC bus into a synthesized 3-phase AC waveform whose fundamental frequency and amplitude follow the V/f command.
6. **Induction Motor**: Receives the synthesized AC supply and develops constant-flux torque throughout the operating range.

## 4.2 V/f Curve: The Voltage vs. Frequency Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Origin: V = 0 f = 0"] --> P1["Voltage Boost Knee at approx 3 Hz"]
    P1 --> P2["Linear V/f Region from 3 Hz to 50 Hz"]
    P2 --> P3["Field Weakening Knee at f = 50 Hz"]
    P3 --> P4["Constant Voltage Region above 50 Hz up to 100 Hz"]
    P0 -- V boost approx 8 V --> P1
    P1 -- Slope equals V rated by f rated --> P2
    P2 -- V clamped at 400 V --> P3
    P3 -- Constant V --> P4
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The **lower segment** (origin → boost knee) is the **Voltage Boost Region** where the curve lifts off the X-axis due to the injected $V_{\text{boost}}$.
- The **middle segment** (boost knee → 50 Hz) is the **linear V/f region** with constant slope $k_{\text{Vf}} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \, \text{V/Hz}$.
- The **upper segment** (50 Hz → 100 Hz) is the **constant voltage region** where $V$ saturates at $400 \, V$ while $f$ continues to rise.

## 4.3 PWM Inverter Switching Architecture (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDC["Positive DC Bus + 565 V"] --> S1["IGBT T1 Upper Leg Phase A"]
    VDC --> S2["IGBT T3 Upper Leg Phase B"]
    VDC --> S3["IGBT T5 Upper Leg Phase C"]
    S1 --> M1["Motor Phase A Terminal"]
    S2 --> M2["Motor Phase B Terminal"]
    S3 --> M3["Motor Phase C Terminal"]
    M1 --> S4["IGBT T4 Lower Leg Phase A"]
    M2 --> S5["IGBT T6 Lower Leg Phase B"]
    M3 --> S6["IGBT T2 Lower Leg Phase C"]
    S4 --> GND["Negative DC Bus 0 V"]
    S5 --> GND
    S6 --> GND
```

This 3-phase, 6-switch Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) is the industry-standard power topology used in over **90% of low-voltage VFDs** sold globally.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q:** Define Voltage/Frequency (V/f) control of an induction motor. Why is it necessary to maintain the V/f ratio constant?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** V/f control is a scalar speed control technique in which the stator voltage $V_s$ supplied to a three-phase induction motor by a variable-frequency inverter is varied in direct proportion to the supply frequency $f$, such that the ratio $\frac{V_s}{f}$ remains equal to its rated value $\frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}$ throughout the operating speed range.

**Necessity (1.5 marks):** The air-gap flux $\Phi_g$ of an induction motor is given by $\Phi_g = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f \, N \, K_w}$. Maintaining $\frac{V_s}{f}$ constant ensures that the air-gap flux remains locked at its rated design value. Without this, any change in frequency alone would either saturate the magnetic circuit (if $V/f$ is too high at low $f$) or weaken the flux (if $V/f$ is too low at high $f$), both of which severely degrade the torque-producing capability of the motor. Hence, V/f invariance is essential to retain rated torque at all operating speeds.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – December 2023]

**Q:** What is voltage boost in a V/f drive? Why is it required only at low frequencies?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Voltage boost is the technique of injecting a small additional constant voltage (typically **$5\%$ to $10\%$ of rated voltage**) into the V/f profile at very low frequencies (usually below **$3 \, \text{Hz}$**). It is implemented in the firmware of the VFD as an offset term: $V_s(f) = V_{\text{boost}} + k_{\text{Vf}} \cdot f$.

**Reason for Low-Frequency Limitation (1.5 marks):** At low frequencies, the stator back-EMF $E_g = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_g K_w$ becomes negligibly small because it is directly proportional to $f$. Consequently, the stator ohmic voltage drop $I_s R_s$ (which is independent of frequency) becomes a significant fraction of the applied terminal voltage, effectively reducing the air-gap flux and the developed torque. By injecting $V_{\text{boost}}$, the drive compensates for this $I_s R_s$ drop and restores rated flux. At higher frequencies ($f > 3 \, \text{Hz}$), $E_g$ dominates over $I_s R_s$, so no boost is needed and the linear V/f law is sufficient.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a V/f controlled PWM inverter-fed three-phase induction motor drive. Discuss the role of the rectifier, DC link, and inverter sections. (**7 marks**)

**Q (b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole, 400 V squirrel-cage induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_s = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_r' = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X_s = X_r' = 1.5 \, \Omega$ at 50 Hz. The motor is operated on a V/f drive. Calculate:
  - (i) The synchronous speed at 30 Hz.
  - (ii) The maximum torque developed at 30 Hz if the voltage follows the V/f law.
  - (iii) The slip at maximum torque at 30 Hz.
  (**7 marks**)

#### Solution to Part A(a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)

The V/f controlled PWM inverter-fed induction motor drive consists of four major subsystems:

**1. Controlled/Uncontrolled Rectifier (2 marks):**
The 3-phase, 50 Hz AC mains is fed to a 6-pulse diode bridge rectifier (or fully controlled thyristor bridge for regenerative drives). The rectifier converts the AC supply into a fixed DC voltage. For a 400 V line-to-line input, the DC link voltage is approximately:
$$V_{DC} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{LL} = 1.35 \times 400 = 540 \, V \text{ (for fully controlled bridge)}$$
$$V_{DC} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{LL} = 565 \, V \text{ (for uncontrolled diode bridge peak)}$$

**2. DC Link Filter (1.5 marks):**
A large electrolytic capacitor (typically in the range of **$2200 \, \mu F$ to $4700 \, \mu F$** at **$800 \, V$ DC**) is placed across the DC bus to filter the rectified output into a stiff DC voltage. This capacitor also acts as an energy reservoir to handle the regenerative energy during motor deceleration. A pre-charge resistor and contactor arrangement protects the capacitor from inrush current at power-on.

**3. VSI Inverter with PWM (2.5 marks):**
A 3-phase, 6-switch Voltage Source Inverter (VSI), typically built using **IGBTs** (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors) rated at **$600 \, V$ / $50 \, A$ to $1200 \, V$ / $300 \, A$** depending on drive power rating, chops the DC bus into a synthesized 3-phase AC waveform. The IGBTs are switched using a PWM modulator (SPWM or SVPWM) at a carrier frequency of **$2 \, \text{kHz}$ to $16 \, \text{kHz}$**. The modulating sine wave's frequency and amplitude are derived from the V/f profile generator. The V/f profile generator outputs:
$$V_s(f) = V_{\text{boost}} + k_{\text{Vf}} \cdot f \quad \text{for} \quad f < 50 \, \text{Hz}$$
$$V_s = V_{\text{rated}} = \text{constant} \quad \text{for} \quad f > 50 \, \text{Hz}$$

**4. Induction Motor (1 mark):**
The synthesized 3-phase AC is applied to the squirrel-cage induction motor. Because $V/f$ is held constant, the motor develops rated torque across its operating speed range, with the air-gap flux maintained at its rated value. The rotor speed varies linearly with the supply frequency: $N_r \approx \frac{120 \, f}{P} (1 - s)$.

#### Solution to Part A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Poles $P = 4$
- Rated frequency $f_{\text{rated}} = 50 \, \text{Hz}$, Rated voltage $V_{\text{rated}} = 400 \, \text{V (line-to-line)}$
- $R_s = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_r' = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X_s = X_r' = 1.5 \, \Omega$ at 50 Hz

**Step 1 — Phase Voltage (1 mark):**
For a star-connected stator (assumed standard), the rated phase voltage is:
$$V_{ph,\text{rated}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \, V$$

**Step 2 — V/f Constant and Voltage at 30 Hz (1 mark):**
$$k_{\text{Vf}} = \frac{V_{ph,\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}} = \frac{230.94}{50} = 4.619 \, V/Hz$$
$$V_{ph}(30 \, \text{Hz}) = 4.619 \times 30 = 138.57 \, V$$

**Step 3 — Synchronous Speed at 30 Hz (1 mark):**
$$N_s(30 \, \text{Hz}) = \frac{120 \times 30}{4} = 900 \, \text{RPM}$$
$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 900}{60} = 94.25 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Reactances at 30 Hz (1 mark):**
Since reactances scale linearly with frequency:
$$X_s(30 \, \text{Hz}) = 1.5 \times \frac{30}{50} = 0.9 \, \Omega$$
$$X_r'(30 \, \text{Hz}) = 1.5 \times \frac{30}{50} = 0.9 \, \Omega$$
$$X_{\text{total}} = X_s + X_r' = 1.8 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Slip at Maximum Torque (1.5 marks):**
$$s_{\max} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 1.8^2}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.25 + 3.24}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{3.49}} = \frac{0.4}{1.868} = 0.2141$$

$$\boxed{s_{\max} = 0.2141 \text{ or } 21.41\%}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Torque at 30 Hz (1.5 marks):**
The general expression for maximum torque:
$$T_{\max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2}{2 [R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}]}$$
$$T_{\max} = \frac{3}{94.25} \cdot \frac{(138.57)^2}{2 \times [0.5 + 1.868]} = \frac{3}{94.25} \cdot \frac{19201.7}{2 \times 2.368} = \frac{3}{94.25} \cdot \frac{19201.7}{4.736}$$
$$T_{\max} = 0.03183 \times 4054.5 = 129.05 \, N \cdot m$$

$$\boxed{T_{\max} \approx 129.05 \, N \cdot m \text{ at } 30 \, \text{Hz}}$$

**Verification — Compare with $T_{\max}$ at 50 Hz:**
$$V_{ph}(50 \, \text{Hz}) = 230.94 \, V, \quad \omega_s(50) = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}$$
$$X_s = X_r' = 1.5 \, \Omega, \quad X_{\text{total}} = 3.0 \, \Omega$$
$$s_{\max}(50) = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.25 + 9}} = \frac{0.4}{3.041} = 0.1315$$
$$T_{\max}(50) = \frac{3}{157.08} \times \frac{(230.94)^2}{2(0.5 + 3.041)} = 0.0191 \times \frac{53333}{7.082} = 0.0191 \times 7530.5 = 143.83 \, N \cdot m$$

The slight reduction from $143.83$ to $129.05 \, N \cdot m$ is due to the stator resistance becoming more significant at lower frequency (the $R_s$ term in the denominator cannot be neglected at 30 Hz as it was in the high-frequency approximation). This is precisely why **slip compensation** is required in real V/f drives to maintain a flat torque profile.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative Question) [KTU University Exam – December 2023]

**Q (a)** Draw the V/f characteristic of an industrial drive showing all three operating regions. Explain each region in detail with mathematical justification. (**7 marks**)

**Q (b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V induction motor is controlled by a V/f drive. The drive operates at 25 Hz. Determine:
  - (i) The line voltage applied by the drive (assuming ideal V/f law).
  - (ii) The synchronous speed in RPM and rad/s.
  - (iii) The rotor speed if the operating slip is 4%.
  (**7 marks**)

#### Solution to Part B(a) — V/f Characteristic Explanation (7 marks)

**The V/f Characteristic Has Three Distinct Regions:**

**Region 1: Voltage Boost Region (0 Hz to ~3 Hz) — (2 marks)**
In this region, the stator back-EMF is so small that the stator $I_s R_s$ drop dominates, weakening the flux. To compensate, an additional voltage offset $V_{\text{boost}}$ is added. The mathematical law is:
$$V_s = V_{\text{boost}} + k_{\text{Vf}} \cdot f$$
where $V_{\text{boost}}$ is typically **$5\% - 10\%$** of $V_{\text{rated}}$. The boost ensures rated torque is available even during starting at very low frequencies.

**Region 2: Constant Torque Region (3 Hz to 50 Hz) — (3 marks)**
This is the principal operating region of V/f control. Both voltage and frequency vary in strict linear proportionality. The governing equation is:
$$\frac{V_s}{f} = \frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}} = k_{\text{Vf}}$$
The mathematical justification is two-fold:
  1. Air-gap flux $\Phi_g = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f \, N \, K_w}$ is preserved at rated value (since $V/f$ is constant).
  2. Electromagnetic torque $T_e = \frac{3 P}{2} \Phi_g I_r \cos(\phi_r)$ remains at rated value (since $\Phi_g$ is constant and $I_r$ is determined by load).
The torque-speed curves in this region form a family of parallel curves sliding along the speed axis.

**Region 3: Field Weakening / Constant Power Region (50 Hz to ~100 Hz) — (2 marks)**
Beyond rated frequency, the stator voltage cannot exceed $V_{\text{rated}}$ due to the fixed DC bus voltage limitation. Therefore, $V_s$ is **clamped** at $V_{\text{rated}}$, and only $f$ continues to increase. The flux falls as $\frac{1}{f}$, causing the maximum torque to fall as $\frac{1}{f^2}$. However, the mechanical power $P = T_e \omega_m$ remains roughly constant (hence the name "constant power region"). This region is used for high-speed spindle applications above base speed.

#### Solution to Part B(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $P = 6$, $f_{\text{rated}} = 50 \, \text{Hz}$, $V_{\text{rated,LL}} = 415 \, V$
- Operating frequency $f = 25 \, \text{Hz}$, Operating slip $s = 0.04$

**Step 1 — Line Voltage at 25 Hz (2.5 marks):**
Applying the ideal V/f law (neglecting $V_{\text{boost}}$):
$$\frac{V_{LL}}{f} = \frac{V_{\text{rated,LL}}}{f_{\text{rated}}} = \frac{415}{50} = 8.3 \, V/Hz$$
$$V_{LL}(25 \, \text{Hz}) = 8.3 \times 25 = 207.5 \, V$$

$$\boxed{V_{LL} = 207.5 \, V}$$

**Step 2 — Synchronous Speed at 25 Hz (2.5 marks):**
In RPM:
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 25}{6} = 500 \, \text{RPM}$$

In rad/s:
$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \, N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 500}{60} = 52.36 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{N_s = 500 \, \text{RPM}, \quad \omega_s = 52.36 \, \text{rad/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Rotor Speed (2 marks):**
$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 500 \times 0.96 = 480 \, \text{RPM}$$

$$\boxed{N_r = 480 \, \text{RPM}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Many students use the **line-to-line voltage** ($400 \, V$ or $415 \, V$) directly in the per-phase torque equation instead of first converting to phase voltage by dividing by $\sqrt{3}$. **Always convert to phase voltage** when using $V_s^2$ in torque calculations on a star-connected motor.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Students frequently forget to **scale the reactances with frequency** when computing torque at non-rated frequencies. The reactance $X = 2\pi f L$, so it MUST be multiplied by $\frac{f_{\text{new}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}$ at every calculation.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** When asked to "explain V/f control", students often describe the technique but **fail to state that it preserves air-gap flux** as the underlying objective. This omission alone can cost **2 marks** out of the 3-mark short-answer.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the **field-weakening region** with the **constant-torque region**. If the question gives $f > 50 \, \text{Hz}$, the voltage is clamped at rated value — do not extrapolate the linear $V/f$ law beyond base frequency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember for KTU 2024 Exams**

- **V/f control is a scalar, open-loop control technique** primarily used for squirrel-cage induction motors where rotor parameters are inaccessible.
- **Core invariant:** $\frac{V_s}{f} = \frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}} = k_{\text{Vf}}$ (in the constant-torque region).
- **Physical purpose:** Preserve air-gap flux $\Phi_g$ at rated value, since $\Phi_g \propto \frac{V}{f}$.
- **Result of flux preservation:** Constant $T_{\max}$, constant $T_e$ (for given slip), and linear torque-speed characteristics below base speed.
- **Voltage Boost Region (0–3 Hz):** $V_s(f) = V_{\text{boost}} + k_{\text{Vf}} \cdot f$ to compensate for stator $I_s R_s$ drop.
- **Constant Torque Region (3 Hz–50 Hz):** Linear V/f law, flux and torque held constant.
- **Constant Power Region (>50 Hz):** Voltage clamped at $V_{\text{rated}}$, flux falls as $\frac{1}{f}$, torque falls as $\frac{1}{f^2}$.
- **System Architecture:** AC Mains → Diode Rectifier → DC Link Capacitor → 3-phase IGBT VSI (PWM modulated) → Induction Motor.
- **Synchronous Speed formula:** $N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$ — speed control is achieved by varying $f$.
- **Torque formula (per-phase equivalent circuit basis):** $T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot \frac{R_r'}{s}}{\left( R_s + \frac{R_r'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$
- **Slip at pull-out:** $s_{\max} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}}$ — note that $X$ is frequency-dependent.
- **Slip compensation** is an optional enhancement that improves speed regulation by automatically boosting voltage as load current increases.
- **Industrial applications:** HVAC fans, centrifugal pumps, conveyors, machine tool spindles, elevators — anywhere soft-start and variable speed are needed.
- **Energy savings:** For fan/pump loads ($T_L \propto N^2$, $P_L \propto N^3$), V/f drives can save up to **50%** electrical energy compared to fixed-speed throttled operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip Power Recovery Scheme - Using Inverters and AC Voltage Regulators - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT754 - INDUSTRIAL DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slip Power Recovery Scheme - Using Inverters and AC Voltage Regulators - Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slip Power Recovery Scheme (SPRS)** is a *variable-speed control method* for **slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motors** in which the *slip-frequency power* extracted from the rotor terminals is **rectified, conditioned through a DC link, and either fed back to the supply (subsynchronous operation)** or **drawn from the supply (supersynchronous operation)** through an inverter or AC voltage regulator, instead of being dissipated as heat in external rotor resistors.

The system is also historically known as the **Static Kramer Drive** (uses diode bridge + line-commutated inverter) or **Static Scherbius Drive** (uses thyristor-controlled rectifier on rotor side + inverter). The block essentially converts an inefficient rotor-resistance controller into an *energy-recovering* controller.

### Key Subsystems

1. **Slip-ring induction motor** – the prime mover whose speed is to be controlled.
2. **Rotor-side converter** – a three-phase uncontrolled (diode) bridge for the **Kramer** topology, or a controlled (thyristor) bridge for the **Scherbius** topology.
3. **DC-link inductor (L_d)** – smooths the DC current; stores energy as a current source interface.
4. **Line-side inverter** – converts DC back to AC at line frequency; usually a *line-commutated* thyristor bridge (Kramer) or a *force-commutated / PWM* VSI (modern Scherbius).
5. **Step-down transformer** – matches inverter output voltage to the supply line voltage.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a hydroelectric power station where water (representing rotor slip power) falls through different heights (representing slip). In **rotor-resistance control**, this falling water just hits concrete blocks and *heats them up* (energy is wasted as $I^2R$ losses). In the **Slip Power Recovery Scheme**, instead of wasting it, we install a *turbine and generator* at the bottom, capturing the falling water and *pumping electricity back into the national grid*. The amount of water we extract controls the speed.

- When the slip-ring rotor's **slip rings** rotate at a frequency $f_2 = s \cdot f_1$, they produce a three-phase voltage of magnitude proportional to slip.
- A **diode bridge** rectifies this low-frequency AC into DC.
- An **inverter** then pumps this DC power *back to the mains* through a transformer.
- By controlling the **firing angle (α)** of the inverter, we dictate how much of the rotor's induced voltage is "absorbed" — this directly sets the **slip (s)** and hence the **motor speed (N)**.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Air-gap power** $P_g$ in **watts** (W)
- **Synchronous speed** $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f_1}{p}$ in **rad/s**; $N_s = \dfrac{120 f_1}{p}$ in **rpm**
- **Operating slip range:** $0 < s < 1$ for sub-synchronous motoring (typical Kramer)
- **$-1 < s < 0$** for super-synchronous motoring (requires bidirectional power flow — Scherbius with controlled rotor bridge)
- **Transformer turns ratio (a)** is a design constant chosen to match the rated slip voltage.

---

## 1.3 Slip Power — The Central Idea

The induction motor's air-gap power splits as:

$$P_g \;=\; \underbrace{s \, P_g}_{\text{slip power (rotor side)}} \;+\; \underbrace{(1-s) \, P_g}_{\text{mechanical power developed}}$$

- The **slip power** $s P_g$ flows through the rotor circuit.
- In **conventional rotor-resistance control**, this is dissipated in external resistors → **efficiency drops sharply at low speeds**.
- In **SPRS**, $s P_g$ is recovered and returned to the line → efficiency is preserved across the speed range.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A motor driving a **fan/pump load** (torque ∝ speed²) consumes power ∝ speed³. Therefore, SPRS gives the **largest energy savings** for such *variable-torque* loads at reduced speeds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Subsynchronous Motoring (Static Kramer)

### Step-by-step operational logic

1. The rotor of the slip-ring induction motor runs at slip $s$ relative to the synchronous field. The rotor frequency is $f_2 = s f_1$ and the line-to-line rotor voltage magnitude is $V_L' = s \cdot V_{L0}$ where $V_{L0}$ is the standstill rotor line voltage.
2. A **three-phase diode bridge** rectifies this voltage. The average DC output voltage of an uncontrolled 3-φ bridge is:

$$V_d \;=\; \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \, s \, V_{L0}$$

3. This DC voltage drives current through the smoothing inductor $L_d$ and opposes the back-EMF of the **line-side thyristor inverter**. The average DC voltage at the inverter input (referred to the DC link) is:

$$V_{inv} \;=\; \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \, V_t \cos(\alpha)$$

where $V_t$ is the line-to-line voltage on the transformer secondary, and α is the **firing angle (delay angle)** of the inverter, with $90° < α < 165°$ in normal inverter operation.

4. **DC-link equilibrium** (current can flow only when $V_d \geq V_{inv}$ for diode bridge to conduct) gives:

$$V_d \;=\; V_{inv} \;+\; I_d R_d$$

where $R_d$ is the total DC-link resistance. Neglecting $R_d$ (ideal):

$$\dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} s V_{L0} \;=\; \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_t \cos(\alpha)$$

$$\boxed{\,s \;=\; \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos(\alpha)\,}$$

5. Therefore, **speed control is obtained by varying α**. As α increases from 90° toward 180°, $\cos(\alpha)$ becomes more negative, $s$ decreases, and the motor speeds up **toward synchronous speed**.
6. At $α = 90°$: $\cos α = 0 → s = 0 → N = N_s$ (synchronous speed, theoretically).
7. At $α = 180°$: $\cos α = -1 → s = -V_t / V_{L0}$ (theoretically supersynchronous, but diode bridge cannot reverse — practical limit is $α ≈ 165°$ giving minimum subsynchronous slip).
8. The **minimum subsynchronous speed** occurs at maximum allowed α (just below commutation/extinction angle limit $\gamma$):

$$s_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos(\alpha_{\max})$$

where $α_{max} = 180° - γ$ and γ is the extinction advance angle needed for successful thyristor commutation.

---

## 2.2 Power-Flow Equations (Subsynchronous Kramer)

| Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| Air-gap power | $P_g = T_e \, \omega_s$ |
| Slip power (DC link flow) | $P_{slip} = s \, P_g = V_d \, I_d$ |
| Mechanical power developed | $P_m = (1-s) P_g$ |
| Power recovered to line | $P_{rec} = V_{inv} \, I_d$ |
| Rotor copper loss | $P_{rcl} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2'$ |
| Net DC-link loss | $P_{dc} = I_d^2 R_d$ |

Power balance at DC link:

$$s P_g \;=\; P_{rec} \;+\; P_{rcl} \;+\; P_{dc}$$

The **DC-link inductor stores no average energy** (it only smooths the current).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear every year in KTU ESE Module 4 questions.**

| Sl. No. | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = N_s (1 - s) = \dfrac{120 f_1}{p}(1-s)$ | Actual rotor speed |
| 2 | $P_g = \dfrac{T_e \, \omega_s}{1000}$ (kW) | Air-gap power (with $T_e$ in N·m, $\omega_s$ in rad/s) |
| 3 | $P_{slip} = s \cdot P_g$ | Slip-ring power available for recovery |
| 4 | $P_m = (1-s) P_g$ | Developed mechanical power |
| 5 | $V_d = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} s V_{L0}$ | DC output of uncontrolled 3-φ bridge |
| 6 | $V_{inv} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_t \cos α$ | DC input of line-commutated inverter |
| 7 | $s = \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α$ | Slip vs firing angle (Kramer) |
| 8 | $s_{\min} = \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α_{\max}$ | Minimum subsynchronous slip |
| 9 | $η_{drive} = \dfrac{P_m}{P_{in}} \approx \dfrac{(1-s) P_g}{P_g} = 1 - s$ (idealised) | Ideal efficiency at slip s |
| 10 | $P_{slip,\,max} \approx s_{max} \cdot P_{rated}$ | Inverter rating design rule |
| 11 | $α_{\max} = 180° - (γ + \beta)$ | Commutation, extinction, and margin angles |
| 12 | $V_t = a \cdot V_{line}$ | Transformer turns-ratio relation |
| 13 | $I_d = \dfrac{s P_g - I_d^2 R_d}{V_{inv}}$ | Steady-state DC-link current |
| 14 | $P_{shaft} = (1-s) P_g - P_{rot}$ | Shaft output after rotational losses |
| 15 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{(R_{th}+R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th}+X_2')^2}$ | Torque from rotor equivalent (used to plot Kramer characteristic) |

**Where to substitute carefully (pipe-free notation):**
- Absolute rotor current magnitude: write $I_2'$ (no vertical bars).
- Slip magnitude: $s \ge 0$ in subsynchronous mode.

---

## 2.4 Static Scherbius (Supersynchronous + Subsynchronous)

When **supersynchronous operation** ($N > N_s$, i.e., $s < 0$) is required, the rotor-side bridge must also be **thyristor-controlled** (Scherbius topology). The rotor bridge then operates in **inverter mode** (α₂ > 90°) while the line-side bridge operates in **rectifier mode** (α₁ < 90°) — power *flows from line → rotor circuit → mechanical shaft*, supplementing the air-gap power.

**Modified slip equation** (Scherbius):

$$s \;=\; \dfrac{V_t \cos α_1 \;-\; V_{L0} \cos α_2}{V_{L0} \cos α_2}$$

---

## 2.5 Slip-Power Recovery Using AC Voltage Regulators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In some textbooks this is called the **"subsynchronous cascade with AC regulator"** or **"secondary excitation control"** scheme, often used for **doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG) wind turbines**.

Here, instead of a DC-link inverter, an **AC-to-AC converter (cycloconverter or AC voltage regulator)** directly processes the slip-frequency rotor voltage. There are two common topologies:

### Topology A — Rotor-side AC Voltage Regulator (Rotor Chopper Method)

- A **3-φ diode bridge** rectifies rotor voltage.
- A **DC chopper (PWM-controlled IGBT/MOSFET)** switches the DC link at high frequency.
- The chopper duty cycle δ controls the equivalent DC voltage applied to a fixed line-commutated inverter.
- Equivalent effect: slip $s = \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cdot \dfrac{\cos α}{\delta}$.

### Topology B — Cycloconverter (Direct AC-AC) on Rotor

- The cycloconverter on the rotor side allows *bidirectional power flow* without a DC link.
- Used for **very large drives** (MW class) and modern **DFIG wind energy systems**.
- Slip range achievable: $-0.5 \leq s \leq +0.5$ in typical designs.

### Real-world utility

- **Large pumps and fans** (cement mills, irrigation pumps) → high-energy savings at reduced speed.
- **Compressors and conveyors** → smooth start-up and energy-efficient speed control.
- **Wind energy conversion (DFIG)** → rotor-side converter regulates slip power; gearbox stress reduced.
- **Hydro-pumped storage generator-motors** → both motoring and generating modes use SPRS principles.

---

## 2.6 Power-Flow Diagram (Subsynchronous Kramer)

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │       Stator Input = P_in (3-φ supply)       │
   └────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
                        │
              ┌─────────▼─────────┐
              │  Stator Copper +  │
              │  Iron Losses      │
              └─────────┬─────────┘
                        │  Air-gap power P_g
              ┌─────────▼─────────┐
              │    Air Gap        │
              │    Power P_g      │
              └─────────┬─────────┘
                        │
            ┌───────────┴───────────┐
            ▼                       ▼
   (1-s)·P_g = P_mech      s·P_g = P_slip
            │                       │
            ▼                       ▼
   Mechanical Power         Diode Rectifier
   (Shaft + Rot. Loss)             │
                                   ▼
                          DC-Link (L_d, I_d)
                                   │
                                   ▼
                          Line Inverter (α)
                                   │
                                   ▼
                       Transformer → SUPPLY
                       (Recovered Energy)
```

> Efficiency of SPRS at slip $s$ in ideal case ≈ $(1-s)$ — vastly superior to rotor-resistance control whose efficiency ≈ $(1-s)$ *minus* $s P_g$ lost as heat.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Slip Equation for Static Kramer Drive

**Given:** A slip-ring induction motor with standstill rotor line-to-line rms voltage $V_{L0}$. The rotor is connected to a diode bridge, then a DC link with smoothing inductor $L_d$, then a line-commutated thyristor inverter fed through a transformer with secondary line voltage $V_t$. Firing angle = α.

**To derive:** The expression $s = \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α$.

### Step 1 — Rotor Voltage at Slip s

When the rotor runs at slip $s$, the induced EMF in the rotor windings is:

$$E_{2,\,s} = s \cdot E_{20}$$

where $E_{20}$ is the standstill rotor EMF per phase. The line-to-line rotor voltage at the slip-ring terminals is:

$$V_{2s} = s \cdot V_{L0}$$

### Step 2 — Output of the Diode Bridge (Uncontrolled Rectifier)

For a three-pulse uncontrolled bridge (six-pulse for 3-φ), the average DC output voltage is:

$$
V_d \;=\; \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{LL,\text{input}}
$$

Substituting the rotor line voltage:

$$
V_d \;=\; \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot (s \cdot V_{L0})
$$

### Step 3 — Input to the Line-side Inverter (Line-commutated)

For a fully-controlled thyristor bridge operating in inverter mode (firing angle α in the range $90° < α < 180°$), the average DC input voltage is:

$$
V_{inv} \;=\; -\dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_t \cos(\alpha - 180°) \;=\; \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_t \cos α
$$

Note: When α > 90°, cos α is negative, meaning $V_{inv}$ appears as a back-EMF opposing $V_d$.

### Step 4 — DC-Link Equilibrium (No-Load Steady State)

In steady state with negligible resistance and large $L_d$ (ripple-free DC current), the inductor has zero average voltage. Therefore, KVL around the DC loop gives:

$$
V_d + V_{inv} = 0
$$

Substituting both:

$$
\dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} s V_{L0} + \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_t \cos α = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Solving for Slip s

Cancel the common factor $\dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi}$ (non-zero):

$$
s \, V_{L0} + V_t \cos α = 0
$$

$$
s \, V_{L0} = - V_t \cos α
$$

$$
\boxed{\,s = -\,\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α\,}
$$

For subsynchronous operation ($s > 0$) with α in $(90°, 180°)$, $\cos α < 0$, so $-cos α > 0$, confirming $s > 0$.

### Step 6 — Interpretation

- When α = 90°: cos α = 0 → s = 0 → rotor runs at synchronous speed.
- When α = 180°: cos α = -1 → s = $V_t/V_{L0}$ → maximum subsynchronous slip (lowest speed).
- The choice of $V_t$ (transformer secondary voltage) determines the **maximum available slip** without exceeding commutation limits.

### Step 7 — Including DC-Link Resistance (Practical)

If the DC link has total resistance $R_d$ (cable + inductor winding + thyristor on-state drops), then:

$$
V_d = V_{inv} + I_d R_d
$$

$$
\dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} s V_{L0} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_t \cos α + I_d R_d
$$

Solving:

$$
s = \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α + \dfrac{\pi I_d R_d}{3 \sqrt{2} V_{L0}}
$$

The second term is positive (increases $s$) — practical drive runs slightly slower than the ideal equation predicts.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Air-Gap Power and Slip Power Relationship

### Step 1 — Power Crossing the Air Gap

The torque $T_e$ developed at the air gap times the synchronous mechanical angular velocity $\omega_s$ gives the air-gap power:

$$
P_g = T_e \cdot \omega_s \quad \text{(watts, with } \omega_s \text{ in rad/s)}
$$

### Step 2 — Rotor Copper Loss in Terms of P_g

From the rotor equivalent circuit, the total rotor copper loss equals $s$ times the air-gap power (a standard induction-machine identity):

$$
P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_g
$$

### Step 3 — Mechanical Power Developed

Subtracting rotor copper loss from air-gap power:

$$
P_m = P_g - P_{rcl} = P_g - s P_g = (1-s) P_g
$$

### Step 4 — Slip Power

The **slip power** flowing in the rotor circuit (or recoverable from slip rings) is, by definition, the rotor copper loss + the recovered power:

$$
P_{slip} = s P_g
$$

In a rotor-resistance controller, all $s P_g$ is dissipated. In SPRS, a fraction $k$ of it is recovered:

$$
P_{slip,\,rec} = k \cdot s P_g
$$

The remainder is dissipated in the rotor + DC link:

$$
P_{dissipated} = (1-k) s P_g
$$

### Step 5 — Overall Efficiency

Assuming stator losses are small and rotational losses are negligible for clarity:

$$
η_{SPRS} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{in}} \approx \dfrac{(1-s) P_g}{P_g} = 1 - s
$$

vs. rotor-resistance controller:

$$
η_{RR} = \dfrac{(1-s) P_g}{P_g} = 1 - s \quad \text{(shaft out only)}
$$

But in rotor-resistance control, **electrical input is still** $P_g$ (since $s P_g$ goes to external resistors, supplied from stator). So the input differs:

$$
P_{in,\,RR} = P_g + P_{stator\,losses}
$$

While in SPRS:

$$
P_{in,\,SPRS} = P_g + P_{stator\,losses} - k \cdot s P_g
$$

Hence **SPRS gives strictly better efficiency** whenever $k > 0$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Inverter Firing Angle and Minimum Speed

The thyristor inverter needs a finite extinction angle γ (typically 15°–20°) to ensure reliable turn-off. Therefore:

$$
α_{max} = 180° - γ
$$

Plus a commutation overlap margin β ≈ 5°–10°. So:

$$
α_{max,\text{practical}} = 180° - γ - β
$$

Substituting:

$$
s_{min} = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos(α_{max}) = \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos(180° - γ - β) = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos(γ + β)
$$

For γ + β = 25°: cos(25°) ≈ 0.906.

$$
s_{min} \approx -0.906 \cdot \dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}}
$$

If $V_t = V_{L0}/3$ (transformer chosen to give 1/3 rated slip recovery):

$$
s_{min} \approx -0.302
$$

i.e., motor can be controlled down to $N \approx 0.30 \, N_s$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Rotor Resistance Control | Static Kramer (SPRS) | Static Scherbius (SPRS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed range | $0$ to $N_s$ (theoretical) | $0.3 N_s$ to $N_s$ (typical) | $-0.5 N_s$ to $+0.5 N_s$ (around $N_s$) |
| Efficiency at low speed | Very poor (≈ 1 − s) | High (≈ 1 − s) | High (≈ 1 − ∣s∣) |
| Power recovered | None | Subsynchronous only | Both sub- and super-synchronous |
| Rotor-side converter | None (resistors) | Diode bridge | Thyristor bridge (controlled) |
| Line-side converter | None | Line-commutated inverter | Either line-commutated or PWM VSI |
| Starting | External resistors used | Smooth via α control | Smooth |
| Harmonics | None from drive | Significant (12-pulse preferred) | Lower with PWM |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | High |
| Applications | Cranes, small drives | Pumps, compressors, fans | Wind turbines, large MW drives |
| Power factor | Good (resistive) | Poor at low speed (α large) | Better with PWM SPRS |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, Y-connected slip-ring induction motor has a standstill rotor line-to-line voltage of 200 V. The rotor is connected to a diode bridge and a line-commutated inverter through a transformer with secondary voltage 150 V. The smoothing inductor ensures ripple-free DC current. Calculate the operating slip when the inverter firing angle is **(i)** 120° and **(ii)** 150°.

### Solution — Part (i), α = 120°

Using the formula:

$$
s = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α = -\dfrac{150}{200} \cos(120°)
$$

$$
\cos(120°) = -0.5
$$

$$
s = -0.75 \cdot (-0.5) = 0.375
$$

Synchronous speed:

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

Actual rotor speed:

$$
N = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \cdot (1 - 0.375) = 1500 \cdot 0.625 = 937.5 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Solution — Part (ii), α = 150°

$$
\cos(150°) = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx -0.866
$$

$$
s = -0.75 \cdot (-0.866) = 0.6495
$$

$$
N = 1500 \cdot (1 - 0.6495) = 1500 \cdot 0.3505 \approx 525.75 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Observation:** As α increases from 120° to 150°, slip increases from 0.375 to 0.6495, and speed drops from 937.5 rpm to 525.75 rpm. This confirms the **speed-control mechanism** via α.

### Power-Flow Direction (Kramer — Subsynchronous)

- Slip rings → diode bridge → DC link → inverter → transformer → **mains** (slip power recovered).
- Mains → stator → air gap → rotor (mechanical + electrical power).
