# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a Slip Power Recovery Scheme? Mention any two of its industrial applications.

**Model Answer:**

> Slip Power Recovery Scheme (SPRS) is a method of speed control of slip-ring induction motors in which the power available in the rotor circuit at slip frequency is rectified and fed back to the supply through an inverter, instead of being dissipated in external resistors.
>
> **Two applications:**
> 1. Large-capacity **centrifugal pumps** in water-supply schemes — energy-efficient speed control.
> 2. **Variable-speed wind energy conversion systems (DFIG)** — rotor-side power conditioning.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks applications)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** State the expression for slip in terms of firing angle α for a static Kramer drive and explain how speed control is achieved.

**Model Answer:**

> For a static Kramer drive:
>
> $$s = -\,\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α$$
>
> As the firing angle α is increased from 90° toward 180°, cos α becomes more negative, so $|cos α|$ increases, leading to higher slip and **lower speed**. Conversely, decreasing α toward 90° reduces slip and the motor accelerates toward synchronous speed. Thus, **firing angle α is the speed-control variable**.

*(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 2 marks explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the static Kramer drive for speed control of a slip-ring induction motor. Derive the expression for slip in terms of the inverter firing angle. Discuss its advantages and limitations.*

#### (a) **Part (a) — 7 Marks** — Block Diagram, Operation, and Derivation

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram** (text version — sketch in exam):

```
            3-φ Mains
                │
        ┌───────▼────────┐
        │ Transformer     │
        └───────┬────────┘
                │
        ┌───────▼────────┐
        │ Line-commutated │   ← Thyristor bridge (firing angle α)
        │ Inverter        │
        └───────┬────────┘
                │ DC link (L_d)
        ┌───────▼────────┐
        │ Diode Bridge    │   ← Rotor-side rectifier
        └───────┬────────┘
                │ Slip rings
        ┌───────▼────────┐
        │ Slip-ring       │
        │ Induction Motor │
        └────────────────┘
```

**Operation:** The rotor slip rings supply a low-frequency AC voltage of magnitude $s V_{L0}$. The diode bridge rectifies this to DC. The line-commutated inverter then pumps the DC power back to the mains via the transformer. The DC-link inductor $L_d$ smooths the current. By varying the inverter firing angle α, the DC back-EMF $V_{inv}$ is controlled, which in turn changes the equilibrium slip $s$ and hence the motor speed.

**Derivation (see Section 3.1):**

Applying KVL around the DC link at steady state:

$$
V_d = -V_{inv}
$$

$$
\dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} s V_{L0} = -\dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_t \cos α
$$

$$
\boxed{\,s = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α\,}
$$

#### (b) **Part (b) — 7 Marks** — Advantages, Limitations, and Applications

**Advantages:**

1. **High efficiency** at all speeds because slip power is recovered, not dissipated.
2. **Smooth and continuous** speed control over a wide range (0.3 N_s to N_s).
3. **Soft starting** capability built-in — no separate starter needed.
4. **Reduced heating** in rotor — cooler operation.
5. Suitable for **large high-rating motors** (MW range) where rotor-resistance control would waste huge power.

**Limitations:**

1. **Poor power factor at low speeds** (because α is large, cos α is small and negative).
2. **Line-side harmonics** from thyristor bridge — needs filtering.
3. **Speed range limited to subsynchronous only** in the basic Kramer topology.
4. **Transformer cost** adds to capital expenditure.
5. **Inverter rating ≈ slip power**, so for high-speed range the inverter must be sized for large $s P_g$.

**Applications:** Centrifugal pumps, fans, compressors, large cement-mill drives, hydro-electric pumped storage motor-generators, DFIG wind turbines.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Compare the slip power recovery scheme using (i) inverters and (ii) AC voltage regulators for slip-ring induction motor drives. Derive the slip expression for each case and explain how they achieve speed control.*

#### (a) **Part (a) — 7 Marks** — SPRS Using Inverters (Kramer / Scherbius)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) SPRS using Inverters (Static Kramer):** Already covered above. Slip:

$$
s = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α
$$

Speed control by α. Subsynchronous only.

**(ii) SPRS using Inverters (Static Scherbius):** With controlled rotor bridge (firing angle α₂) and line-side bridge (firing angle α₁):

$$
s = \dfrac{V_t \cos α_1 - V_{L0} \cos α_2}{V_{L0} \cos α_2}
$$

**Bidirectional** — allows sub- and super-synchronous motoring and regeneration.

#### (b) **Part (b) — 7 Marks** — SPRS Using AC Voltage Regulators

**Model Answer:**

**Two main topologies:**

**(i) Rotor Chopper + Inverter:**
- Diode bridge on rotor → DC chopper (duty cycle δ) → fixed-angle inverter → mains.
- Equivalent slip:

$$
s = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cdot \dfrac{\cos α}{\delta}
$$

- Speed control by duty cycle δ of chopper.
- Lower inverter rating (chopper handles most of the power).

**(ii) Cycloconverter on Rotor:**
- Direct AC-AC conversion on rotor side.
- No DC link.
- Slip range typically −0.5 ≤ s ≤ +0.5.
- Used in DFIG wind turbines and large MW drives.
- Bidirectional power flow.

**Comparative Table:**

| Feature | Inverter-based (Kramer) | AC Voltage Regulator |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion stages | AC–DC–AC | AC–DC–AC (with chopper) or direct AC-AC |
| DC link | Yes (large L) | Yes (with chopper) or None (cycloconverter) |
| Speed range | Sub-synchronous only (Kramer); both (Scherbius) | Both sub- and super-synchronous (cycloconverter) |
| Power factor | Poor at low speed | Better with PWM chopper |
| Switching device | Thyristor (line-commutated) | Thyristor / IGBT |
| Application | Pumps, fans (subsync only) | Wind turbines (DFIG), large drives |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $s = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α$ is the **most common error** — students write $s = +\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α$ and get a negative slip (supersynchronous) when α = 120°, which contradicts the Kramer (subsynchronous) operating range.
> 2. **Mixing up** $V_d$ and $V_{inv}$ signs in KVL — remember: in inverter mode, the thyristor bridge acts as a **negative** voltage source opposing $V_d$.
> 3. **Forgetting** that the inverter is **line-commutated** (relies on supply for commutation) — this is why power factor deteriorates at low speed and why a minimum α_max (≈165°) must be respected.
> 4. **Calculating** efficiency as $\dfrac{P_m}{P_g}$ ignoring stator losses — full marks require mentioning **stator copper + iron losses** separately.
> 5. **Confusing** Kramer (subsynchronous only) with Scherbius (both) — examiners test this distinction specifically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must remember for KTU ESE Module 4**

- **Core idea:** SPRS = recover slip power instead of dissipating it.
- **Kramer = diode bridge + line-commutated inverter**, **subsynchronous only**.
- **Scherbius = controlled rotor bridge + line-side inverter**, **sub + super synchronous**.
- **Key equation (memorize):** $s = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α$ — note the **negative sign**.
- **At α = 90°:** $s = 0$ → motor at synchronous speed.
- **At α = α_max (≈165°):** $s = s_{min}$ → minimum subsynchronous speed.
- **Air-gap power splits:** $P_g = s P_g \, (\text{slip}) + (1-s) P_g \, (\text{mechanical})$.
- **Slip power:** $P_{slip} = s P_g$.
- **Mechanical power developed:** $P_m = (1-s) P_g$.
- **Shaft output:** $P_{shaft} = P_m - P_{rotational\,losses}$.
- **Diode bridge DC output:** $V_d = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} s V_{L0}$.
- **Inverter DC input:** $V_{inv} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_t \cos α$.
- **DC-link equilibrium:** $V_d + V_{inv} = 0$ (ideal), giving the slip equation.
- **Practical limit:** extinction angle γ ≈ 15°–20° + commutation overlap β ≈ 5°–10°.
- **AC voltage regulator methods:** rotor chopper (duty cycle δ controls slip) and cycloconverter (direct AC-AC).
- **Cycloconverter advantage:** no DC link, bidirectional, used in DFIG wind turbines.
- **Largest energy savings:** variable-torque loads (fans, pumps) at reduced speed.
- **Disadvantages of SPRS:** poor power factor at low speed, harmonics, transformer cost, inverter rating = slip power.
- **Applications (must list 2–3 in exam):** centrifugal pumps, fans, compressors, cement mills, DFIG wind turbines, pumped-storage hydro.
- **Power flow sketch:** remember the three arrows — input, slip recovered, shaft output.
- **SPRS efficiency** is approximately $(1-s)$, vastly superior to rotor-resistance control whose input still includes $s P_g$.
- **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f_1}{p}$ rpm.
- **Speed formula:** $N = N_s (1-s)$.
- **For numerical problems:** always compute $\cos α$ value, plug into $s = -\dfrac{V_t}{V_{L0}} \cos α$, then compute $N$.
- **Sketch the speed-vs-α curve** (decreasing exponential-like) for full marks.
- **Mention commutation/extinction angles** when discussing limitations — examiners love this.
